Let the net (unbalanced) torque be $T_{net} = T_a - T_r$. The instantaneous angular acceleration is:

$$\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{T_{net}}{I}$$

**Step 2 — Work done in a small crank rotation $d\theta$.**

Work done on the flywheel is:

$$dE = T_{net} \, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Rate of change of energy with respect to angle.**

$$\dfrac{dE}{d\theta} = T_{net} = T_a - T_r$$

**Step 4 — Condition for extremum (max or min) of energy.**

At an extremum of the energy curve, the slope vanishes:

$$\dfrac{dE}{d\theta} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T_a = T_r$$

**Step 5 — Identify the mean speed.**

The mean speed is conventionally defined as the speed corresponding to the **mean torque** (the constant torque that does the same total work per cycle). Under steady running, the instant when $T_a = T_r$ corresponds to the flywheel passing through its **mean angular speed** $\omega$. Hence the extremum of energy coincides with $\omega = $ mean speed.

$$\therefore \quad \text{Maximum (and minimum) fluctuation of energy} \leftrightarrow \omega = \omega_{mean}$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering takeaway:** This is why the "mean speed" line on a TMD is the **reference for measuring fluctuation** — you draw the horizontal line at the mean height, and the area enclosed between the TMD and this line is the **fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE] **Sanity-check example** (not for exam submission — just for intuition).
> A flywheel has $I = 50 \, kg \cdot m^2$, $\omega_1 = 105 \, rad/s$, $\omega_2 = 95 \, rad/s$. Verify both forms of $\Delta E$ agree.

Mean speed: $\omega = \dfrac{105 + 95}{2} = 100 \, rad/s$

Coefficient of fluctuation: $C_s = \dfrac{105 - 95}{100} = 0.10$

**Form 1 (first principles):**

$$\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot (105^2 - 95^2)$$

$$\Delta E = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot (11025 - 9025) = 25 \cdot 2000 = 50000 \, J = 50 \, kJ$$

**Form 2 (master equation):**

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s = 50 \cdot 100^2 \cdot 0.10 = 50 \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.10 = 50000 \, J$$

Both give **$50 \, kJ$** ✓ — confirming the derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — How the Two Coefficients Interact

This block-level architecture shows how the driving torque, resisting torque, flywheel inertia, and the two coefficients form a closed design chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIME MOVER DRIVING TORQUE Ta] --> B[TURNSHAFT AND FLYWHEEL ASSEMBLY]
    L[LOAD RESISTING TORQUE Tr] --> B
    B --> C[NET TORQUE Tnet EQUALS Ta MINUS Tr]
    C --> D[SPEED FLUCTUATION omega1 TO omega2]
    D --> E[SQUARED SPEED GIVES KINETIC ENERGY E]
    E --> F[FLUCTUATION deltaE EQUALS E1 MINUS E2]
    F --> G[Cs DEFINED AS omega1 MINUS omega2 OVER omega]
    F --> H[Ce DEFINED AS deltaE OVER Ecycle]
    G --> I[MASTER EQUATION deltaE EQUALS I times omega SQUARED times Cs]
    H --> I
    I --> J[FLYWHEEL SIZING SOLVE FOR I]
    I --> K[COUPLED EQUATION Ce times E EQUALS I times omega SQUARED times Cs]
    J --> L2[ENGINE AND MACHINE DESIGN SPECIFICATION]
    K --> L2
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Graph — Speed vs Crank Angle

The diagram below shows how the flywheel speed oscillates between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$, crossing the mean speed $\omega$ exactly at the **points of maximum and minimum energy**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPEED_PROFILE[SPEED VS CRANK ANGLE]
        direction LR
        P1[CRANK ANGLE ZERO] --> Q1[SPEED RISES TO omega1 MAX]
        Q1 --> R1[SPEED FALLS CROSSING omega MEAN SPEED]
        R1 --> S1[SPEED FALLS TO omega2 MIN]
        S1 --> T1[SPEED RISES CROSSING omega MEAN SPEED]
        T1 --> U1[BACK TO omega1 COMPLETING CYCLE]
    end
    subgraph ENERGY_PROFILE[ENERGY VS CRANK ANGLE]
        direction LR
        E1[ENERGY PEAKS AT E1] --> F1[ENERGY FALLS TO MINIMUM E2]
        F1 --> G1[ENERGY RISES BACK TO E1]
    end
    Q1 -. SAME ANGLE .-> E1
    S1 -. SAME ANGLE .-> F1
    R1 -. MEAN SPEED POINT .-> T1
```

## 4.3 Visual Summary Table

| Phase of Cycle | Torque Balance | Speed | Energy | Coefficient Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak torque region | $T_a > T_r$ | Accelerating: $\omega \uparrow$ | Energy stored: $E \uparrow$ | $C_s$ for speed swing |
| Mean speed instant | $T_a = T_r$ | $\omega = \omega_{mean}$ | $E$ at extremum | $\Delta E$ measurable |
| Low torque region | $T_a < T_r$ | Decelerating: $\omega \downarrow$ | Energy released: $E \downarrow$ | $C_E$ for energy swing |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the "1/2"** in $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ while deriving $\Delta E$ — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. Using $\omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2\omega$ incorrectly when the problem gives **mean rpm** (not rad/s). Convert rpm → rad/s **first**.
> 3. For a **4-stroke IC engine**, the work done per cycle uses **2 revolutions**, not 1. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 4. In **punching-machine problems**, the punching work is done **entirely by the flywheel** (motor cannot respond fast enough) — the time ratio is a distractor for the energy side, not for the kinetic side.
> 5. **Units of $I$** must be $kg \cdot m^2$ when $\omega$ is in rad/s. Inconsistency here = 1 mark lost.
> 6. Mixing up $C_s$ (speed swing) with $C_E$ (energy swing) in the final answer — read the question twice.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define coefficient of fluctuation of speed and coefficient of fluctuation of energy. State their units. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_s$):** It is the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed of the flywheel during one working cycle.

$$C_s = \dfrac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \dfrac{2(N_1 - N_2)}{N_1 + N_2}$$

**Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):** It is the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of energy to the work done per cycle.

$$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E}$$

**Units:** Both are **dimensionless** (no units), since each is a ratio of two quantities with identical dimensions.

---

### Q2. The mean speed of a flywheel is $20 \, rad/s$ and $C_s = 0.10$. Find the maximum and minimum speeds. Also find $\Delta E$ if the moment of inertia is $I = 100 \, kg \cdot m^2$. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

Given: $\omega = 20 \, rad/s$, $C_s = 0.10$, $I = 100 \, kg \cdot m^2$.

Step 1 — Fluctuation of speed:

$$\omega_1 - \omega_2 = C_s \cdot \omega = 0.10 \times 20 = 2 \, rad/s$$

Step 2 — Mean speed relation:

$$\omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2\omega = 40 \, rad/s$$

Step 3 — Solve the two equations:

$$\omega_1 = \dfrac{(2\omega_1) - (2\omega_2)}{2} \text{ algebra}$$

$$\omega_1 = \dfrac{40 + 2}{2} = 21 \, rad/s$$

$$\omega_2 = \dfrac{40 - 2}{2} = 19 \, rad/s$$

Step 4 — Maximum fluctuation of energy:

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s = 100 \times 20^2 \times 0.10 = 4000 \, J = 4 \, kJ$$

**Result:** $\omega_1 = 21 \, rad/s$, $\omega_2 = 19 \, rad/s$, $\Delta E = 4000 \, J$.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A single-cylinder, 4-stroke IC engine develops power at a mean speed of $300 \, rpm$. The turning moment diagram is approximated by a sine curve of amplitude $200 \, N \cdot m$ about a mean line of $1000 \, N \cdot m$. The maximum fluctuation of energy is $\Delta E = 9000 \, J$. A flywheel of mass $250 \, kg$ is fitted to the crankshaft.

**(a)** Find the coefficient of fluctuation of energy $C_E$.
**(b)** Find the coefficient of fluctuation of speed $C_s$ and the maximum & minimum speeds.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 300 \, rpm$, $\Delta E = 9000 \, J$, $m_{fw} = 250 \, kg$, 4-stroke engine.

**Part (a) — Find $C_E$:**

Step 1 — Convert mean speed to rad/s:

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 300}{60} = 10 \pi = 31.416 \, rad/s$$

Step 2 — Work done per cycle for a 4-stroke engine (1 cycle = 2 revolutions):

Work done per cycle = (mean torque) $\times$ (angle turned per cycle).

$T_{mean} = 1000 \, N \cdot m$ (given mean line of TMD), angle per cycle = $2 \times 2\pi = 4\pi \, rad$.

$$E = T_{mean} \times 4\pi = 1000 \times 4\pi = 4000 \pi = 12566.4 \, J$$

Step 3 — Apply the definition of $C_E$:

$$C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E} = \dfrac{9000}{12566.4} = 0.7162$$

**Result (a): $C_E \approx 0.716$** *(Note: This is unusually high — in practice the TMD is approximated to give a *fractional* fluctuation; the figure is retained for the calculation.)*

**Part (b) — Find $C_s$ and the speed limits:**

Step 1 — Use the coupling equation:

$$C_s = \dfrac{C_E \cdot E}{I \cdot \omega^2} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{I \cdot \omega^2}$$

Step 2 — We need $I$. Assume the flywheel is a solid disc of radius $R$ to be determined, or — more commonly in KTU — the radius of gyration $k$ is taken. For this problem, **assume the flywheel has a radius of gyration $k = 0.4 \, m$** (typical KTU value for 250 kg flywheel).

$$I = m \cdot k^2 = 250 \times 0.4^2 = 250 \times 0.16 = 40 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

Step 3 — Compute $C_s$:

$$C_s = \dfrac{9000}{40 \times (31.416)^2} = \dfrac{9000}{40 \times 986.96} = \dfrac{9000}{39478.4} = 0.2280$$

Step 4 — Speed limits:

$$\omega_1 - \omega_2 = C_s \cdot \omega = 0.2280 \times 31.416 = 7.16 \, rad/s$$

$$\omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2 \omega = 62.83 \, rad/s$$

$$\omega_1 = \dfrac{62.83 + 7.16}{2} = 34.998 \approx 35.0 \, rad/s \;\; (\approx 334 \, rpm)$$

$$\omega_2 = \dfrac{62.83 - 7.16}{2} = 27.835 \approx 27.84 \, rad/s \;\; (\approx 266 \, rpm)$$

**Result (b):** $C_s \approx 0.228$, $\omega_1 \approx 35.0 \, rad/s$ ($334 \, rpm$), $\omega_2 \approx 27.84 \, rad/s$ ($266 \, rpm$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A punching machine performs $25$ punching operations per minute on a $20 \, mm$ thick mild steel plate. The diameter of each punched hole is $30 \, mm$ and the shearing strength of the plate is $350 \, MPa$. The punching operation occupies $\dfrac{1}{10}$ of the cycle time. The flywheel has a mass of $300 \, kg$ and a radius of gyration of $350 \, mm$. The mean speed of the flywheel is $18 \, rad/s$.

**(a)** Find the coefficient of fluctuation of speed of the flywheel.
**(b)** Find the power required to drive the punching machine.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n_{punch} = 25 \, /min$, plate thickness $t = 20 \, mm = 0.02 \, m$, hole dia $d = 30 \, mm = 0.03 \, m$, $\tau_{sh} = 350 \, MPa = 350 \times 10^6 \, N/m^2$, $m_{fw} = 300 \, kg$, $k = 350 \, mm = 0.35 \, m$, $\omega = 18 \, rad/s$.

**Part (a) — Find $C_s$:**

Step 1 — Shearing area of one hole (perimeter $\times$ thickness):

$$A_{sh} = \pi d \times t = \pi \times 0.03 \times 0.02 = 1.885 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2$$

Step 2 — Shearing force required:

$$F_{sh} = \tau_{sh} \times A_{sh} = 350 \times 10^6 \times 1.885 \times 10^{-3} = 659,734 \, N$$

Step 3 — Work done in punching one hole (force $\times$ plate thickness, since the punch travels one plate thickness):

$$W_{punch} = F_{sh} \times t = 659,734 \times 0.02 = 13,194.7 \, J$$

Step 4 — Time per cycle:

$$T_{cycle} = \dfrac{60}{n_{punch}} = \dfrac{60}{25} = 2.4 \, s$$

Step 5 — Power supplied by the motor (assumed constant over the cycle):

$$P = \dfrac{W_{punch}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{13,194.7}{2.4} = 5498 \, W \approx 5.5 \, kW$$

> [!NOTE] **Why use $T_{cycle}$ and not just the punch time?**
> The motor delivers constant power over the **whole** cycle (it cannot react fast enough to follow the impulsive load). Over the punch time ($T_{cycle}/10 = 0.24 \, s$) the motor supplies only $\frac{1}{10}$ of $W_{punch}$. The remaining $\frac{9}{10}$ of the punch energy **must come from the flywheel** — this is the source of the fluctuation.

Step 6 — Energy that the flywheel must supply during punching:

$$W_{fly} = W_{punch} - P \times t_{punch} = W_{punch} - P \times \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{10}$$

$$W_{fly} = 13,194.7 - 5498 \times 0.24 = 13,194.7 - 1319.5 = 11,875.2 \, J$$

Step 7 — Moment of inertia of the flywheel:

$$I = m \cdot k^2 = 300 \times 0.35^2 = 300 \times 0.1225 = 36.75 \, kg \cdot m^2$$

Step 8 — Maximum fluctuation of energy (equate to $W_{fly}$):

$$\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$$

$$C_s = \dfrac{\Delta E}{I \omega^2} = \dfrac{11,875.2}{36.75 \times 18^2} = \dfrac{11,875.2}{36.75 \times 324} = \dfrac{11,875.2}{11,907} = 0.9972$$

Hmm — $C_s \approx 1.0$ is unrealistically high. In **standard KTU convention**, when the time-of-punch is given but the motor is rated to provide the **full** punch energy continuously, the simplifying assumption is:

$$\Delta E = W_{punch} \text{ (full punch energy buffered by flywheel)}$$

Step 8 (revised — KTU standard assumption):

$$C_s = \dfrac{W_{punch}}{I \omega^2} = \dfrac{13,194.7}{36.75 \times 324} = \dfrac{13,194.7}{11,907} = 1.108$$

Step 9 — **Practical KTU approach** — apply both forms and write:

$$C_s = \dfrac{W_{punch}}{I \, \omega^2} = \dfrac{13,194.7}{11,907} \approx 1.108 \;\; (\text{very rough, indicates flywheel is undersized})$$

**Result (a): $C_s \approx 1.108$** (the high value signals the flywheel must be re-sized for a lower $C_s \approx 0.05$ typical for machinery).

**Part (b) — Power required:**

$$P = \dfrac{W_{punch}}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{13,194.7}{2.4} \approx 5.5 \, kW$$

**Result (b): $P \approx 5.5 \, kW$.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **$C_s$** = $(\omega_1 - \omega_2) / \omega$ — measures **speed swing**.
- [x] **$C_E$** = $\Delta E / E$ — measures **energy swing**.
- [x] Both are **dimensionless**.
- [x] **Master equation:** $\Delta E = I \omega^2 C_s$ (verify by first principles: $\frac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2) \to I \omega^2 C_s$).
- [x] **Coupling equation:** $C_s = (C_E \cdot E) / (I \cdot \omega^2)$.
- [x] **Maximum fluctuation of energy occurs at mean speed** — because at that instant $T_a = T_r$, so $dE/d\theta = 0$.
- [x] **Work per cycle $E$**: use $T_{mean} \times 2\pi$ for 2-stroke, $T_{mean} \times 4\pi$ for 4-stroke engines; use $P \times 60/N$ for general prime movers.
- [x] **Punching machine** — the *entire* punch energy is typically taken as $\Delta E$ (motor contribution during the punch instant is neglected in the simple KTU model).
- [x] **Always convert rpm → rad/s** before using $\omega$ in the master equation.
- [x] **$I = m k^2$** for a flywheel with radius of gyration $k$.
- [x] **Typical $C_s$ values:** $1/50$ to $1/100$ for normal machinery; $1/10$ to $1/15$ for rough machinery; $1/200$ for precision tools.
- [x] **Equation of a TMD area** = work done = $\Delta E$ between successive speed extrema.
- [x] **Sign convention:** $\omega_1 > \omega > \omega_2$; $\Delta E$ is always taken as positive ($E_1 - E_2$).
- [x] **Examiner's mantra:** "Show your factorisation — $\omega_1^2 - \omega_2^2 = (\omega_1 - \omega_2)(\omega_1 + \omega_2)$ — explicitly in the derivation."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Flywheels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Flywheels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **flywheel** is a heavy, rotating mechanical energy-storage device, typically a massive disc or rim-and-spoke wheel, mounted on the crankshaft of an engine or machine to smooth out fluctuations in rotational speed and torque during each operating cycle. It functions on the principle of **conservation of angular momentum**, absorbing energy when the driving torque exceeds the resisting torque (acceleration phase) and releasing energy when the resisting torque exceeds the driving torque (retardation phase).

In KTU terminology, the **Design of Flywheels** involves determining:
1. The required **mass moment of inertia ($I$)** to limit the speed fluctuation within permissible limits.
2. The geometric proportions of the **rim**, **arms (webs/spokes)**, and **hub**.
3. The **factor of safety** against failure due to centrifugal bursting stresses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition:**
> A flywheel is an energy-storing mechanical element that stores energy in the form of **kinetic energy of rotation**, given by $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$, and the energy fluctuation during a cycle determines its required moment of inertia.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a **commuter riding a bicycle up and down a hilly road**.

- When going **downhill** (engine supplies excess energy), the bicycle speeds up. The wheels act as a flywheel — they **store kinetic energy**.
- When going **uphill** (resistive load exceeds input), the bicycle slows down. The previously stored kinetic energy in the wheels is **released** to push the rider over the hill.

Similarly, in an **internal combustion engine**:
- During the **power stroke**, the exploding gas pushes the piston with great force → the engine speeds up.
- During the other three strokes (intake, compression, exhaust), no power is supplied → the engine tends to slow down.
- The **flywheel**, being massive and rotating, stores energy during the power stroke and returns it during the idle strokes, keeping speed nearly constant.

**Key Real-World Examples of Flywheels:**
| Application | Role of Flywheel |
|---|---|
| **Car engine** (manual transmission) | Smooths out torque pulses, allows clutch engagement |
| **Punch press / Shearing machine** | Stores energy from a small motor to deliver huge impact force |
| **Steam engine / Steam hammer** | Maintains rotation between piston strokes |
| **Energy storage systems (FES)** | Modern grid energy storage using high-vacuum magnetic flywheels |
| **Lathe spindles** | Maintains constant cutting speed |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Quantities for Flywheel Design:**
> - Density of **cast iron** = $\rho_{CI} = 7200 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ (commonly used for rims)
> - Density of **steel** = $\rho_{st} = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$ (used for high-stress rims)
> - Permissible rim speed for **cast iron** ≈ $v_{max} = 30 \text{ to } 45$ m/s
> - Permissible rim speed for **steel** ≈ $v_{max} = 60 \text{ to } 100$ m/s
> - Permissible rim stress for cast iron ≈ $\sigma_{t,max} = 6$ MPa
> - Permissible rim stress for steel ≈ $\sigma_{t,max} = 14$ MPa
> - Typical **fluctuation of speed** $C_{s} = 0.01$ to $0.05$ for engines
> - Typical **fluctuation of energy** $C_{E} = 0.05$ to $0.15$ for punching machines

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Theory: Why is a Flywheel Needed?

In any prime mover (engine) or machine driven by a non-uniform torque, the **crank effort diagram** or **torque diagram** shows fluctuation across one cycle. Let:

- $\omega$ = mean angular velocity (rad/s)
- $N$ = mean speed (rpm)
- $N_{1}$ = maximum speed (rpm)
- $N_{2}$ = minimum speed (rpm)
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of flywheel (kg·m²)
- $W$ = work done per cycle (J)

### Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_{s}$)

$$C_{s} = \frac{N_{1} - N_{2}}{N} = \frac{\omega_{1} - \omega_{2}}{\omega}$$

It is the **ratio** of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed. A small $C_s$ means a smoother machine.

### Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_{E}$)

$$C_{E} = \frac{\Delta E}{W} = \frac{E_{1} - E_{2}}{W}$$

It is the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of energy** to the work done per cycle.

### Maximum Fluctuation of Energy ($\Delta E$)

$$\Delta E = E_{1} - E_{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **key design equation** is obtained by writing:
> $$\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2} = (\omega_{1} + \omega_{2})(\omega_{1} - \omega_{2}) = 2\omega \cdot (\omega \cdot C_{s})$$
> Therefore:
> $$\Delta E = I \, \omega^{2} \, C_{s}$$
> And:
> $$I = \frac{\Delta E}{C_{s} \, \omega^{2}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-day essential** for KTU problems on flywheel design. Memorize the symbols and units.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic energy of flywheel | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | J | Energy stored in rotation |
| 2 | Fluctuation of speed | $C_{s} = \dfrac{N_{1} - N_{2}}{N}$ | dimensionless | Usually 0.01–0.05 |
| 3 | Fluctuation of energy | $C_{E} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ | dimensionless | Usually 0.05–0.15 |
| 4 | Max. fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E = I \omega^{2} C_{s}$ | J | Fundamental design eqn |
| 5 | Required moment of inertia | $I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_{s} \omega^{2}}$ | kg·m² | Solve this first |
| 6 | Solid disc moment of inertia | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}$ | kg·m² | Disc of mass $M$, radius $R$ |
| 7 | Thin rim moment of inertia | $I = M R^{2}$ | kg·m² | Rim of mass $M$, radius $R$ |
| 8 | Rim hoop stress (centrifugal) | $\sigma_{t} = \rho \, v^{2}$ | Pa (N/m²) | $v$ = rim velocity in m/s |
| 9 | Rim velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = mean rim diameter |
| 10 | Flywheel mass (solid disc) | $M = \rho \, \pi \, B \, R^{2}$ | kg | Thickness $B$, density $\rho$ |
| 11 | Flywheel mass (thin rim) | $M = \rho \, \pi \, D \, A$ | kg | $A$ = cross-section area of rim |
| 12 | Stress in arms (bending) | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{32 \, M_{b}}{\pi \, d^{3} \, n}$ | Pa | $n$ = no. of arms, $d$ = arm dia. |
| 13 | Bursting tangential stress in rim | $\sigma_{t} = \dfrac{\rho \, v^{2} \, (R + b)}{b}$ | Pa | For rectangular rim section, $b$ = width |
| 14 | Shear stress in arms (torsion) | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3} \, n}$ | Pa | $T$ = transmitted torque |
| 15 | Hub (key) stress | $\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{2 T}{d_{k} \, l_{k} \, t}$ | Pa | $d_k$ = key depth, $l_k$ = key length |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a KTU Mechanical Engineer need this?**
> - **Engine design**: Determines the smooth-running mass needed for a 4-stroke IC engine.
> - **Punch press design**: A punch press running at constant motor speed delivers a sudden huge force — only a flywheel can supply this burst energy. The flywheel's kinetic energy equals the work done in one stroke: $\tfrac{1}{2}I(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}) = F \cdot s$.
> - **Forging hammers, rolling mills, riveting machines**: All intermittent high-load machines use flywheels.
> - **Modern KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System)**: Formula-1 cars use a small flywheel to recover braking energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between $\Delta E$, $I$, $\omega$, and $C_s$

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams. We derive the equation $\Delta E = I\omega^{2}C_{s}$ from first principles.

### Step 1: Define Maximum and Minimum Kinetic Energies

The flywheel has two extreme speeds during one cycle:
- Maximum speed $\omega_1$ → Maximum kinetic energy $E_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{1}^{2}$
- Minimum speed $\omega_2$ → Minimum kinetic energy $E_2 = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{2}^{2}$

### Step 2: Maximum Fluctuation of Energy

By definition, the maximum fluctuation of energy is the difference:
$$\Delta E = E_{1} - E_{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{1}^{2} - \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega_{2}^{2}$$

### Step 3: Factor Out

$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2}\right)$$

### Step 4: Apply the Algebraic Identity

Using $a^{2} - b^{2} = (a+b)(a-b)$:
$$\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2} = \left(\omega_{1} + \omega_{2}\right)\left(\omega_{1} - \omega_{2}\right)$$

### Step 5: Introduce Mean Angular Velocity

The **mean angular velocity** is the arithmetic average:
$$\omega = \dfrac{\omega_{1} + \omega_{2}}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{1} + \omega_{2} = 2\omega$$

### Step 6: Introduce Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed

By definition:
$$C_{s} = \dfrac{\omega_{1} - \omega_{2}}{\omega} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{1} - \omega_{2} = C_{s} \, \omega$$

### Step 7: Substitute Back

$$\omega_{1}^{2} - \omega_{2}^{2} = \left(2\omega\right)\left(C_{s} \, \omega\right) = 2 \, C_{s} \, \omega^{2}$$

### Step 8: Final Result

$$\boxed{\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \cdot 2 C_{s} \omega^{2} = I \, \omega^{2} \, C_{s}}$$

Hence, the required moment of inertia is:

$$\boxed{I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_{s} \, \omega^{2}}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Centrifugal (Hoop) Stress in a Thin Rim

Consider a thin rim of **mean radius $R$**, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. The rim is essentially a thin rotating ring. Consider a small element subtending angle $d\theta$ at the center.

### Step 1: Geometry of Element

The element has:
- Two radial faces of width $R \, d\theta$ each
- Inner and outer curved surfaces (we consider the mean radius $R$ for thin rim)
- Cross-sectional area of rim = $A$ (m²)
- Density of rim material = $\rho$ (kg/m³)

### Step 2: Mass of the Element

The arc length of the element at the mean circle is $R \, d\theta$. Therefore:
$$\text{Volume} = A \cdot R \, d\theta \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; dm = \rho \, A \, R \, d\theta$$

### Step 3: Centrifugal Force on the Element

The centrifugal force acts radially outward. Its magnitude is:
$$dF_{c} = dm \cdot \omega^{2} \cdot R = \rho \, A \, R^{2} \, \omega^{2} \, d\theta$$

### Step 4: Resolve the Forces on the Two Cut Faces

The element is in equilibrium. The two tangential tensions $T$ at the cut faces balance the radial centrifugal force when resolved radially:

Each tension $T = \sigma_t \cdot A$ acts tangentially. The component of the tension along the radial direction (toward the center) for each face is:
$$T \sin\!\left(\dfrac{d\theta}{2}\right) \approx T \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{2} \quad \text{(for small } d\theta\text{)}$$

### Step 5: Equilibrium Equation

The two radial components of the two tensions balance the centrifugal force:
$$2 \cdot T \cdot \dfrac{d\theta}{2} = dF_{c}$$

$$T \, d\theta = \rho \, A \, R^{2} \, \omega^{2} \, d\theta$$

$$T = \rho \, A \, R^{2} \, \omega^{2}$$

### Step 6: Divide by Area to Get Stress

Since $T = \sigma_t \cdot A$:
$$\sigma_t \cdot A = \rho \, A \, R^{2} \, \omega^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_t = \rho \, R^{2} \, \omega^{2} = \rho \, v^{2}}$$

where $v = R\omega$ is the **rim velocity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The hoop stress in a thin rim is **independent of the cross-section area** — it depends only on the material density and the rim velocity. To limit stress, the design chooses the **largest allowable $v$** and finds the diameter accordingly.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stresses in the Arms (Webs)

The arms (spokes) of a flywheel transfer torque from the hub to the rim. They experience:

1. **Bending stress** due to centrifugal pull of the rim.
2. **Shear/torsional stress** due to transmitted torque $T$.

### Step 3.3.1: Bending Stress Due to Rim Centrifugal Force

Consider $n$ arms, each of circular cross-section of diameter $d$. The total centrifugal pull of the rim (treated as a thin ring) is balanced by the tension in the arms.

**Total outward pull from rim:**
$$F_{c,total} = M \, \omega^{2} \, R$$

where $M$ is the rim mass. This force is shared equally by the $n$ arms.

**Force per arm (axial pull, inducing tension, not pure bending in classical sense):**

Actually, in the classical KTU/Shigley treatment, this pull is often converted into a **bending moment** at the arm root (near the hub) by considering the arm as a cantilever fixed at the hub. The bending moment at the root of each arm is:

$$M_{b} = \dfrac{F_{c,total}}{n} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2} = \dfrac{M \omega^{2} R}{n} \cdot \dfrac{L}{2}$$

where $L$ is the arm length (approximately equal to $R$).

**Bending stress in arm:**
$$\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M_{b}}{Z} = \dfrac{32 \, M_{b}}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

For the arm to be safe, $\sigma_b \leq \sigma_{permissible}$.

### Step 3.3.2: Shear Stress Due to Transmitted Torque

The torque $T$ transmitted to the rim is shared by $n$ arms. Each arm carries a torque:
$$T_{arm} = \dfrac{T}{n}$$

The shear stress in a circular arm under torsion is:
$$\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T_{arm}}{\pi \, d^{3}} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3} \, n}$$

The **maximum shear stress** theory (or von Mises) is then used to find the safe diameter.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing Flywheel Type vs. Application

| Application | Type of Flywheel | Typical $C_s$ | Energy Storage Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| **4-stroke IC engine (car)** | Heavy rim-type, 4–6 spokes | 0.01–0.03 | Smooths torque between power strokes |
| **2-stroke IC engine** | Smaller rim-type | 0.02–0.05 | Power stroke every revolution |
| **Punch press** | Large solid disc | 0.10–0.20 | Single massive energy release |
| **Shearing machine** | Large rim-and-spoke | 0.08–0.15 | Smooths intermittent cutting |
| **Steam hammer / forge** | Very large rim | 0.15–0.30 | Stores energy for the impact blow |
| **Water pump (uniform load)** | Small or no flywheel | $< 0.005$ | Not always needed |
| **Rolling mill** | Massive, multiple flywheels | 0.10–0.25 | Intermittent reduction of ingots |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since a physical flywheel drawing requires a cross-sectional engineering sketch, we provide a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** mapping the **energy flow, force flow, and stress flow** within a typical rim-and-spoke flywheel, rendered in Mermaid. This complements the textual description in the KTU textbook.

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Flywheel System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[External Torque Input from Crankshaft] --> nodeB[Hub Assembly with Key]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Arms / Spokes under Combined Loading]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Rim under Centrifugal Hoop Tension]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stored Kinetic Energy in Rotating Mass]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Output Torque to Driven Load]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Speed Fluctuation Monitoring]

    subgraph STRESS[Flywheel Structural Stress States]
        nodeH[Hub: Bearing Contact and Key Shear]
        nodeI[Arms: Bending plus Torsion]
        nodeJ[Rim: Pure Hoop Tension sigma t equals rho v squared]
    end

    nodeB --> STRESS
    nodeC --> STRESS
    nodeD --> STRESS
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **energy flow** follows the chain: Input Torque → Hub → Arms → Rim → Kinetic Energy → Output Torque.
- The **stress states** (subgraph) show what each component experiences under operation.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Flywheel Design Procedure (KTU Standard Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Step 1: Obtain crank effort or torque diagram] --> stepB[Step 2: Compute mean torque Tmean equals W per cycle divided by angle]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Plot energy fluctuations and find max delta E]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Compute mean angular velocity omega]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Read permissible Cs from problem]
    stepE --> stepF[Step 6: Compute I equals delta E divided by Cs times omega squared]
    stepF --> stepG[Step 7: Choose flywheel geometry solid disc or rim]
    stepG --> stepH[Step 8: Determine diameter from rim stress sigma t equals rho v squared]
    stepH --> stepI[Step 9: Compute arm diameter from bending plus torsion]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 10: Design hub and key for transmitted torque]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Comparison of Solid Disc vs. Rim-Type Flywheel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOLID[Solid Disc Flywheel]
        s1[Material: Cast Iron or Steel]
        s2[Moment of Inertia: I equals half M R squared]
        s3[Hoop Stress: Centrifugal stress varies with radius]
        s4[Application: Punch press and shearing machine]
    end

    subgraph RIM[Rim Type Flywheel with Arms]
        r1[Material: Rim is cast iron or steel]
        r2[Moment of Inertia: I equals M R squared for thin rim]
        r3[Hoop Stress: Uniform sigma t equals rho v squared]
        r4[Application: IC engines and steady energy storage]
    end

    SOLID --- RIM
```

**Engineering Insight from the Diagram:**
- A **solid disc** has a non-uniform hoop stress: at the center, stress is zero; at the rim, it is maximum ($\rho v^2$). This wastes material near the center.
- A **rim-type flywheel** concentrates mass at the rim where the moment of inertia is highest (since $I = MR^2$ depends on $R^2$, not $R^4$). For a given $I$, rim-type uses less mass — making it the **preferred choice for engines**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "coefficient of fluctuation of speed" and "coefficient of fluctuation of energy" as applied to a flywheel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($C_s$):** It is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of speed to the mean speed of the flywheel.

$$C_{s} = \dfrac{N_{1} - N_{2}}{N} = \dfrac{\omega_{1} - \omega_{2}}{\omega}$$

**Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($C_E$):** It is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of energy to the work done per cycle.

$$C_{E} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W} = \dfrac{E_{1} - E_{2}}{W}$$

These two are the key parameters governing the **size (moment of inertia)** of a flywheel for a given machine. *(1 mark each definition + 1 mark for explanation/importance.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Why is a flywheel not needed in a water pump driven by a uniform electric motor load, but essential in a punch press driven by the same motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A water pump requires **nearly uniform torque** throughout its cycle, so the resisting torque closely matches the driving torque — the speed fluctuation $C_s$ is negligible (less than 0.005). A flywheel would be redundant.

In contrast, a punch press requires a **sudden, massive force** during the punching stroke, but only operates intermittently (one stroke every few seconds). The motor cannot supply this peak torque instantaneously. A **flywheel stores kinetic energy** from the motor during idle time (energy build-up phase) and **releases it during the punching stroke** (energy release phase), allowing a small motor to drive a large intermittent load. Therefore, a flywheel is essential. *(1 mark water pump + 1 mark punch press + 1 mark energy storage explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

An IC engine has a mean speed of **300 rpm** and the fluctuation of energy is found to be **25% of the work done per cycle**. The work done per cycle is **3000 J**. The permissible fluctuation of speed is **2%**. Determine:
**(a)** The moment of inertia of the flywheel required. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The dimensions of a solid disc flywheel made of cast iron ($\rho = 7200$ kg/m³), given that the thickness is **1/4 of the diameter**, and verify that the rim stress is within safe limits ($v_{max} = 40$ m/s for cast iron). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Moment of Inertia**

**Given:**
- $N = 300$ rpm
- $C_E = 0.25$, $W = 3000$ J
- $C_s = 0.02$

**Step 1: Compute $\Delta E$ (max fluctuation of energy).**

$$\Delta E = C_E \times W = 0.25 \times 3000 = 750 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2: Convert $N$ to $\omega$ (mean angular velocity).**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the design equation.**

$$I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_{s} \, \omega^{2}} = \dfrac{750}{0.02 \times (31.416)^{2}}$$

Compute $\omega^{2}$:
$$\omega^{2} = (10\pi)^{2} = 100 \pi^{2} \approx 986.96 \text{ (rad/s)}^{2}$$

So:
$$I = \dfrac{750}{0.02 \times 986.96} = \dfrac{750}{19.7392} \approx 37.99 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 38.0 \text{ kg·m}^{2}}$$

#### Part (b): Solid Disc Flywheel Dimensions

**Given:**
- $\rho = 7200$ kg/m³
- Thickness $B = D/4$, so $B = R/2$ (since $D = 2R$)
- $I = 38.0$ kg·m²
- $v_{max} = 40$ m/s

**Step 1: Use the relation between $I$ and rim velocity.**

For a solid disc, the moment of inertia is:
$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} M R^{2}$$

The mass of the solid disc is:
$$M = \rho \times \text{Volume} = \rho \times \pi R^{2} B = \rho \times \pi R^{2} \times \dfrac{R}{2} = \dfrac{\rho \pi R^{3}}{2}$$

**Step 2: Substitute $M$ into $I$:**

$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} \times \left(\dfrac{\rho \pi R^{3}}{2}\right) \times R^{2} = \dfrac{\rho \pi R^{5}}{4}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R$:**

$$R^{5} = \dfrac{4I}{\rho \pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 38.0}{7200 \times \pi} = \dfrac{152}{22619.47} \approx 6.720 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{5}$$

$$R = \left(6.720 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{1/5}$$

**Computing the fifth root step by step:**
- $R = (6.720 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2}$
- $\log_{10}(6.720 \times 10^{-3}) = \log_{10}(6.720) + \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = 0.8274 - 3 = -2.1726$
- $0.2 \times (-2.1726) = -0.4345$
- $R = 10^{-0.4345} \approx 0.368 \text{ m}$

So **$R \approx 0.368$ m, $D \approx 0.736$ m**.

**Step 4: Find thickness $B$:**

$$B = \dfrac{D}{4} = \dfrac{0.736}{4} = 0.184 \text{ m} = 184 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Verify rim stress.**

The actual rim velocity is:
$$v = R \omega = 0.368 \times 31.416 \approx 11.56 \text{ m/s}$$

This is well within the safe limit of $v_{max} = 40$ m/s for cast iron. ✅

Alternatively, compute hoop stress at rim:
$$\sigma_t = \rho v^{2} = 7200 \times (11.56)^{2} = 7200 \times 133.6 = 961{,}920 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.96 \text{ MPa}$$

This is far below the typical allowable $\sigma_t = 6$ MPa for cast iron. ✅

$$\boxed{\text{Design: } D \approx 0.736 \text{ m}, \quad B \approx 0.184 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_{t} \approx 0.96 \text{ MPa (safe)}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

A punching machine is driven by an electric motor developing **15 kW at 200 rpm**. Each punching operation requires **1200 J of energy** and is completed in **1 second**. The machine performs **15 punching operations per minute**. The mean speed of the flywheel during the cycle is to be **0.95 times the no-load speed**. Determine:
**(a)** The moment of inertia of the flywheel. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The required diameter of a solid cast iron flywheel ($\rho = 7200$ kg/m³) of thickness **75 mm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Moment of Inertia**

**Step 1: Compute the no-load speed (i.e., motor-driven, no punching).**

Given that the **mean speed** during the cycle is **0.95 times the no-load speed**:
$$N_{mean} = 0.95 \times 200 = 190 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2: Mean angular velocity during cycle.**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 190}{60} = 19.897 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Energy supplied by motor per second.**

Motor power = 15 kW = 15,000 J/s. The flywheel **absorbs** energy during idle time and **releases** during punching.

**Step 4: Compute time for one full cycle.**

There are 15 punching operations per minute, so cycle time = 60/15 = **4 seconds per cycle**.

Of this, **1 second** is the punching stroke (energy released by flywheel), and **3 seconds** is the idle period (energy absorbed from motor).

**Step 5: Energy supplied by motor per cycle.**

$$E_{motor} = 15{,}000 \text{ J/s} \times 4 \text{ s} = 60{,}000 \text{ J per cycle}$$

**Step 6: Energy released by flywheel per cycle.**

$$E_{release} = 1200 \text{ J}$$

**Step 7: Energy gained by flywheel per cycle.**

The motor supplies 60,000 J, and 1200 J is used for punching. The rest (58,800 J) goes into raising the speed of the flywheel. The flywheel returns 1200 J during the punch.

The **maximum fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E$ equals the energy released during punching stroke = **1200 J** (the flywheel drops from max to min energy).

$$\Delta E = 1200 \text{ J}$$

**Step 8: Determine $C_s$ from given information.**

The mean speed is 0.95 times the no-load speed. The no-load speed corresponds to the **maximum speed** (when motor has been charging the flywheel). The minimum speed is the speed at the end of the punching stroke.

By convention, the no-load speed (without any load on flywheel) corresponds to the maximum speed. So:
$$\omega_1 = 200 \text{ rpm} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 200}{60} = 20.944 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\omega_2 = ?$$

Since $\omega_{mean} = 0.95 \times \omega_1$:
$$\omega_1 + \omega_2 = 2 \omega_{mean} = 2 \times 0.95 \times \omega_1 = 1.9 \, \omega_1$$

So:
$$\omega_2 = 1.9 \, \omega_1 - \omega_1 = 0.9 \, \omega_1$$

**Step 9: Compute $C_s$:**

$$C_s = \dfrac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{\omega_{mean}} = \dfrac{\omega_1 - 0.9\omega_1}{0.95 \omega_1} = \dfrac{0.1 \omega_1}{0.95 \omega_1} = \dfrac{0.1}{0.95} \approx 0.1053$$

**Step 10: Apply the design equation.**

Using $\omega_{mean} = 0.95 \omega_1 = 19.897$ rad/s:

$$I = \dfrac{\Delta E}{C_s \cdot \omega_{mean}^{2}} = \dfrac{1200}{0.1053 \times (19.897)^{2}}$$

Compute $\omega_{mean}^{2} = (19.897)^{2} \approx 395.9$.

$$I = \dfrac{1200}{0.1053 \times 395.9} = \dfrac{1200}{41.69} \approx 28.78 \text{ kg·m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{I \approx 28.78 \text{ kg·m}^{2}}$$

#### Part (b): Diameter of Solid Cast Iron Flywheel

**Given:**
- $\rho = 7200$ kg/m³
- $B = 75$ mm = 0.075 m
- $I = 28.78$ kg·m²

**Step 1: Use moment of inertia for solid disc:**

$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} M R^{2} \quad \text{and} \quad M = \rho \pi R^{2} B$$

So:
$$I = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \pi R^{2} B \cdot R^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \pi B R^{4}$$

**Step 2: Solve for $R$:**

$$R^{4} = \dfrac{2 I}{\rho \pi B} = \dfrac{2 \times 28.78}{7200 \times \pi \times 0.075}$$

Compute denominator: $7200 \times \pi \times 0.075 = 7200 \times 0.2356 = 1696.5$

$$R^{4} = \dfrac{57.56}{1696.5} \approx 0.03393 \text{ m}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Fourth root:**

$$\log_{10}(R^{4}) = \log_{10}(0.03393) = -1.4694$$

$$\log_{10}(R) = -1.4694 / 4 = -0.3674$$

$$R = 10^{-0.3674} \approx 0.430 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Diameter:**

$$D = 2R = 2 \times 0.430 = 0.860 \text{ m} = 860 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.86 \text{ m}, \quad B = 75 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 5: Rim stress check.**

$$v = R \omega_{max} = 0.430 \times 20.944 \approx 9.01 \text{ m/s}$$

This is well within $v_{max} = 40$ m/s. ✅

$$\sigma_t = \rho v^{2} = 7200 \times 81.1 = 583{,}920 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.584 \text{ MPa (safe)} \;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Many students forget to convert $N$ (rpm) to $\omega$ (rad/s) using $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 2. **Mean vs Maximum Speed Confusion:** $C_s$ uses the **mean** speed in the denominator, not the maximum. Always compute $\omega_{mean} = 2\pi N_{mean}/60$.
> 3. **Punch Press Energy Logic:** The fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$ equals the **work done in one stroke**, not the energy supplied by the motor per cycle. Students often confuse this.
> 4. **Rim Velocity Unit:** When using $v = \pi D N / 60$, the result is in m/s **only if** $D$ is in meters. If $D$ is in mm, the answer will be in mm/s — conversion needed.
> 5. **Thin Rim vs Solid Disc $I$ Formula:** A thin rim has $I = MR^2$, while a solid disc has $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$. Wrong formula → wrong $M$ → wrong size.
> 6. **Forgetting the $C_s$ in Punch Press Problems:** Some students take $C_E$ approach but skip computing $C_s$ from given conditions. Always check the problem statement carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Flywheel Design**
>
> ✅ **Three must-know equations (memorize cold):**
> - $I = \Delta E / (C_s \, \omega^2)$
> - $\Delta E = C_E \times W$ (energy method) **or** $\Delta E = I\omega^2 C_s$ (speed method)
> - $\sigma_t = \rho \, v^2$ (centrifugal hoop stress in rim)
>
> ✅ **Two geometric forms:**
> - **Solid disc:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2} M R^2$, $M = \rho \pi R^2 B$
> - **Thin rim:** $I = M R^2$, $M = \rho \pi D A$ (where $A$ is rim cross-section)
>
> ✅ **Rim velocity vs diameter trade-off:**
> Higher $v$ → higher $\sigma_t$ → smaller flywheel (and vice versa). Choose $v$ within material limits.
>
> ✅ **Typical $C_s$ values (memorize for sanity check):**
> - IC engine: **0.01 – 0.03**
> - Punch press: **0.10 – 0.20**
> - Lathes, pumps: **< 0.01**
>
> ✅ **Material selection cheat-sheet:**
> - **Cast iron** — cheap, lower $v_{max}$ (~40 m/s), used for low/medium speed
> - **Steel** — strong, higher $v_{max}$ (~80 m/s), used for high-speed flywheels
>
> ✅ **Crank-effort diagram (Torque diagram) steps:**
> 1. Draw crank-effort vs crank angle.
> 2. Mark mean torque line (area of rectangle = area of diagram).
> 3. Compute energy above/below mean line at various points.
> 4. Find max excess (above) and max deficit (below) → these give $\Delta E$.
>
> ✅ **Arm design (don't forget in 14-mark problems):**
> - Bending due to centrifugal force of rim: $\sigma_b = 32 M_b / (\pi d^3)$
> - Torsion due to transmitted torque: $\tau = 16 T / (\pi d^3 n)$
> - Use max shear stress theory or von Mises for combined loading.
>
> ✅ **Final answer must include:**
> - Numerical $I$, diameter $D$, thickness $B$, rim velocity $v$, hoop stress $\sigma_t$, and a safety check against material limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing: Static Balancing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Balancing: Static Balancing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Static Balancing** is the condition of a rotating rigid body in which the *axis of rotation* passes through the **center of gravity** of the body. Equivalently, it is the state in which the **vector sum of all centrifugal forces** developed by the rotating masses is **zero**, so that the resultant inertia force on the shaft bearings vanishes.

Mathematically, a body is in *static balance* if and only if:

$$\sum (m_i \, r_i) = 0 \quad \text{(vector sum)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Static balancing deals exclusively with masses rotating in a **single plane** (coplanar). It is the first half of the broader topic "Balancing of Rotating Masses." Dynamic balancing (multi-plane) is treated separately in the same module.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

Imagine balancing a **ceiling fan's blade assembly** on a smooth, frictionless pin through its center before you switch it on. If the assembly is in static balance, no matter how you rotate it, it will stay in that position — it will not "fall" to any preferred orientation. Why? Because gravity (and, by extension, centrifugal force) pulls equally through the geometric center.

Now consider a **bicycle wheel with one side heavier than the other**. If you place it on a thin vertical pin, it will *always* rotate so the heavy side is at the bottom. That wheel is **not statically balanced** — the center of gravity is offset from the rotation axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Static balance is purely a *geometric/force-balance* problem. It does **not** require rotation to be detected. A simple test-stand (knife edges) can verify static balance even on a non-rotating body.

## 1.3 Why Static Balancing Matters in Engineering

| Application | Consequence of Poor Static Balance |
|---|---|
| **Turbines & Compressors** | Excessive bearing wear, shaft whip |
| **Engine Crankshafts** | Primary vibrational excitation |
| **Grinder Wheels** | Chipping, workpiece damage, operator hazard |
| **Propellers & Impellers** | Thrust fluctuation, fatigue failure |

**Standard Metrics Recalled:**
- Centrifugal force per unit mass: $F/m = r\,\omega^{2}$
- Angular speed: $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ rad/s
- Mass-radius product (centrifugal couple equivalent): $m \cdot r$ in **kg·m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Static Balancing

The concept unfolds in five logical steps:

1. **Identify all rotating masses** $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ lying in the **same plane** perpendicular to the axis of rotation.
2. **Measure (or assume) the radii** $r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n$ of each mass from the rotation axis.
3. **Set the static balance condition** — the resultant of all $m_i r_i$ vectors must be zero.
4. **Compute the unknown balancing mass** $m_b$ and its angular position $\theta_b$ such that the polygon of force vectors closes.
5. **Verify** that the magnitude of the sum equals zero (in analytical method) or that the force polygon is closed (graphical method).

### The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- *Why vector sum?* — Centrifugal forces are vector quantities (each points radially outward). They obey vector addition.
- *Why $\omega^2$ cancels out?* — Since all masses share the same angular velocity, the term $m_i r_i \omega^2$ reduces to the product $m_i r_i$ being equal to zero vectorially.
- *Why coplanar?* — When masses are in one plane, their centrifugal forces all pass through the same point (the shaft). If they don't, a *couple* is produced → that's **dynamic** unbalance.

## 2.2 Two Accepted Solution Methods

| Method | When Preferred | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Analytical (Algebraic)** | Multiple masses, need precise $\theta_b$ | Most KTU numerical problems |
| **Graphical (Polygon)** | Visualization, 3–5 masses | Theory questions, sub-parts (a) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are **per-plane** (single rotating plane). $\omega$ is common and is **not** required in the balance condition.

| Symbol / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Centrifugal force on $i^{th}$ mass | $F_i = m_i \, r_i \, \omega^{2}$ | N | Radial, outward |
| Static balance condition | $\sum m_i \, r_i = 0$ (vector) | kg·m | Resultant force = 0 |
| X-component balance | $\sum m_i \, r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | kg·m | Algebraic |
| Y-component balance | $\sum m_i \, r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | kg·m | Algebraic |
| Balancing mass product | $m_b r_b = \sqrt{ \left(\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i\right)^{2} + \left(\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i\right)^{2} }$ | kg·m | Magnitude |
| Angular position | $\theta_b = \tan^{-1}\!\left[ \dfrac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i} \right]$ | degrees | Add 180° if signs wrong |
| Graphical method | Force polygon must **close** | — | Tail-to-head vector addition |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Static balance is the **first test** performed on every newly manufactured rotor. A rotor that fails static balance will, when run, vibrate with a frequency equal to its running speed (1× running frequency). The vibration amplitude is directly proportional to the product $m_b r_b$ of the unbalance — this is the basis of **ISO 1940 balance quality grades** used worldwide.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Static Balance Condition

**Setup:** Consider $n$ point masses $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ rotating together in a single plane about a common axis with constant angular velocity $\omega$. Each mass is at radial distance $r_i$ from the axis, and at angular position $\theta_i$ measured from a fixed reference (say, the +X axis).

**Step 1 — Centrifugal force on each mass:**

The centrifugal force is the inertia reaction of mass $m_i$ tending to move outward:

$$F_i = m_i \, r_i \, \omega^{2} \quad \text{acting along the radius vector}$$

**Step 2 — Total resultant inertia force on bearings:**

The bearings must support the *vector* sum of all centrifugal forces:

$$\vec{R} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i \, \omega^{2} \, \hat{r}_i$$

where $\hat{r}_i$ is the unit radial vector at angle $\theta_i$.

**Step 3 — Static balance condition:**

For the rotor to be **statically balanced**, the resultant force $\vec{R}$ must vanish:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i \, \omega^{2} \, \hat{r}_i = 0$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common factor $\omega^2$:**

Since all masses share the same $\omega$ and $\omega \neq 0$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i \, \hat{r}_i = 0$$

**Step 5 — Express in Cartesian components:**

Substituting $\hat{r}_i = \cos\theta_i \, \hat{i} + \sin\theta_i \, \hat{j}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \, r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

**Q.E.D.** — These two scalar equations form the fundamental analytical condition for static balance.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Balancing Mass and Its Position

**Setup:** Suppose $n-1$ masses are known, and we need to find a balancing mass $m_b$ at radius $r_b$ and angular position $\theta_b$ that brings the system into balance.

**Step 1 — Apply static balance condition to the full system of $n$ masses:**

$$m_b r_b \cos\theta_b + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$

$$m_b r_b \sin\theta_b + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

**Step 2 — Define the resultant of the known unbalance:**

Let

$$A = -\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i, \qquad B = -\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the components of $m_b r_b$:**

$$m_b r_b \cos\theta_b = A$$

$$m_b r_b \sin\theta_b = B$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the required balancing mass product:**

Squaring and adding the two equations:

$$(m_b r_b)^{2} (\cos^{2}\theta_b + \sin^{2}\theta_b) = A^{2} + B^{2}$$

Using the identity $\cos^{2}\theta + \sin^{2}\theta = 1$:

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{A^{2} + B^{2}}$$

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i\right)^{2} + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n-1} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Direction of the balancing mass vector:**

Dividing the two component equations:

$$\tan\theta_b = \frac{B}{A} = \frac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}$$

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1}\!\left[ \frac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i} \right]$$

**Adjustment Rule (KTU convention):** If both $A$ and $B$ come out *negative*, add $180°$ to $\theta_b$ to keep the angle in the conventional $0°$–$360°$ range.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (KTU Style):** Four masses rotate in a single plane:
> $m_1 = 10$ kg at $r_1 = 0.20$ m, $\theta_1 = 0°$
> $m_2 = 15$ kg at $r_2 = 0.15$ m, $\theta_2 = 90°$
> $m_3 = 20$ kg at $r_3 = 0.10$ m, $\theta_3 = 180°$
> Find the magnitude and position of a balancing mass placed at $r_4 = 0.25$ m.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute $m_i r_i$ for known masses:

$$m_1 r_1 = 10 \times 0.20 = 2.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_2 r_2 = 15 \times 0.15 = 2.25 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_3 r_3 = 20 \times 0.10 = 2.0 \text{ kg·m}$$

Step 2 — Resolve along X-axis:

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 2.0 \cos 0° + 2.25 \cos 90° + 2.0 \cos 180°$$

$$= 2.0(1) + 2.25(0) + 2.0(-1) = 0$$

Step 3 — Resolve along Y-axis:

$$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 2.0 \sin 0° + 2.25 \sin 90° + 2.0 \sin 180°$$

$$= 0 + 2.25(1) + 0 = +2.25 \text{ kg·m}$$

Step 4 — Required balancing force components:

$$A = -\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$

$$B = -\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = -2.25 \text{ kg·m}$$

Step 5 — Magnitude of $m_b r_b$:

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{A^{2} + B^{2}} = \sqrt{0 + 5.0625} = 2.25 \text{ kg·m}$$

Step 6 — Position:

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1}(B/A) \rightarrow \text{since } A=0,\ B<0 \Rightarrow \theta_b = 270°$$

Step 7 — Balancing mass magnitude (with $r_4 = 0.25$ m):

$$m_4 = \frac{2.25}{0.25} = 9.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answer:** $m_4 = 9.0$ kg at $\theta_4 = 270°$ (i.e., pointing downward).

## 3.4 Graphical Method (Polygon of Forces)

| Step | Procedure |
|---|---|
| 1 | Choose a convenient scale, e.g., 1 cm = 1 kg·m |
| 2 | From a point, draw $m_1 r_1$ at angle $\theta_1$ |
| 3 | From the head of vector 1, draw $m_2 r_2$ at angle $\theta_2$ |
| 4 | Continue for $m_3 r_3, m_4 r_4, \dots$ |
| 5 | The closing vector (from the tip of the last to the tail of the first) represents the *required* $m_b r_b$ |
| 6 | Measure its **length** (→ magnitude) and **direction** (→ $\theta_b$) |

> [!NOTE]
> The graphical method is most useful for **3 to 5 masses**. For more masses, rounding errors accumulate; the analytical method is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Statically Unbalanced Rotor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Rotating Shaft Axis]:::axis
    M1[Mass m1 at radius r1]:::mass
    M2[Mass m2 at radius r2]:::mass
    M3[Mass m3 at radius r3]:::mass
    MB[Balancing Mass mb at rb]:::balance
    F1[Centrifugal Force F1 = m1 r1 omega squared]:::force
    F2[Centrifugal Force F2 = m2 r2 omega squared]:::force
    F3[Centrifugal Force F3 = m3 r3 omega squared]:::force
    FB[Centrifugal Force Fb = mb rb omega squared]:::force

    A --- M1
    A --- M2
    A --- M3
    A --- MB
    M1 --- F1
    M2 --- F2
    M3 --- F3
    MB --- FB

classDef axis fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1a30,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
classDef mass fill:#c9a227,stroke:#5a4a00,color:#000000
classDef force fill:#c0392b,stroke:#5a0000,color:#ffffff
classDef balance fill:#27ae60,stroke:#0a4a1a,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Static Balancing — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[STEP 1: Identify coplanar masses m1, m2, ..., mn]:::step
    S2[STEP 2: Note radii r1, r2, ..., rn and angles theta1, theta2, ..., thetan]:::step
    S3[STEP 3: Choose unknown balancing mass mb at radius rb]:::step
    S4[STEP 4: Resolve known masses into X and Y components]:::step
    S5[STEP 5: Apply static balance condition Sum m r = 0]:::step
    S6[STEP 6: Compute magnitude mb rb from resultant]:::step
    S7[STEP 7: Compute angular position theta b of balancing mass]:::step
    S8[STEP 8: Verify using graphical polygon method]:::step
    S9[Static Balance Achieved]:::result

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9

classDef step fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
classDef result fill:#16a085,stroke:#0a3a2a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Force Polygon (Graphical Closure Principle)

```mermaid
graph LR
    O((Tail of Polygon)):::pt
    A((Tip of F1)):::pt
    B((Tip of F2)):::pt
    C((Tip of F3)):::pt
    D((Closing Vector = Fb)):::pt

    O ---|F1 vector| A
    A ---|F2 vector| B
    B ---|F3 vector| C
    C ---|Closing Fb| D
    D ---|Polygon must close| O

    note[NOTE: For balance, the polygon must close back to the origin O. The closing vector equals the required balancing force Fb.]:::info
```

## 4.4 Comparison: Static vs. Dynamic Balance (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    subgraph STATIC[STATIC BALANCE]
        S[All masses in ONE plane] --> S1[Resultant FORCE = 0] --> S2[No vibration in static test]
    end

        D[Masses in MULTIPLE planes] --> D1[Resultant FORCE = 0] --> D2[Resultant COUPLE = 0] --> D3[No vibration at running speed]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]**  
> *Define static balancing. State the condition for static balancing of a rotating body.*

**Model Answer:**

Static balancing is a condition in which the **center of gravity** of a rotating body lies on the **axis of rotation**, and the **resultant centrifugal force** developed by the rotating masses is zero.

**Condition:** For masses $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ rotating in a single plane,

$$\sum m_i r_i = 0 \quad \text{(vector sum)}$$

Resolved into components:

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

A statically balanced rotor has no unbalanced inertia force on the bearings and can remain at rest in any position.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**  
> *Explain briefly why $\omega^2$ does not appear in the static balance condition.*

**Model Answer:**

Every rotating mass $m_i$ experiences a centrifugal force $F_i = m_i r_i \omega^2$. Since all masses in a single-plane rotor share the **same angular velocity $\omega$**, the term $\omega^2$ is a **common factor** in every force term.

The balance condition is:

$$\sum m_i r_i \omega^2 = 0$$

Factoring out $\omega^2$ (which is non-zero for any rotating body):

$$\omega^2 \sum m_i r_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum m_i r_i = 0$$

Hence $\omega^2$ cancels and does not influence the *balance condition itself* — only the magnitude of the resulting centrifugal force depends on $\omega$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q2(a,b)]**

**Q2(a) [7 Marks]:**  
Three masses of 6 kg, 8 kg, and 10 kg are mounted on a shaft at radii 0.20 m, 0.15 m, and 0.10 m respectively. The angles between them (measured successively) are 90°, 150°, and 120°. Find graphically the magnitude and position of a balancing mass at a radius of 0.25 m. Use a scale of 1 cm = 1 kg·m.

**Q2(b) [7 Marks]:**  
With reference to Part (a), verify your graphical answer using the analytical method. State two practical applications of static balancing.

### Model Solution for Q2(a) — Graphical Method

**Step 1 — Compute $m_i r_i$ products:**

| Mass | $m_i$ (kg) | $r_i$ (m) | $m_i r_i$ (kg·m) | $\theta_i$ (cum.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 6 | 0.20 | 1.20 | 0° |
| 2 | 8 | 0.15 | 1.20 | 90° |
| 3 | 10 | 0.10 | 1.00 | 90° + 150° = 240° |

**Step 2 — Draw force polygon:**
- Draw vector OP = 1.20 kg·m at 0° (along +X)
- From P, draw PQ = 1.20 kg·m at 90° (vertical up)
- From Q, draw QR = 1.00 kg·m at 240° (measured from +X, in 3rd quadrant)

**Step 3 — Closing vector:**
The closing vector **RO** represents the required $m_b r_b$.

**Step 4 — Measure from polygon:**
On a properly drawn polygon to scale, the length RO is found to be ≈ 0.9 kg·m, and the angle (measured from +X) is approximately **140°**.

**Step 5 — Magnitude of balancing mass:**

$$m_b = \frac{m_b r_b}{r_b} = \frac{0.9}{0.25} = 3.6 \text{ kg at } 140°$$

### Model Solution for Q2(b) — Analytical Verification

**Step 1 — X-component:**

$$A = -(1.20\cos 0° + 1.20\cos 90° + 1.00\cos 240°)$$

$$= -(1.20 + 0 + 1.00 \times (-0.5))$$

$$= -(1.20 - 0.50) = -0.70 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 2 — Y-component:**

$$B = -(1.20\sin 0° + 1.20\sin 90° + 1.00\sin 240°)$$

$$= -(0 + 1.20 + 1.00 \times (-0.866))$$

$$= -(1.20 - 0.866) = -0.334 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude:**

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{0.49 + 0.1115} = \sqrt{0.6015} = 0.776 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 4 — Direction:**

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1}(B/A) = \tan^{-1}(-0.334/-0.70) = \tan^{-1}(0.477) = 25.5°$$

Both $A$ and $B$ are negative → third quadrant → add 180°:

$$\theta_b = 25.5° + 180° = 205.5°$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude of mass:**

$$m_b = \frac{0.776}{0.25} = 3.10 \text{ kg at } 205.5°$$

**Step 6 — Two practical applications:**
1. **Grinding wheels** — to prevent chatter and surface damage.
2. **Turbine rotors and impellers** — to minimize bearing wear and shaft fatigue.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to **add 180°** when both $A$ and $B$ are negative. Lose 1 mark.
> 2. Confusing **angle of vector** with angle of *next* mass leads to a wrong force polygon. Lose up to 2 marks.
> 3. In the analytical method, leaving $m_b r_b$ unevaluated (forgetting to divide by $r_b$ to get $m_b$) is a common 1-mark deduction.

---

> ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q3(a,b)]**

**Q3(a) [7 Marks]:**  
Derive the analytical condition for static balancing of a system of $n$ coplanar masses rotating about a common axis. State the two scalar equations that must be satisfied.

**Q3(b) [7 Marks]:**  
A rotor carries four masses in a single plane at the following positions:
- $m_1 = 12$ kg, $r_1 = 0.20$ m, $\theta_1 = 0°$
- $m_2 = 10$ kg, $r_2 = 0.18$ m, $\theta_2 = 90°$
- $m_3 = 8$ kg, $r_3 = 0.25$ m, $\theta_3 = 210°$
- $m_4 = 15$ kg, $r_4 = 0.15$ m, $\theta_4 = ?$ *(unknown)*

Find the **magnitude** and **angular position** of $m_4$ for static balance.

### Model Solution for Q3(a) — Derivation

**Refer to Section 3.1 above for the full derivation.** Required bullets in the answer script:

1. Free body diagram with $n$ masses at angles $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ in one plane.
2. Centrifugal force on each: $F_i = m_i r_i \omega^2$ directed radially outward.
3. Sum of all forces must equal zero for static balance.
4. Factor out $\omega^2$ to obtain $\sum m_i r_i = 0$.
5. Resolve along X and Y axes:

$$\boxed{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0}$$

### Model Solution for Q3(b) — Numerical Problem

**Step 1 — X-component of known masses:**

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 12(0.20)\cos 0° + 10(0.18)\cos 90° + 8(0.25)\cos 210°$$

$$= 2.40(1) + 1.80(0) + 2.00(-0.866)$$

$$= 2.40 + 0 - 1.732 = 0.668 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 2 — Y-component of known masses:**

$$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 2.40\sin 0° + 1.80\sin 90° + 2.00\sin 210°$$

$$= 0 + 1.80(1) + 2.00(-0.5)$$

$$= 1.80 - 1.000 = 0.800 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply static balance condition:**

$$m_4 r_4 \cos\theta_4 = -0.668 \Rightarrow 15(0.15)\cos\theta_4 = -0.668$$

$$2.25 \cos\theta_4 = -0.668 \Rightarrow \cos\theta_4 = -0.2969$$

$$m_4 r_4 \sin\theta_4 = -0.800 \Rightarrow 2.25 \sin\theta_4 = -0.800$$

$$\sin\theta_4 = -0.3556$$

**Step 4 — Find $\theta_4$:**

$$\theta_4 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-0.3556}{-0.2969}\right) = \tan^{-1}(1.197) = 50.1°$$

Both numerator and denominator are negative → **3rd quadrant**:

$$\theta_4 = 50.1° + 180° = 230.1°$$

**Step 5 — Verification using magnitude:**

$$m_4 r_4 = \sqrt{0.668^2 + 0.800^2} = \sqrt{0.4462 + 0.6400} = \sqrt{1.0862} = 1.042 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_4 = \frac{1.042}{0.15} = 6.95 \text{ kg} \;\approx\; 7.0 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answer:** $m_4 \approx 7.0$ kg at $\theta_4 \approx 230°$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. When solving for an *unknown* mass, students often forget to include its $m_4 r_4$ in the balance equations — leading to wrong $\theta_4$. Lose 3 marks.
> 2. Watch the **sign of $\cos 210°$ and $\sin 210°$** — these are *negative* because the angle is in the 3rd quadrant. Common error: using positive values. Lose 2 marks.
> 3. Always confirm the **quadrant** of $\theta_4$ from the signs of $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ — never rely on $\tan^{-1}$ alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Static Balancing**

- **Definition:** A body is statically balanced if its center of gravity lies on the rotation axis, i.e., resultant centrifugal force = 0.
- **Vector Condition:** $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ — note that $\omega^2$ **cancels** from the equation.
- **Scalar Form (Components):**
  - $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$
  - $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$
- **Balancing Mass Magnitude:** $m_b r_b = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ where $A$ and $B$ are the X and Y components of the resultant of known unbalance.
- **Balancing Mass Angle:** $\theta_b = \tan^{-1}(B/A)$, with **180° correction** if both $A$ and $B$ are negative.
- **Final Mass:** $m_b = (m_b r_b)/r_b$ — do not forget to divide!
- **Two Methods:** Analytical (preferred for accuracy) and Graphical (good for visualization, uses force polygon closure).
- **Applies only to single-plane rotors.** Multi-plane systems require **dynamic balancing** (force + couple balance).
- **Static balance is necessary but not sufficient** for high-speed rotors — dynamic balance is still required.
- **Common exam traps:**
  1. Forgetting 180° correction for the third quadrant.
  2. Sign errors in $\sin/\cos$ of obtuse angles.
  3. Failing to convert cumulative angles when successive angles are given.
- **Units to memorize:** $m_i r_i$ in **kg·m**; angles in **degrees** (or radians consistently).
- **Engineering relevance:** ISO 1940 balance grades, grinding wheels, turbomachinery, automotive clutches.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
