<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Balancing** is the process of distributing the masses of a rotating body (rotor) such that the **resultant centrifugal force** is zero AND the **resultant couple** (moment) about the axis of rotation is also zero. When both these conditions are satisfied simultaneously for all positions of rotation, the rotor is said to be in *complete dynamic balance*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, dynamic balancing is treated as a direct extension of single-plane (static) balancing, with the additional requirement of **couple elimination** when the masses lie in *different axial planes*.

### The Two Governing Conditions of Dynamic Balance

A multi-plane rotating system is dynamically balanced if and only if:

1. **Force Condition:** The vector sum of all centrifugal forces developed by the unbalanced masses equals **zero**.
$$\sum \vec{F}_c = 0$$

2. **Couple Condition:** The algebraic sum of the moments (couples) of all these centrifugal forces about ANY reference plane is **zero**.
$$\sum \vec{M}_c = 0$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Office Chair Wheel"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a car's alloy wheel.** When you place a tiny lead weight on one side of the rim (because the tire has a heavy spot), the wheel no longer "wobbles" at low speed — this is *static* balance. However, at highway speeds (≈ 100 km/h), the wheel still vibrates violently because there is a **wobbling couple** acting between the front and back faces of the wheel. The mechanic must therefore add a second weight on the *opposite face* to cancel the couple. **That second weight is what converts static balance into dynamic balance.**

The same principle applies to:
- **Turbine rotors** in jet engines
- **Centrifugal pump impellers**
- **Grinding wheels** and **flywheels**
- **Crankshafts** of I.C. engines

### 1.3 Why Dynamic Balancing is Non-Negotiable in Engineering

| System at Risk | Consequence of Unbalance |
|---|---|
| **Turbogenerator** (3000 rpm) | Bearing failure within hours |
| **Aircraft engine** | Catastrophic structural fatigue |
| **Paper mill rollers** | Strip vibration, poor product quality |
| **Grinding wheel** | Wheel disintegration, operator injury |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Rotors operating above **1000 rpm** are classified as *high-speed machinery* and **must** undergo dynamic balancing. The permissible residual unbalance is typically expressed in **gram-millimetres (g·mm)** and is prescribed by ISO 1940-1 balance quality grades.

### 1.4 Classification of Balancing (KTU Module 3 Scope)

```
Balancing
├── 1. Static Balancing (Single Plane)
│      └── m·r products are equal in magnitude
│           and opposite in direction
│
├── 2. Dynamic Balancing (Two or More Planes)
│      ├── Analytical / Polygon Method
│      ├── Influence Coefficient Method
│      └── Matrix (Transfer-Function) Method
│
└── 3. Field Balancing
       └── Trial-weight correction on the machine
```

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Origin of Unbalance — The Centrifugal Force

Consider a mass *m* attached to a rotor at radius *r*, rotating with angular velocity *ω* rad/s. The mass experiences a **centrifugal force** directed radially outward:

$$F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$

When we deal with the *balancing* problem, we factor out the common term *ω²* (since it is the same for every mass on the same shaft) and work with the **mass-radius product**, denoted as:

$$m \cdot r \quad [\text{kg·m or g·mm}]$$

This product is the *unbalance magnitude* and forms the **fundamental vector quantity** in every balancing calculation.

## 2.2 Why Single-Plane Balancing Fails for Long Rotors

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A rotor can be *statically* balanced (no net force at any instant) yet still vibrate violently. This happens when the mass-centre coincides with the **geometric axis** but there is still a **mass-axis parallel offset** at different axial locations. The two offsets form a *couple* that rotates with the shaft and produces lateral vibration at the bearings.

This is precisely why the **couple condition** is added in dynamic balancing.

## 2.3 Two-Plane Balancing — The Reference Plane Concept

For a rotor with *n* unbalance masses located in *two* transverse planes (say Plane L at the left bearing and Plane R at the right bearing), we assign a **reference plane** to convert the problem into a vector polygon construction.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Two-Plane Balancing

1. **List all unbalance vectors** *mᵢrᵢ* in their respective planes with their angular positions.
2. **Shift all vectors to the reference plane** by adding (or subtracting) a corrective mass in the reference plane — this gives a *static* balance.
3. **Form the force polygon** for the *shifted* vectors — the closing side represents the **residual couple** *C*.
4. **Divide the couple** *C* between the two actual balancing planes (L and R) using the lever-arm ratio:
$$\frac{m_L r_L}{m_R r_R} = \frac{b}{a}$$
   where *a* and *b* are the distances of the balancing planes from the resultant couple plane.

## 2.4 Three-Plane and Four-Plane Balancing

| # Planes | Method Used | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|
| **2 planes** | Force + Couple polygons | Short rigid rotors |
| **3 planes** | Influence coefficient method | Intermediate-length rotors |
| **4 planes** | Matrix method | Long flexible rotors (e.g., turbogenerators) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Module 3)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$ | Centrifugal force on a single mass | N |
| 2 | $u = m \cdot r$ | Unbalance (mass-radius product) | kg·m |
| 3 | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Force balance — X component | kg·m |
| 4 | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Force balance — Y component | kg·m |
| 5 | $\sum m_i r_i \cdot x_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Couple balance — X axis | kg·m² |
| 6 | $\sum m_i r_i \cdot x_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Couple balance — Y axis | kg·m² |
| 7 | $I_C = m_C \cdot r_C$ | Couple polygon closure vector | kg·m |
| 8 | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Angular speed from rpm | rad/s |
| 9 | $m_L \cdot r_L = C \cdot \dfrac{b}{L}$ | Plane-L correction | kg·m |
| 10 | $m_R \cdot r_R = C \cdot \dfrac{a}{L}$ | Plane-R correction | kg·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Board Exams:** Always convert angles from a **clock diagram (0° at 12 o'clock, clockwise positive)** to the **mathematical convention (0° at 3 o'clock, anticlockwise positive)** before doing the trigonometry. Most KTU answer-script errors arise from this single conversion.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ISO 1940-1 Compliance:** Determines permissible residual unbalance *G* in mm/s for a given service speed.
- **Shop-Floor Balancing Machines:** Use the *influence coefficient* technique to measure vibration response at trial weights and compute the corrective mass.
- **In-Situ Balancing of Turbines:** The matrix method allows on-site correction without dismantling the machine.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Centrifugal Force Expression

Consider a point mass *m* constrained to move in a circle of radius *r* with constant angular velocity *ω*.

The instantaneous position vector is:

$$\vec{r}_i = r \cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + r \sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}$$

Differentiating twice with respect to time:

$$\vec{a}_i = \dfrac{d^2\vec{r}_i}{dt^2} = -r\omega^2 \cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} - r\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}$$

$$\vec{a}_i = -r\omega^2 \left[\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + \sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}\right]$$

The magnitude of this acceleration is:

$$\vert \vec{a}_i \vert = r\omega^2$$

Applying Newton's second law, the centrifugal force (acting radially outward on the mass) is:

$$\vec{F}_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \quad \text{(radially outward)}$$

The vector form, in complex-plane representation, is:

$$\vec{F}_c = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \, e^{j(\omega t + \phi)}$$

For a *rigid rotor*, since all masses share the same *ω*, the factor *ω²* is constant. Therefore the balancing condition reduces to the *m·r* polygon.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Two-Plane Balancing Equations

Consider a shaft carrying masses *m₁, m₂, m₃, …, mₙ* located in two transverse planes **L** and **R**, separated by distance *L*. The angular position of each mass is measured from an arbitrary reference.

### 3.2.1 Force Equilibrium (Σ F = 0)

The sum of all unbalance vectors must close on itself:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \, e^{j\theta_i} = 0$$

Splitting into real and imaginary parts:

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### 3.2.2 Couple Equilibrium (Σ M = 0)

Taking moments about Plane L, with Plane R at distance *L*:

$$\sum m_i r_i \, x_i \, e^{j\theta_i} = 0$$

where $x_i$ is the axial distance of mass *i* from Plane L.

This expands to:

$$\sum m_i r_i \, x_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i \, x_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Algebraic Insight:** Equations (i) & (ii) eliminate the net force, while (iii) & (iv) eliminate the net couple. Together, they guarantee *complete* dynamic balance — no vibration at the bearings at any speed.

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — KTU 14-Mark Style

> **Problem:** A rotor carries four masses in two planes L and R, separated by 600 mm. The mass-radius products and angular positions (measured from a fixed reference, clockwise positive) are:
>
> | Mass | Plane | *m·r* (kg·m) | Angle θᵢ | Axial dist. from L (mm) |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | A | L | 0.30 | 0° | 0 |
> | B | L | 0.40 | 90° | 0 |
> | C | R | 0.25 | 135° | 600 |
> | D | R | 0.20 | 270° | 600 |
>
> Find the magnitude and angular position of the balance masses to be placed in Planes L and R at a radius of 200 mm.

### Step 1 — Convert angles to mathematical convention (anticlockwise from +X axis)

Clockwise positive → Anticlockwise positive means **multiply the angle by −1** (i.e., 360° − θ).

| Mass | θ (math) | cos θ | sin θ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0° | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| B | 90° | 0.000 | 1.000 |
| C | 225° | −0.707 | −0.707 |
| D | 90° | 0.000 | 1.000 |

### Step 2 — Sum the unbalance vectors in each plane

**Plane L components:**
- ΣX = (0.30)(1) + (0.40)(0) = **+0.30 kg·m**
- ΣY = (0.30)(0) + (0.40)(1) = **+0.40 kg·m**
- Resultant magnitude $R_L = \sqrt{0.30^2 + 0.40^2} = 0.50$ kg·m
- Angle $\phi_L = \tan^{-1}(0.40 / 0.30) = 53.13°$

**Plane R components:**
- ΣX = (0.25)(−0.707) + (0.20)(0) = **−0.1768 kg·m**
- ΣY = (0.25)(−0.707) + (0.20)(1) = **+0.0232 kg·m**
- Resultant magnitude $R_R = \sqrt{0.1768^2 + 0.0232^2} = 0.1783$ kg·m
- Angle $\phi_R = 180° - \tan^{-1}(0.0232/0.1768) = 180° - 7.48° = 172.5°$

### Step 3 — Apply the Balance Conditions

For Plane L, the corrective mass-radius product $m_L r_L$ is equal and opposite to $R_L$:

$$m_L r_L = 0.50 \text{ kg·m at angle } (53.13° + 180°) = 233.13°$$

Given $r_L = 0.20$ m:

$$m_L = \dfrac{0.50}{0.20} = 2.50 \text{ kg}$$

For Plane R, the corrective mass-radius product is equal and opposite to $R_R$:

$$m_R r_R = 0.1783 \text{ kg·m at angle } (172.5° + 180°) = 352.5°$$

Given $r_R = 0.20$ m:

$$m_R = \dfrac{0.1783}{0.20} = 0.892 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Verification of Couple Condition

The two corrective vectors must be separated by axial distance 600 mm but should produce zero couple with the *original* masses. This is automatically satisfied by the *opposite-polarity* placement we just derived.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (KTU Board Format):**
> - Plane L: **2.50 kg at 200 mm radius, at 233.13°** (mathematical convention)
> - Plane R: **0.892 kg at 200 mm radius, at 352.5°** (mathematical convention)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Dynamic Balancing Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor with Unbalanced Masses] --> B[Measure m-r Product and Angular Position of Each Mass]
    B --> C[Define Reference Plane R0]
    C --> D[Shift All Unbalance Vectors to R0 Using Axial Lever Rule]
    D --> E[Form Force Polygon: Sum of m-r Vectors]
    E --> F{Check: Is Resultant Zero?}
    F -- No --> G[Compute Residual Force and Residual Couple]
    G --> H[Divide Couple Between End Planes A and B]
    H --> I[Compute Balance Mass in Plane A: mA-rA]
    H --> J[Compute Balance Mass in Plane B: mB-rB]
    F -- Yes --> K[Rotor is Dynamically Balanced]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Verify by Re-running the Shaft at Operating Speed]
```

## 4.2 Vector Polygon for Force and Couple Balance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FPL[Force Polygon - Plane L]
        L1[m1-r1] --> L2[m2-r2]
        L2 --> L3[m3-r3]
        L3 --> LR[mL-rL - Closing Side]
    end
    subgraph FPR[Couple Polygon - Plane R]
        R1[m4-r4] --> R2[m5-r5]
        R2 --> R3[m6-r6]
        R3 --> RR[mR-rR - Closing Side]
    end
    LR -.Residual Force.-> X[Zero Vector]
    RR -.Residual Couple.-> X
```

## 4.3 Multi-Plane Decision Tree (KTU Examiner-Oriented)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S([Start: Identify Number of Unbalance Planes]) --> S1{One Plane?}
    S1 -- Yes --> P1[Use Static Balancing - Single Polygon]
    S1 -- No --> S2{Two Planes?}
    S2 -- Yes --> P2[Use Force plus Couple Polygon Method]
    S2 -- No --> S3{Three or More Planes?}
    S3 -- Yes --> P3[Use Influence Coefficient Method]
    P3 --> P4[Number of Unknowns = 2 x Number of Planes]
    P4 --> P5[Set up Linear System: Ax = b]
    P5 --> P6[Solve by Matrix Inversion or Gauss Elimination]
    P6 --> P7[Verify on Balancing Machine]
    S3 -- No --> P8[Check Problem Statement]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Influence Coefficient Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Original Unbalance: Vector U0] --> B0[Place Trial Mass mT in Plane J]
    B0 --> C0[Measure Vibration Response Vector VJ at Plane I]
    C0 --> D0[Influence Coefficient alpha-IJ = VJ divided by mT]
    D0 --> E0[Repeat for Each Trial Plane to Get Matrix alpha]
    E0 --> F0[Balance Mass: mJ = -1 divided by alpha-JJ times U0]
    F0 --> G0[Apply Balance Mass and Re-measure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Architecture Instead of Physical Drawings?**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme examiner pattern rewards the *methodology* over the *artwork*. The block diagrams above map the **decision flow** and **vector operations** far more clearly than any hand-drawn rotor sketch. They are fully compilable and follow all Mermaid safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, plain-text labels).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

### Q1. Differentiate clearly between static balancing and dynamic balancing. Under what operating condition does a rotor that is statically balanced still require dynamic balancing?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Static balancing** requires the *centre of mass* of the rotor to lie on the axis of rotation. It eliminates the net centrifugal *force* but does **not** guarantee the elimination of a *couple* (moment). (1 mark)
- **Dynamic balancing** requires both the net centrifugal *force* **and** the net centrifugal *couple* about the rotation axis to be zero. (1 mark)
- A long, slender rotor operating at **high angular speeds** (typically > 1000 rpm) will exhibit *couple-induced vibration* even when statically balanced, because the residual mass distribution forms a *whirling couple* about the bearing axis. (1 mark)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

### Q2. State the two fundamental conditions that must be simultaneously satisfied for complete dynamic balancing of a multi-plane rotor.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The **vector sum of all centrifugal forces** developed by the unbalanced masses must be equal to zero:
$$\sum m_i r_i \, e^{j\theta_i} = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$
2. The **algebraic sum of the moments** (couples) of these forces about any chosen reference plane must be zero:
$$\sum m_i r_i \, x_i \, e^{j\theta_i} = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$
3. Both conditions must be **simultaneously satisfied** for dynamic balance to be achieved at all operating speeds. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1)

**(a)** A shaft carries three masses in two planes L and R. The mass-radius products and angular positions (measured clockwise from the vertical) are:

| Mass | Plane | *m·r* (kg·m) | Angle (clockwise from vertical) | Distance from L (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | L | 0.60 | 60° | 0 |
| 2 | L | 0.50 | 150° | 0 |
| 3 | R | 0.80 | 240° | 500 |

Determine the magnitudes and angular positions of the balance masses required in planes L and R at a radius of 250 mm. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain, with the help of neat sketches, the influence coefficient method of dynamic balancing. Why is this method preferred for rotors with three or more balancing planes? (7 marks)

---

### ✅ SOLUTION TO QUESTION A (14 MARKS)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Convert clock angles to mathematical (anticlockwise from +X axis)**
The reference line is the *vertical* (12 o'clock). Converting to standard math convention (anticlockwise from +X horizontal), each clock angle θ_clock becomes:
$$\theta_{math} = 90° - \theta_{clock}$$
- Mass 1: 90° − 60° = **30°**
- Mass 2: 90° − 150° = **−60°** (or 300°)
- Mass 3: 90° − 240° = **−150°** (or 210°)

**Step 2: Compute components in Plane L**

For Mass 1 (m·r = 0.60 at 30°):
- X = 0.60 × cos 30° = 0.60 × 0.866 = +0.5196
- Y = 0.60 × sin 30° = 0.60 × 0.500 = +0.3000

For Mass 2 (m·r = 0.50 at 300°):
- X = 0.50 × cos 300° = 0.50 × 0.500 = +0.2500
- Y = 0.50 × sin 300° = 0.50 × (−0.866) = −0.4330

**Plane L sums:**
$$\Sigma X_L = 0.5196 + 0.2500 = +0.7696 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$\Sigma Y_L = 0.3000 - 0.4330 = -0.1330 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Plane L resultant magnitude and angle:**
$$R_L = \sqrt{0.7696^2 + 0.1330^2} = \sqrt{0.5923 + 0.0177} = \sqrt{0.6100} = 0.7810 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\phi_L = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{-0.1330}{+0.7696}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.1728) = -9.81°$$

Since X is positive and Y is negative, the vector lies in the **fourth quadrant**:
$$\phi_L = 360° - 9.81° = 350.19° \text{ (math convention)}$$

**Step 3: Balance mass for Plane L**

The corrective vector is **equal and opposite** to R_L:
$$m_L r_L = 0.7810 \text{ kg·m at } (350.19° + 180°) = 170.19°$$

Given $r_L = 0.25$ m:
$$m_L = \dfrac{0.7810}{0.25} = 3.124 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Plane R resultant**

For Mass 3 (m·r = 0.80 at 210°):
- X = 0.80 × cos 210° = 0.80 × (−0.866) = −0.6928
- Y = 0.80 × sin 210° = 0.80 × (−0.500) = −0.4000

**Plane R sums:**
$$\Sigma X_R = -0.6928 \text{ kg·m}$$
$$\Sigma Y_R = -0.4000 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Plane R resultant magnitude and angle:**
$$R_R = \sqrt{0.6928^2 + 0.4000^2} = \sqrt{0.4800 + 0.1600} = \sqrt{0.6400} = 0.8000 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\phi_R = 180° + \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{0.4000}{0.6928}\right) = 180° + 30° = 210° \text{ (math convention)}$$

**Step 5: Balance mass for Plane R**

The corrective vector is equal and opposite to R_R:
$$m_R r_R = 0.8000 \text{ kg·m at } (210° + 180°) = 30°$$

Given $r_R = 0.25$ m:
$$m_R = \dfrac{0.8000}{0.25} = 3.200 \text{ kg}$$

#### ✅ Final Answer for Part (a) [7 marks]

| Plane | Balance Mass (kg) | Radius (mm) | Angular Position (math) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L** | **3.124** | 250 | **170.19°** |
| **R** | **3.200** | 250 | **30.00°** |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Influence Coefficient Method

**Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):**
The *influence coefficient* method treats the balancing problem as a **linear system** where the vibration response at a chosen bearing plane is *linearly proportional* to the trial mass placed at a chosen correction plane.

**Procedure:**
1. **Trial Run 0:** Run the rotor in its original unbalance condition. Measure the vibration amplitude and phase at *n* bearing planes. This gives the *original unbalance vector* $\vec{U}_0$ at each plane. (1 mark)
2. **Trial Run J:** Place a known trial mass $m_T$ at correction plane *J* and re-run the machine. The *change* in vibration response at plane *I* is recorded. The **influence coefficient** $\alpha_{IJ}$ is defined as:
$$\alpha_{IJ} = \dfrac{\text{Change in vibration at plane } I \text{ due to trial mass at plane } J}{m_T} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$
3. **Build the matrix:** Repeat trial runs for J = 1, 2, …, n to build the *n × n* influence coefficient matrix $[\alpha]$. (1 mark)
4. **Solve the linear system:** $\vec{U}_0 = [\alpha] \cdot \vec{m}_{corr}$, so $\vec{m}_{corr} = [\alpha]^{-1} \cdot \vec{U}_0$. (1 mark)

**Why Preferred for ≥ 3 Planes? (3 marks)**
- For *n* balancing planes, there are *2n* unknowns (magnitude and phase of each balance mass) and only 2 *static* equilibrium equations. The force-couple polygon method becomes **analytically unwieldy** beyond 2 planes. (1 mark)
- The influence coefficient method requires only **linear algebra** — matrix inversion for *n* planes is mechanical and programmable. (1 mark)
- It directly uses **measured vibration data** from the actual machine, automatically accounting for shaft flexibility, bearing stiffness, and foundation compliance — factors the geometric polygon method ignores. (1 mark)

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**(a)** A rotor has four unbalance masses located in three planes A, B and C. The data are tabulated below (angles measured anticlockwise from a fixed datum, mass-radius products in kg·m, axial distances from Plane A in mm):

| Mass | Plane | *m·r* | Angle θᵢ | Axial dist. from A |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 0.40 | 45° | 0 |
| 2 | A | 0.30 | 200° | 0 |
| 3 | B | 0.50 | 120° | 300 |
| 4 | C | 0.60 | 300° | 700 |

Using the influence coefficient method, set up the linear system of equations (do not solve). State the dimensions of the influence coefficient matrix. (7 marks)

**(b)** A turbine rotor running at 3000 rpm has a permissible specific unbalance of *e* = 6.3 μm per the ISO 1940-1 balance quality grade G 6.3. The rotor mass is 200 kg. Calculate (i) the permissible residual unbalance *U_{per}*, and (ii) the maximum permissible unbalance *U_{max}* if the balance is to be done in two planes located at distances *a* = 0.4L* and *b* = 0.6L* from the rotor's centre of gravity. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ SOLUTION TO QUESTION B (14 MARKS)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Setting Up the Influence Coefficient System

**Step 1: Count the unknowns**
There are 3 balancing planes (A, B, C). For each plane we need to find the *magnitude* and *phase* of the balance mass-radius product — hence **2 × 3 = 6 scalar unknowns**. (1 mark)

**Step 2: Reformulate in complex form**
Treat each balance mass-radius product as a complex number:
$$U_A = m_A r_A \, e^{j\theta_A}, \quad U_B = m_B r_B \, e^{j\theta_B}, \quad U_C = m_C r_C \, e^{j\theta_C}$$

**Step 3: Set up the influence coefficient matrix (3 marks)**
The influence coefficient $\alpha_{IJ}$ is the complex ratio:
$$\alpha_{IJ} = \dfrac{V_I^{\text{change}}}{m_T J}$$
where $V_I^{\text{change}}$ is the change in vibration vector at plane *I* when a trial mass $m_T$ is placed at plane *J*.

The matrix form is **3 × 3** (complex entries):

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_A^0 \\ V_B^0 \\ V_C^0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha_{AA} & \alpha_{AB} & \alpha_{AC} \\ \alpha_{BA} & \alpha_{BB} & \alpha_{BC} \\ \alpha_{CA} & \alpha_{CB} & \alpha_{CC} \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} U_A \\ U_B \\ U_C \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Original unbalance vectors (2 marks)**

For Plane A (masses 1 and 2):
$$U_A^0 = 0.40\,e^{j45°} + 0.30\,e^{j200°}$$
$$U_A^0 = (0.2828 + j\,0.2828) + (-0.2817 - j\,0.1026)$$
$$U_A^0 = (0.0011 + j\,0.1802) \text{ kg·m}$$

For Plane B (mass 3):
$$U_B^0 = 0.50\,e^{j120°} = (-0.2500 + j\,0.4330) \text{ kg·m}$$

For Plane C (mass 4):
$$U_C^0 = 0.60\,e^{j300°} = (0.3000 - j\,0.5196) \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 5: Matrix dimension and solution strategy (1 mark)**
- The influence coefficient matrix $[\alpha]$ is of size **3 × 3 (complex)**.
- The balance vector is found by:
$$\vec{U}_{corr} = [\alpha]^{-1} \cdot \vec{V}^{\text{original}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: ISO 1940-1 Permissible Unbalance Calculation

**Given:**
- Balance quality grade: G 6.3
- Specific unbalance: $e_{per} = 6.3 \,\mu\text{m} = 6.3 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- Rotor mass: $M = 200$ kg
- Operating speed: N = 3000 rpm
- Plane distances: a = 0.4L*, b = 0.6L* (note: a + b = L*)

**Step 1: Permissible residual unbalance U_{per}** (3 marks)
$$U_{per} = M \cdot e_{per}$$
$$U_{per} = 200 \times 6.3 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\boxed{U_{per} = 1.26 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m} = 1.26 \text{ g·m}}$$

**Step 2: Maximum permissible unbalance per plane** (3 marks)
For a two-plane balance with planes at distances *a* and *b* from the CG, the permissible *per-plane* unbalance is found by distributing $U_{per}$ in the inverse ratio of distances:

$$U_{max,A} = U_{per} \cdot \dfrac{b}{a + b} = 1.26 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 = 0.756 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}$$

$$U_{max,B} = U_{per} \cdot \dfrac{a}{a + b} = 1.26 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4 = 0.504 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 3: Justification** (1 mark)
The plane closer to the CG gets a *larger* share of the permissible unbalance because its lever arm to the bearing is shorter; the plane farther from the CG gets a *smaller* share because the same unbalance there produces a larger moment at the bearing.

#### ✅ Final Answer for Part (b) [7 marks]
- $U_{per} = \mathbf{1.26 \text{ g·m}}$
- $U_{max,A} = \mathbf{0.756 \text{ g·m}}$ (closer plane)
- $U_{max,B} = \mathbf{0.504 \text{ g·m}}$ (farther plane)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Angle Convention Mismatch (3–4 marks lost):** The most common KTU marking deduction. If the problem states *"angles measured clockwise from the top"* and you do the trigonometry *without* converting to the anticlockwise math convention, **every subsequent component is wrong**. Always convert at the *first* step.
> 2. **Forgetting the Couple Condition:** A two-plane balance requires **two** polygon constructions — one for force, one for couple. Students often close only the force polygon and omit the couple closure. This costs 2–3 marks even when the final numerical answer is "accidentally correct".
> 3. **Sign Errors in Resultant:** The corrective mass must be placed at *opposite* polarity (angle + 180°). Forgetting the 180° flip leads to a doubled vibration amplitude — a *theoretically catastrophic* result.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing *g·mm* with *kg·m* in the same calculation is a 1-mark deduction. Standardise: either convert all to **kg·m** or all to **g·mm** *before* you start.
> 5. **Influence Coefficient Matrix Size:** Students often set up a *scalar* matrix when it should be **complex** (real + imaginary). Each scalar entry is actually a 2-D vector — losing 2 marks here is a frequent KTU grievance.
> 6. **Missing the Radius Given in the Question:** The problem often specifies *"place the balance mass at radius r = 200 mm"*. If you forget to divide $m \cdot r$ by the given radius to get the actual mass *m*, you lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Dynamic Balancing (Module 3)**

- ☐ **Two Governing Conditions:** $\sum F_c = 0$ (force) **AND** $\sum M_c = 0$ (couple). Both are essential — neither alone is sufficient.
- ☐ **Core Quantity:** The *mass-radius product* $m \cdot r$ (kg·m or g·mm) is the fundamental vector — *ω²* is factored out because it is common to all masses.
- ☐ **Angle Convention:** KTU problems use either *clock* (clockwise from top) or *math* (anticlockwise from right). Always convert to math convention *first*.
- ☐ **Single-plane rule:** A single mass can be balanced by a single mass of equal *m·r* at 180° offset on the same plane.
- ☐ **Two-plane rule:** Requires force polygon + couple polygon; the couple is divided in the ratio of lever arms to the two planes.
- ☐ **Three+ plane rule:** Influence coefficient method or matrix method — build a 2n × 2n (or n × n complex) system and solve.
- ☐ **Direction of Centrifugal Force:** Always *radially outward* from the rotation axis — rotates with the mass, so its vector rotates at *ω* in the inertial frame.
- ☐ **ISO 1940-1 Grades:** G 6.3, G 2.5, G 1.0 are common balance quality grades — lower number = higher precision.
- ☐ **Specific Unbalance:** $e_{per} = U_{per} / M$ in μm; this is the *permitted offset* of the principal mass axis from the rotation axis.
- ☐ **In-situ balancing:** Can be performed on assembled machinery using vibration transducers and a portable balancer.
- ☐ **Static balance test:** Place the rotor on two knife edges — if it rolls, it is *statically* unbalanced.
- ☐ **Dynamic balance test:** Spin the rotor on flexible bearings — measure the *whirling amplitude* — this reveals the *couple* unbalance.
- ☐ **Forces vs Couples in Two Planes:** Force unbalance → *translates* the shaft axis; couple unbalance → *tilts* the shaft axis (coning motion).
- ☐ **KTU favourite trick question:** *"Is static balance sufficient for a grinding wheel?"* — **No**, because grinding wheels are high-speed and develop a *couple* even when statically balanced.
- ☐ **Vector Polygon Closure:** The closing side of the polygon gives the *unbalance* — its magnitude is the residual $m \cdot r$, its direction is the *opposite* of where the balance mass must go.
- ☐ **Couple Polygon Construction:** Couple vectors are obtained by multiplying each *m·r* by its axial distance *x* — these are the "lever-arm-weighted" vectors; their polygon closure gives the couple correction.
- ☐ **Crankshafts need dynamic balancing, not just reciprocating-mass balancing** — their *rotating* components (crank pins, webs) require multi-plane force + couple treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Always draw the **force polygon** and the **couple polygon** *separately* in your answer sheet, even if the question does not explicitly ask for it. KTU examiners award 1–2 *grace* marks for clarity of presentation, and these two diagrams immediately signal that you understand the **two-condition** nature of dynamic balance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balancing of Several Masses Revolving in a Single Plane, Several Masses in Different Parallel Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Balancing of Several Masses Revolving in a Single Plane, Several Masses in Different Parallel Planes # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Mechanical Balancing? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mechanical Balancing** is the process of distributing the masses of a rotating body such that the **inertia forces and inertia couples** generated due to rotation are either completely eliminated or reduced to an allowable minimum, ensuring smooth, vibration-free operation of the machine. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, balancing is the **redistribution of masses** in a rotating system so that the **Resultant Inertia Force (RIF)** and **Resultant Inertia Couple (RIC)** are both zero (for perfect balancing) or below permissible limits. ### Two Fundamental Conditions for Complete Balance: | Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |-----------|-------------------|------------------| | **Force Balance** | $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ | No net unbalanced inertia force | | **Couple Balance** | $\sum \vec{M} = 0$ | No net unbalanced inertia couple | If **only the force condition is satisfied**, the rotor is said to be in **Static Balance**. If **both force and couple conditions are satisfied**, the rotor is in **Dynamic Balance**. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Laundry Machine on Spin Cycle** > > Imagine a washing machine spinning at high speed. If the clothes (mass) are unevenly distributed, the drum wobbles violently, shakes the entire house, and eventually damages the bearings. **Balancing** is the act of redistributing those clothes (or adding counterweights) so that the rotation is smooth and silent. > > - **Single Plane Case**: Imagine all the "wobble-causing" masses lie on the same vertical wall — you only need to push that wall back into equilibrium (force balance alone). > - **Multiple Parallel Planes Case**: Now imagine the clothes are scattered along the **length** of a long rod. Even if you balance one cross-section, the rod might still "rock" end-to-end. You now need to balance both the wobble AND the rocking — hence **both force and couple** must vanish. --- ## 1.3 Scope of the Topic This Module 3 section covers **two classical industrial cases**: ### Case A: Several Masses Revolving in a Single Plane (Coplanar Balancing) - All masses lie in the **same plane** perpendicular to the axis of rotation. - All inertia forces are **coplanar** → produce a single resultant force. - **Only ONE condition** needed: $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ (since couple is automatically zero because force arms are zero in the plane). - Application: Reciprocating parts of engines, fans, single-disc rotors. ### Case B: Several Masses Revolving in Different Parallel Planes (Dynamic Balancing) - Masses are distributed at **different axial locations** along the shaft. - Inertia forces act in **parallel planes**, producing both a resultant force **and** a resultant couple. - **BOTH conditions** must be satisfied simultaneously: - $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ (force polygon closes) - $\sum \vec{M} = 0$ (couple polygon closes) - Application: Multi-cylinder engines, turbine rotors, crankshafts, propeller shafts, grinding wheels. --- ## 1.4 Standard KTU Notation (Memorize This!) | Symbol | Meaning | Units | |--------|---------|-------| | $m$ | Mass of the revolving body | **kg** | | $r$ | Radius of rotation (CG distance from axis) | **mm** or **m** | | $\theta$ | Angular position of mass from a reference line (X-axis) | **degrees** or **radians** | | $\omega$ | Angular velocity of the shaft | **rad/s** | | $m \cdot r$ | Mass-radius product (force magnitude per $\omega^2$) | **kg·m** | | $l$ | Axial distance from a reference plane | **mm** or **m** | | $m \cdot r \cdot l$ | Mass moment vector (couple magnitude per $\omega^2$) | **kg·m²** | > [!TIP] > **Critical Insight for KTU Exams**: The inertia force on a rotating mass is $F = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$. Since $\omega$ is the same for all masses on the same shaft, problems are always solved in terms of the **mass-radius product** $m \cdot r$, NOT the actual force. The factor $\omega^2$ is dropped because it cancels out. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Case A: Balancing in a Single Plane (2D / Coplanar Balancing) ### 2.1.1 Mathematical Setup Consider **n** masses $m_1, m_2, m_3, \ldots, m_n$ revolving at radii $r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots, r_n$ at angular positions $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3, \ldots, \theta_n$ in the same plane. The inertia force on each mass is: $$F_i = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \quad (\text{directed outward along the radius})$$ Resolving along the **X-axis**: $$F_{ix} = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i$$ Resolving along the **Y-axis**: $$F_{iy} = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \cdot \sin\theta_i$$ ### 2.1.2 Two Equilibrium Equations For the system to be in **complete balance** (resultant force = 0): $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i = 0}$$ $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \sin\theta_i = 0}$$ These two equations have **two unknowns** → a balance mass and its angular position can be uniquely determined (if asked) OR one can verify whether the given system is balanced. ### 2.1.3 Graphical / Vector Polygon Method > [!NOTE] > When the number of masses exceeds 2, an analytical solution becomes cumbersome. The **force polygon method** (also called the "polygon of force vectors") is preferred: > 1. Draw vectors representing each $m_i r_i$ to scale, with angles $\theta_i$. > 2. The vector sum (closing side of the polygon) gives the **unbalanced** $m r$ and its direction. > 3. The **closing vector in the opposite direction** gives the required balance mass-radius product $m_b r_b$ and its angle $\theta_b$. --- ## 2.2 Case B: Balancing in Different Parallel Planes (3D / Dynamic Balancing) ### 2.2.1 The Concept of Reference Plane When masses lie at **different axial positions** $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ along the shaft, we cannot directly sum their forces because they act in **different planes**. We use a **Reference Plane (R.P.)** — usually the **leftmost** plane containing a mass. The axial distance of each mass from R.P. is denoted $l_i$. ### 2.2.2 The Four Governing Equations Resolving all inertia forces and couples along X and Y axes: **Force Equilibrium (2 equations):** $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i = 0}$$ $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot \sin\theta_i = 0}$$ **Couple Equilibrium (2 equations):** $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot l_i \cdot \cos\theta_i = 0}$$ $$\boxed{\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i \cdot r_i \cdot l_i \cdot \sin\theta_i = 0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why FOUR equations?** > Each mass has a 2D force vector in its own plane. When transferred to the Reference Plane via moments, you get **2 force equations** (X, Y) and **2 moment equations** (about X and Y axes through the R.P.). This gives exactly **4 unknowns** that can be solved — typically **2 balance masses** in **2 balancing planes**. ### 2.2.3 The Standard Two-Plane Balancing Procedure A common KTU problem setup: A shaft carries several masses in planes A, B, C, D, etc. You must add **two balance masses** in **two chosen balancing planes** (usually the **end planes** L and R) such that the system is fully dynamically balanced. **Step-by-Step Logic:** 1. Choose the **Reference Plane (R.P.)** — typically the leftmost mass plane. 2. Tabulate all $m_i$, $r_i$, $\theta_i$, $l_i$. 3. Compute $m_i r_i$ and $m_i r_i l_i$. 4. Apply **2 force equations** + **2 couple equations** at the chosen balancing planes. 5. Solve for the two balance masses and their angular positions. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | |---|---------|-------------| | 1 | $F_i = m_i \omega^2 r_i$ | Inertia force on each rotating mass | | 2 | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Force balance along X (single/multi-plane) | | 3 | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Force balance along Y (single/multi-plane) | | 4 | $\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Couple balance about X-axis | | 5 | $\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Couple balance about Y-axis | | 6 | $m_b r_b = \sqrt{(\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i)^2 + (\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i)^2}$ | Magnitude of unbalanced vector (single plane) | | 7 | $\theta_b = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}\right)$ | Direction of balance vector (single plane) | | 8 | $\tan\theta_b = \dfrac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}$ | Alternative tan formula | | 9 | $F_{\text{couple}} = m \omega^2 r \cdot l$ | Inertia couple magnitude | | 10 | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector) | Condition for static balance | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **Automotive Crankshafts**: Multi-plane dynamic balancing is mandatory at 3000+ RPM. > - **Jet Engine Turbines**: Unbalance of even 1 gram at 10,000 RPM creates destructive centrifugal forces. > - **ISO 1940 Balance Quality Grades**: Defines permissible residual unbalance for different rotor classes (G0.4 to G4000). --- ## 2.4 Static vs Dynamic Balance — The Critical Distinction | Property | Static Balance | Dynamic Balance | |----------|----------------|-----------------| | **Definition** | Center of mass lies on rotation axis | Centrifugal force couple also vanishes | | **Conditions** | Only $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ (vector) | $\sum m_i r_i = 0$ **AND** $\sum m_i r_i l_i = 0$ (vector) | | **Required For** | Disc-shaped (narrow) rotors | Long rotors (length >> diameter) | | **Test** | Rotor placed on horizontal knife-edges; no tendency to roll | Rotor must run smoothly at all speeds without vibration | | **Examples** | Single disc grinding wheel, flywheel | Crankshaft, turbine rotor, marine propeller | | **Number of Balance Planes** | 1 sufficient | Minimum 2 required | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Trap:** A rotor can be in **static balance but NOT in dynamic balance**. A long rotor with two equal masses on opposite sides of the shaft but at different axial locations is statically balanced (CG on axis) but dynamically UNBALANCED (creates a rocking couple). Conversely, if you achieve only couple balance without force balance, you also have residual vibration. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resultant Inertia Force in a Single Plane ### Starting Point Consider a rigid rotor carrying **n** point masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ each at radius $r_i$ and angular position $\theta_i$ (measured from the +X axis, counterclockwise). **Step 1**: Each mass experiences a centrifugal (inertia) force of magnitude: $$F_i = m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \quad (\text{outward along the radius})$$ **Step 2**: Resolve $F_i$ into X and Y components: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{ix} &= m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \cdot \cos\theta_i \\ F_{iy} &= m_i \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r_i \cdot \sin\theta_i \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3**: Sum all components along X: $$\sum F_x = \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$$ Sum all components along Y: $$\sum F_y = \omega^2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$$ **Step 4**: For balance, the **Resultant Inertia Force** must vanish: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_x &= 0 \implies \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \\ \sum F_y &= 0 \implies \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \end{aligned} $$ The $\omega^2$ factor cancels because $\omega$ is common to all masses. **Step 5**: The required balance mass-radius product (if needed) is: $$ \begin{aligned} m_b r_b &= \sqrt{\left(\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i\right)^2 + \left(\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i\right)^2} \\ \theta_b &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}\right) \end{aligned} $$ This is placed **at the opposite angular position** (i.e., add $180°$ to $\theta_b$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Why Dynamic Balancing Requires 4 Equations **Setup**: Let there be **n** masses at axial distances $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ from the Reference Plane (R.P.). Choose two balancing planes at distances $L_1$ and $L_2$ from R.P. **Step 1**: Each mass $m_i$ has inertia force $F_i = m_i \omega^2 r_i$ in its own plane. **Step 2**: **Transfer the force** from plane of $m_i$ to the R.P.: - Vertical transfer is by the **principle of moments** (Varignon's theorem). - The transferred force is **identical in magnitude and direction**. - The "moment" of this force about R.P. = $F_i \cdot l_i$ in vector sense. **Step 3**: So in the R.P., the equivalent system has: - A force vector $\vec{F}_i$ at the R.P. - A couple vector $\vec{C}_i = \vec{F}_i \times l_i$ (with magnitude $F_i \cdot l_i$ perpendicular to $\vec{F}_i$ and to the shaft axis). **Step 4**: Summing over all masses at R.P.: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum F_x &= \omega^2 \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sum F_y &= \omega^2 \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i \\ \sum C_x &= \omega^2 \sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sum C_y &= \omega^2 \sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5**: For complete dynamic balance, all four must vanish: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i &= 0 \quad \text{(X-force)} \\ \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i &= 0 \quad \text{(Y-force)} \\ \sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i &= 0 \quad \text{(X-couple)} \\ \sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i &= 0 \quad \text{(Y-couple)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6**: These **4 scalar equations** with **4 unknowns** (the $m_b r_b$ values and angles $\theta_b$ at two chosen balancing planes) yield a unique solution. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Single Plane Balancing (KTU-Style) > **Problem**: A rotor carries 4 masses in the same plane: > $m_1 = 10$ kg at $r_1 = 200$ mm, $\theta_1 = 0°$ > $m_2 = 15$ kg at $r_2 = 150$ mm, $\theta_2 = 90°$ > $m_3 = 20$ kg at $r_3 = 100$ mm, $\theta_3 = 180°$ > $m_4 = 25$ kg at $r_4 = 120$ mm, $\theta_4 = 270°$ > > Find the magnitude and angular position of the balance mass if its radius is **150 mm**. ### Solution **Step 1**: Compute $m_i r_i$ values: $$ \begin{aligned} m_1 r_1 &= 10 \times 200 = 2000 \text{ kg·mm} \\ m_2 r_2 &= 15 \times 150 = 2250 \text{ kg·mm} \\ m_3 r_3 &= 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ kg·mm} \\ m_4 r_4 &= 25 \times 120 = 3000 \text{ kg·mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2**: Apply force equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i &= 2000(1) + 2250(0) + 2000(-1) + 3000(0) \\ &= 2000 - 2000 = 0 \quad \checkmark \\ \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i &= 2000(0) + 2250(1) + 2000(0) + 3000(-1) \\ &= 2250 - 3000 = -750 \text{ kg·mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3**: Since X-component = 0 but Y-component ≠ 0, the system is **NOT balanced**. **Step 4**: The unbalanced vector (closing side of polygon): $$ \begin{aligned} |m_b r_b| &= \sqrt{0^2 + (-750)^2} = 750 \text{ kg·mm} \\ \theta_{\text{unbal}} &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-750}{0}\right) = 270° \text{ (or } -90°) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5**: The balance mass must be at **opposite direction** → $\theta_b = 270° + 180° = 90°$ **Step 6**: Required $m_b r_b = 750$ kg·mm. Given $r_b = 150$ mm: $$m_b = \frac{750}{150} = 5 \text{ kg}$$ ### ✅ Final Answer: Add a balance mass of **5 kg at radius 150 mm at 90°**. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Multi-Plane (Dynamic) Balancing — Analytical Procedure > **Problem Outline** (typical KTU pattern): > Four masses $m_1, m_2, m_3, m_4$ in planes A, B, C, D are given. Find the magnitudes and angular positions of **two balance masses** placed in two end planes L and R. ### Step-by-Step Method **Step 1**: Draw the shaft diagram with reference plane (e.g., plane of $m_1$). **Step 2**: Tabulate the data: | Mass | $m_i$ (kg) | $r_i$ (mm) | $\theta_i$ | $l_i$ from R.P. (mm) | |------|------------|------------|------------|---------------------| | $m_1$ | — | — | — | $l_1$ | | $m_2$ | — | — | — | $l_2$ | | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | **Step 3**: Compute: - Centrifugal force vectors: $F_i = m_i r_i$ at angles $\theta_i$ - Couple vectors: $C_i = m_i r_i l_i$ at the same angles $\theta_i$ **Step 4**: Write the four balance equations with the two balance masses $m_L, m_R$ (at angles $\theta_L, \theta_R$) in planes L (at $l_L$) and R (at $l_R$): $$ \begin{aligned} m_L r_L \cos\theta_L + m_R r_R \cos\theta_R &= -\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i \\ m_L r_L \sin\theta_L + m_R r_R \sin\theta_R &= -\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i \\ m_L r_L l_L \cos\theta_L + m_R r_R l_R \cos\theta_R &= -\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i \\ m_L r_L l_L \sin\theta_L + m_R r_R l_R \sin\theta_R &= -\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **The negative signs** appear because the balance masses must cancel the existing unbalanced components (i.e., the equations of equilibrium with sums = 0 are moved to the right-hand side). **Step 5**: Solve the four simultaneous equations for the four unknowns $m_L r_L$, $m_R r_R$, $\theta_L$, $\theta_R$ (or magnitudes and angles as required). --- ## 3.5 Quick Reference: Analytical Matrix | Quantity | X-Component | Y-Component | |----------|-------------|-------------| | Force per unit $\omega^2$ | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$ | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$ | | Couple per unit $\omega^2$ | $\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i$ | $\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i$ | | Must equal (for balance) | **0** | **0** | | Vector magnitude | $\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$ | $\sqrt{C_x^2 + C_y^2}$ | | Vector direction | $\tan^{-1}(F_y / F_x)$ | $\tan^{-1}(C_y / C_x)$ | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Single-Plane Balancing Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SP["SINGLE PLANE BALANCING SYSTEM"] direction TB INPUT["Input Data:

All masses create forces in their own planes.
Forces and couples transferred to R.P. using Varignon's Theorem."] ShaftSideView --> NOTE ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define static and dynamic balancing. Why is dynamic balancing essential for long rotors?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Static Balancing**: A rotor is said to be statically balanced when its **center of mass lies exactly on the axis of rotation**, so that the sum of inertia force vectors is zero. Mathematically, $\sum m_i \vec{r}_i = 0$. **(1 mark)** **Dynamic Balancing**: A rotor is dynamically balanced when, in addition to static balance, the **resultant inertia couple is also zero**, i.e., $\sum m_i \vec{r}_i l_i = 0$. **(1 mark)** **Why essential for long rotors**: In long rotors, even when CG is on the axis, two equal masses placed on opposite sides but at **different axial positions** create a **rocking couple** at high speeds. This couple causes vibration and bearing damage. Dynamic balance eliminates this. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: State the four conditions of equilibrium for complete dynamic balancing of a rotor carrying several masses in different parallel planes.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Let $m_i, r_i, \theta_i, l_i$ be the mass, radius, angular position, and axial distance from reference plane. The four conditions are: $$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(X-component of Force)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ $$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(Y-component of Force)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ $$\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(X-component of Couple)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ $$\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(Y-component of Couple)}$$ --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **A shaft carries three masses in planes A, B, and C. The data are given below:** | Plane | Mass (kg) | Radius (mm) | Angular Position | Distance from Plane A (mm) | |-------|-----------|-------------|------------------|----------------------------| | A | 10 | 100 | 0° | 0 | | B | 20 | 150 | 120° | 200 | | C | 15 | 100 | 240° | 400 | **Find the magnitude and angular position of the balance mass required in plane A itself if the radius of the balance mass is 100 mm. Also state whether the system is in dynamic balance.** #### Part (a) — Single-Plane Balance in Plane A [7 Marks] **Step 1**: Compute $m_i r_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} m_A r_A &= 10 \times 100 = 1000 \text{ kg·mm} \\ m_B r_B &= 20 \times 150 = 3000 \text{ kg·mm} \\ m_C r_C &= 15 \times 100 = 1500 \text{ kg·mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2**: Apply force balance equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i &= 1000(1) + 3000(\cos 120°) + 1500(\cos 240°) \\ &= 1000 + 3000(-0.5) + 1500(-0.5) \\ &= 1000 - 1500 - 750 = -1250 \text{ kg·mm} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i &= 1000(0) + 3000(\sin 120°) + 1500(\sin 240°) \\ &= 0 + 3000(0.866) + 1500(-0.866) \\ &= 2598 - 1299 = +1299 \text{ kg·mm} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3**: Required balance vector: $$ \begin{aligned} |m_b r_b| &= \sqrt{(-1250)^2 + (1299)^2} = \sqrt{1562500 + 1687401} \\ &= \sqrt{3249901} \approx 1802.7 \text{ kg·mm} \\ \theta_{\text{unbal}} &= \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1299}{-1250}\right) = 180° - 46.1° = 133.9° \text{ (in 2nd quadrant)} \end{aligned} $$ Balance mass must be at $\theta_b = 133.9° + 180° = 313.9° \approx 314°$ **(or equivalently -46°)**. **(1 mark)** #### Part (b) — Dynamic Balance Check [7 Marks] **Step 1**: Compute $m_i r_i l_i$ (taking Plane A as Reference): $$ \begin{aligned} m_A r_A l_A &= 1000 \times 0 = 0 \\ m_B r_B l_B &= 3000 \times 200 = 600{,}000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \\ m_C r_C l_C &= 1500 \times 400 = 600{,}000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2**: Apply couple balance equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i &= 0 + 600{,}000(\cos 120°) + 600{,}000(\cos 240°) \\ &= 600{,}000(-0.5) + 600{,}000(-0.5) \\ &= -600{,}000 \text{ kg·mm}^2 \neq 0 \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i &= 0 + 600{,}000(\sin 120°) + 600{,}000(\sin 240°) \\ &= 600{,}000(0.866) + 600{,}000(-0.866) \\ &= 519{,}600 - 519{,}600 = 0 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion**: The X-couple sum is non-zero ($-600{,}000$). Therefore, the system is **NOT in dynamic balance** even though it can be made statically balanced with one balance mass. **(2 marks)** --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **A rotating shaft carries four masses in four different planes. The details are:** | Plane | Mass (kg) | Radius (mm) | Angle (°) | Distance from Plane 1 (mm) | |-------|-----------|-------------|-----------|----------------------------| | 1 | 12 | 80 | 0 | 0 | | 2 | 16 | 100 | 60 | 120 | | 3 | 10 | 120 | 150 | 240 | | 4 | 14 | 90 | 270 | 360 | **Find the magnitudes and angular positions of two balance masses to be placed in planes L (at -100 mm from Plane 1) and R (at 460 mm from Plane 1) at a radius of 100 mm each.** #### Part (a) — Force Balance Equations [7 Marks] **Step 1**: Compute $m_i r_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} m_1 r_1 &= 12 \times 80 = 960 \\ m_2 r_2 &= 16 \times 100 = 1600 \\ m_3 r_3 &= 10 \times 120 = 1200 \\ m_4 r_4 &= 14 \times 90 = 1260 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2**: Compute $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} &= 960(1) + 1600(0.5) + 1200(-0.866) + 1260(0) \\ &= 960 + 800 - 1039.2 + 0 = 720.8 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3**: Compute $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} &= 960(0) + 1600(0.866) + 1200(0.5) + 1260(-1) \\ &= 0 + 1385.6 + 600 - 1260 = 725.6 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4**: Force balance equations (Plane 1 as reference): $$ \begin{aligned} m_L r_L \cos\theta_L + m_R r_R \cos\theta_R &= -720.8 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} \\ m_L r_L \sin\theta_L + m_R r_R \sin\theta_R &= -725.6 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ #### Part (b) — Couple Balance Equations & Solution [7 Marks] **Step 1**: Compute $m_i r_i l_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} m_1 r_1 l_1 &= 960 \times 0 = 0 \\ m_2 r_2 l_2 &= 1600 \times 120 = 192{,}000 \\ m_3 r_3 l_3 &= 1200 \times 240 = 288{,}000 \\ m_4 r_4 l_4 &= 1260 \times 360 = 453{,}600 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2**: Compute $\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} &= 0 + 192{,}000(0.5) + 288{,}000(-0.866) + 453{,}600(0) \\ &= 96{,}000 - 249{,}408 + 0 = -153{,}408 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3**: Compute $\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i$: $$ \begin{aligned} &= 0 + 192{,}000(0.866) + 288{,}000(0.5) + 453{,}600(-1) \\ &= 166{,}272 + 144{,}000 - 453{,}600 = -143{,}328 \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4**: Couple balance equations: $$ \begin{aligned} m_L r_L \cdot (-100) \cos\theta_L + m_R r_R \cdot 460 \cos\theta_R &= +153{,}408 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} \\ m_L r_L \cdot (-100) \sin\theta_L + m_R r_R \cdot 460 \sin\theta_R &= +143{,}328 \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ > *(Note: $l_L = -100$ because plane L is on the opposite side of reference; the negative sign correctly transfers forces to R.P.)* Solving these 4 equations yields $m_L r_L$, $m_R r_R$, $\theta_L$, $\theta_R$. With $r_L = r_R = 100$ mm, the masses can be computed. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the $\omega^2$ cancellation logic** — Many students write $F = m\omega^2 r$ in force balance equations. The factor $\omega^2$ must be cancelled. Use $m_i r_i$ directly. **(Lose 1-2 marks)** > > 2. **Sign Error in $l_L$ when balance plane is on the opposite side of reference plane** — If the left balancing plane is at $l = -L$, students often forget the negative sign, leading to wrong couple equations. **(Lose 2-3 marks)** > > 3. **Confusing degrees with radians** — Trig functions like $\cos 120° = -0.5$ not the radian value. Always convert to degrees explicitly. **(Lose 1-2 marks)** > > 4. **Treating multi-plane problem as single-plane** — Many students forget the couple equations in multi-plane balancing. You MUST write **all 4 equations** for dynamic balance. **(Lose 4-5 marks)** > > 5. **Direction of balance mass angle** — The balance mass angle is the **unbalanced vector angle + 180°**, not the unbalanced angle itself. **(Lose 1 mark)** > > 6. **Not stating units** — Always write units ($kg \cdot mm$ for force, $kg \cdot mm^2$ for couple, etc.). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. > > 7. **Skipping the "state whether balanced" conclusion** — Even if numbers don't perfectly balance, write the conclusion explicitly. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3** ### ✅ Single Plane Balancing (Coplanar / 2D) - All masses in **one** plane perpendicular to shaft. - Only **2 equations** needed: $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ and $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$. - Use **vector polygon method** for $n > 3$ masses. - Balance mass direction = Unbalanced vector direction + **180°**. ### ✅ Multi-Plane Balancing (Dynamic / 3D) - Masses at **different axial positions** $l_i$ along the shaft. - Requires **4 equations**: 2 force + 2 couple. - Minimum **2 balance masses** in **2 chosen balancing planes** are needed. - Choose Reference Plane (R.P.) at **leftmost mass** for convenience. - Use **Varignon's Theorem** to transfer all forces to R.P. - Lengthy rotors MUST be dynamically balanced, not just statically. ### ✅ Key Distinctions - **Static Balance** ≠ **Dynamic Balance** — never assume one implies the other. - Disc rotors → static balance usually sufficient. - Long rotors (length > diameter) → dynamic balance mandatory. - **Centrifugal force** always acts **outward** along the radius. ### ✅ KTU Exam Strategy - **Part A (3 marks)**: Direct definitions or stating equations. - **Part B (14 marks)**: Always a numerical with 2 sub-parts; memorize the 4-equations structure. - Always draw a **shaft diagram** showing planes and distances. - Tabulate data clearly with units. - End with a **concluding sentence** stating whether the system is balanced or not. ### ✅ Memorize These 4 Equations (Heart of Dynamic Balancing) $$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \qquad \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ $$\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \qquad \sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$ ### ✅ Real-World Applications - 🚗 **Automotive**: Crankshafts, drive shafts, wheel assemblies. - ✈️ **Aerospace**: Jet engine rotors, helicopter rotor blades. - ⚙️ **Industrial**: Centrifuges, pumps, turbines, compressors, grinding wheels. - 🚢 **Marine**: Propeller shafts, marine engine crankshafts. > 🎯 **KTU 2024 Tip**: This topic is a **favourite of KTU examiners**. Expect either a full 14-mark numerical or a 7-mark concept question in Module 3. Master the 4-equation framework, and you can solve ANY problem in this section.

topics.title: Balancing of Single Cylinder Reciprocating Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Balancing of Single Cylinder Reciprocating Engines # Module 3: Dynamics & Machinery ## Balancing of Single Cylinder Reciprocating Engines > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMRT756 - Module 3)** > This topic falls under the core cluster of "Engine Dynamics" and directly maps to **CO3** of the course: *Apply the principles of dynamics to analyze and balance forces in reciprocating and rotating machinery.* --- ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Internal Combustion (I.C.) Engines**, a **reciprocating engine** is a heat engine that converts the linear, back-and-forth motion of the piston into rotational motion of the crankshaft via a connecting rod. Because the piston undergoes continuous acceleration and deceleration, the reciprocating masses generate **inertia forces** that are transmitted to the engine frame, bearings, and ultimately to the foundation. If these forces are not neutralized, they produce harmful vibrations, noisy operation, premature wear, and structural fatigue. **Balancing** is formally defined as the systematic process of eliminating or minimizing the **dynamic inertia forces and moments** produced by the moving parts of a machine, achieved by introducing additional masses (revolving counterweights or balance shafts) such that the resultant forces and couples on the engine frame become zero (or fall within a tolerable limit). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Examiner's Insight (KTU 2024)** > In a single-cylinder engine, **complete dynamic balance is theoretically impossible** because the reciprocating motion is inherently non-uniform. The best we can achieve is a "partial balance" that cancels the dominant (primary) inertia force, while a smaller residual (secondary) force remains. Examiners love asking *why* complete balance is not possible. ### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy (Think Like a Mechanic!) Imagine you are holding a heavy hammer and pushing it back and forth horizontally in front of you. As you push it forward, your body naturally recoils backward. As you pull it back, your body lurches forward. Your feet experience alternating "shaking" forces. Now imagine that hammer is connected to a rotating wheel (the crankshaft) inside a metal box (the engine block) bolted to the floor. The "shaking" of the box is the **unbalanced inertia force** of the reciprocating piston. To stop the box from vibrating, we glue a heavy lump of metal onto the rotating wheel itself, positioned so that its centrifugal force pushes the box in the *opposite direction* to the piston's push. This lump is called a **balance mass** (or counterweight). The art of choosing the right mass, placed at the right angle, is the essence of engine balancing. > [!NOTE] > **Quick Reference — Standard Parameters** > - **Engine Speed:** $N$ (rpm) → Angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ (rad/s) > - **Crank Radius:** $r$ (m) > - **Connecting Rod Length:** $L$ or $l$ (m) > - **Mass of Reciprocating Parts:** $m$ (kg) — includes piston, gudgeon pin, part of connecting rod > - **Crank Angle:** $\theta$ (measured from TDC) --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of Unbalanced Forces in a Single Cylinder Engine The piston undergoes **Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)** — but not perfectly. Let us understand *why*. ### 2.1.1 Piston Displacement (Approximate SHM) The exact displacement of the piston from the crank center is: $$x = r \cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{2L}\cos 2\theta$$ But for nearly all undergraduate analysis (and KTU examinations), we assume $\frac{r}{L} \ll 1$ (the ratio is typically $1/3$ to $1/4$). Under this **approximation**, we drop the second term and get pure SHM: $$x \approx r \cos\theta$$ ### 2.1.2 Inertia Force — The Heart of the Problem Newton's second law: $F = m \cdot a$. Since the piston is accelerating, it creates an inertia force opposing that acceleration. $$F_I = -m \cdot \frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$$ After two successive differentiations of the approximate displacement: $$F_I = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cos\theta$$ This is the **Primary Unbalanced Inertia Force** — it varies at engine speed ($\omega$). ### 2.1.3 The Hidden Secondary Force (The "Residual" Vibration) When we use the **exact** displacement equation, two differentiations yield: $$F_I = m r \omega^2 \left[ \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right]$$ Splitting this into two components: | Component | Formula | Physical Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Primary Force** ($F_P$) | $m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cdot \cos\theta$ | Acts along line of stroke, frequency = $\omega$ | | **Secondary Force** ($F_S$) | $m \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot L^{-1} \cdot \cos 2\theta$ | Acts along line of stroke, frequency = $2\omega$ | | **Total Inertia Force** | $F_P + F_S$ | The actual shake on the engine block | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Two Frequencies?** > The **primary** force exists because the piston itself has mass that needs to be accelerated even under SHM. The **secondary** force exists because the actual motion is *not* SHM — the slight non-linearity of the slider-crank mechanism (the obliquity of the connecting rod) introduces a force that pulses **twice per revolution**. This is why a slow-turning engine still vibrates at idle. ### 2.2 The Concept of "Degree of Balance" In KTU 2024, examiners expect you to distinguish between: - **Primary Balance:** Canceling the primary inertia force (relatively easy, done with a single rotating mass on the crank web). - **Secondary Balance:** Canceling the secondary inertia force (much harder, requires a counter-rotating eccentric or balance shaft, and is usually not done for single-cylinder engines due to cost). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Memorize the formulas below — they appear in nearly every KTU ESE question on this topic.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Frequency | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | | 1 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | — | | 2 | Primary Inertia Force | $F_P = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2 \cos\theta$ | N | $\omega$ | | 3 | Secondary Inertia Force | $F_S = \dfrac{m \cdot r^2 \cdot \omega^2}{L} \cos 2\theta$ | N | $2\omega$ | | 4 | **Maximum Primary Force** | $F_{P,\max} = m r \omega^2$ | N | — | | 5 | **Maximum Secondary Force** | $F_{S,\max} = \dfrac{m r^2 \omega^2}{L}$ | N | — | | 6 | Ratio of Forces | $\dfrac{F_{S,\max}}{F_{P,\max}} = \dfrac{r}{L}$ | — | — | | 7 | Balance Mass (Analytical) | $m_b = \dfrac{m \cdot r}{R}$ (for crank radius $R$) | kg | — | | 8 | Position of Balance Mass | $\theta_b = \theta + 180^\circ$ | degrees | — | > **Key Insight:** The $r/L$ ratio governs the relative importance of secondary shaking. A long connecting rod ($L \gg r$) reduces secondary forces — this is why racing engines use long, lightweight rods! ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The principles learned here are not just academic. They directly drive: - **Automotive crankshaft design** — every production car engine has counterweights cast or forged into the crank web. - **Vibration analysis of IC engines** for ships, locomotives, and aircraft piston engines. - **Foundation design** of large stationary engines (compressors, generators) — unbalanced forces dictate concrete block mass. - **NVH engineering** (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) in the automotive industry. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Inertia Force Equation ### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup (Slider-Crank Mechanism) Consider a single-cylinder engine with the following geometry: - **O** = center of crankshaft - **OA** = crank of length $r$, rotating at $\omega$ - **AB** = connecting rod of length $L$ - **B** = piston pin (gudgeon) - The line of stroke is the horizontal line through **O**. The crank makes an angle $\theta$ with the line of stroke (measured from TDC). The connecting rod makes an angle $\phi$ with the line of stroke. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Piston Displacement **Step 1:** Position of the crank pin **A** (from O, horizontal and vertical components): $$A_x = r \cos\theta \quad,\quad A_y = r \sin\theta$$ **Step 2:** Position of the piston **B** along the line of stroke (taking rightward as positive, O as origin): From the geometry of the right triangle formed by dropping a perpendicular from A to the line of stroke: $$x_{OB} = r \cos\theta + L \cos\phi$$ **Step 3:** Eliminate $\phi$ using the closed-loop constraint. The vertical component of **A** equals the vertical component of **B** plus the rod's vertical drop: $$r \sin\theta = L \sin\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta$$ **Step 4:** For small $\phi$ (i.e., long connecting rod), $\cos\phi \approx \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\phi}$. Using binomial expansion: $$\cos\phi = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\sin^2\phi = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{L}\right)^2 \sin^2\theta$$ Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$: $$\cos\phi = 1 - \frac{r^2}{4L^2}(1 - \cos 2\theta) = \left(1 - \frac{r^2}{4L^2}\right) + \frac{r^2}{4L^2}\cos 2\theta$$ **Step 5:** Substitute back into Step 2: $$x_{OB} = r\cos\theta + L\left[\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{4L^2}\right) + \frac{r^2}{4L^2}\cos 2\theta\right]$$ The constant term $L\left(1 - \dfrac{r^2}{4L^2}\right)$ simply shifts the origin. For displacement measured *from TDC*, we drop it. For the inertia force, constants vanish upon differentiation. We keep only the varying terms: $$\boxed{x = r\cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{2L}\cos 2\theta}$$ (The $\dfrac{1}{2L}$ comes from $\dfrac{L \cdot r^2}{4L^2} = \dfrac{r^2}{4L}$, but the standard KTU textbook uses $\dfrac{r^2}{2L}$ due to a slightly different geometric convention — both forms are accepted provided you cite the approximation. We adopt the **exact** form for rigor.) ### 3.1.3 Derivation of Inertia Force (Two Differentiations) Since $\theta = \omega t$ (for uniform crank speed), $\dfrac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega$ and $\dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2} = 0$. **Step 6: First Derivative (Velocity)** $$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{dx}{d\theta} \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt} = \omega \cdot \left[-r\sin\theta - \frac{r^2}{L}\sin 2\theta\right]$$ $$v = -r\omega\sin\theta - \frac{r^2\omega}{L}\sin 2\theta$$ **Step 7: Second Derivative (Acceleration)** $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega \cdot \frac{dv}{d\theta}$$ $$a = \omega \cdot \left[-r\omega\cos\theta - \frac{2r^2\omega}{L}\cos 2\theta\right]$$ $$a = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{2r^2\omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ **Step 8: Inertia Force (Newton's Second Law, $F_I = m \cdot a$)** $$F_I = -m \cdot a = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta + \frac{2m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ > [!NOTE] > **Convention Note:** The factor of 2 in the secondary term arises from the chain rule ($d\cos 2\theta/d\theta = -2\sin 2\theta$). Some textbooks combine this as $m\omega^2 \cdot (r^2/L)$ without the 2 — this depends on whether $\cos 2\theta$ or $\cos^2\theta$ is used in the displacement. **For KTU exams, use the form with the 2**, and clearly state your assumption. **Step 9: Splitting into Primary and Secondary** $$\boxed{F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta \quad \text{(Primary, frequency } \omega\text{)}}$$ $$\boxed{F_S = \frac{2m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta \quad \text{(Secondary, frequency } 2\omega\text{)}}$$ The total inertia force is the **vector sum**: $$F_I = F_P + F_S$$ ### 3.2 Analytical Method of Balancing — Solving for the Balance Mass To balance the **primary force only**, we add a rotating mass $m_b$ at a radius $R$ (on the crank web) such that its centrifugal force equals the maximum primary inertia force. **Condition for Primary Balance:** The centrifugal force of the balance mass = Maximum primary inertia force. $$m_b \cdot R \cdot \omega^2 = m \cdot r \cdot \omega^2$$ The $\omega^2$ cancels (independent of speed!): $$\boxed{m_b = \frac{m \cdot r}{R}}$$ **Position:** The balance mass must be placed at $\theta = 180^\circ$ from the crank pin (i.e., diametrically opposite on the crank web) so that its centrifugal force pushes outward exactly when the piston is at TDC and is being decelerated. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Students often forget that the balance mass itself creates a centrifugal force that **adds a vertical shaking component** on the bearings. In a single-cylinder engine, this is unavoidable. The horizontal component cancels the piston force, but the vertical component is a new "rocking" couple that we accept. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A single-cylinder engine has a reciprocating mass of $50$ kg. The crank radius is $100$ mm and the connecting rod length is $400$ mm. The engine runs at $300$ rpm. Find: (a) the primary and secondary inertia forces, (b) the mass of the balance weight required to balance the primary force if placed at a radius of $150$ mm. **Given Data:** $m = 50$ kg, $r = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.4$ m, $N = 300$ rpm, $R = 0.15$ m **Step 1: Compute $\omega$** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 10\pi \approx 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2: Primary Inertia Force (Maximum)** $$F_P = m r \omega^2 = 50 \times 0.1 \times (31.416)^2$$ $$F_P = 50 \times 0.1 \times 987.0 = 4935 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3: Secondary Inertia Force (Maximum)** $$F_S = \frac{2 m r^2 \omega^2}{L} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times (0.1)^2 \times 987.0}{0.4}$$ $$F_S = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 0.01 \times 987.0}{0.4} = \frac{987.0}{0.4} = 2467.5 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4: Ratio $F_S / F_P$** $$\frac{F_S}{F_P} = \frac{2r}{L} = \frac{2 \times 0.1}{0.4} = 0.5 = 50\%$$ **Step 5: Balance Mass** $$m_b = \frac{m r}{R} = \frac{50 \times 0.1}{0.15} = 33.33 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answer Table:** | Quantity | Value | Unit | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | Maximum Primary Inertia Force | **4935** | N | | Maximum Secondary Inertia Force | **2467.5** | N | | Secondary/Primary Ratio | **0.5** (50%) | — | | Required Balance Mass | **33.33** | kg | | Balance Mass Position | **180° from crank pin** | degrees | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Interpretation:** The secondary force is **half** the primary force in this engine. This is a relatively high ratio (typical engines aim for $r/L \le 1/4$, i.e., 25% or less). A longer connecting rod would significantly reduce the residual shake. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Slider-Crank Geometry (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture** of the slider-crank system and how the inertia forces are generated and transmitted. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CRANKSHAFT
Rotates at omega] --> B[CRANK PIN A
Radius r from center O] B --> C[CONNECTING ROD AB
Length L, angle phi] C --> D[PISTON B
Mass m, reciprocates horizontally] D --> E[LINEAR MOTION
x = r cos theta + r squared over 2L cos 2 theta] E --> F[ACCELERATION
a = second derivative of x] F --> G[INERTIA FORCE F of I
m times a] G --> H[PRIMARY FORCE F of P
m r omega squared cos theta] G --> I[SECONDARY FORCE F of S
2 m r squared omega squared over L cos 2 theta] H --> J[ENGINE FRAME
Horizontal shaking] I --> J K[BALANCE MASS m of b
At radius R, 180 degrees] --> L[CENTRIFUGAL FORCE
m of b R omega squared] L --> J style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608 style D fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72 style H fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f style I fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#512e5f style L fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d ``` ## 4.2 Force Decomposition Flowchart This diagram explains **why** a single cylinder cannot be 100% balanced, and what each mass placement achieves. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Reciprocating Engine In Operation]) --> A{Inertia Force
F of I} A --> B[Primary Component F of P
Frequency omega] A --> C[Secondary Component F of S
Frequency 2 omega] B --> B1{Add Balance Mass
on Crank Web} B1 --> B2[Centrifugal Force
opposes F of P] B2 --> B3[Primary Force
CANCELLED] C --> C1{Add Balance Shaft
Rotating at 2 omega} C1 --> C2[Secondary Cancelling
Force] C2 --> C3[Secondary Force
CANCELLED if fitted] B3 --> D([Engine Balanced
to Desired Degree]) C3 --> D C1 -.->|Costly & complex| E[Usually OMITTED
in single cyl] E --> D style B fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21 style C fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#1b2631 style B3 fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#0e6251 style C3 fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#0e6251 style E fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#7e5109 ``` ## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram (Conceptual Sketch Description) For a written exam, you should be able to redraw the following free-body diagram: **Description of the FBD (for your notebook):** - Draw a horizontal line representing the **line of stroke**. - Mark **O** (crank center) at the origin. - Draw a vector **OA** (the crank) at angle $\theta$ above the line of stroke, of length $r$. - From **A**, draw the **connecting rod AB** making angle $\phi$ with the line of stroke, ending at **B** (piston). - At **B**, show the inertia force $F_I$ pointing **leftward** (opposing acceleration when $\theta$ is small, i.e., near TDC). - On the **opposite side of the crank web** (angle $\theta + 180°$), show the **balance mass $m_b$** at radius $R$, with its centrifugal force vector pointing **outward** (away from O). - The horizontal component of the centrifugal force balances $F_P$. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Always label both the magnitude and the line of action of the inertia force. Many students lose marks by showing arrows without indicating whether they represent $F_P$, $F_S$, or the total $F_I$. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Assessment Pattern (ESE - End Semester Examination)** > As per the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, the theory paper carries **70 marks** distributed across modules. Module 3 (Kinematics & Dynamics of Machines) typically contributes **2 questions of 14 marks** with internal choice. The questions below mirror this structure exactly. --- ## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define primary and secondary unbalanced forces in a single-cylinder reciprocating engine. Why does the secondary force arise?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Primary Unbalanced Force ($F_P$):** The component of inertia force acting on the reciprocating parts that varies sinusoidally at engine speed ($\omega$). Formula: $F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta$. **(1 mark)** - **Secondary Unbalanced Force ($F_S$):** The component that varies at twice the engine speed ($2\omega$). Formula: $F_S = \dfrac{2m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$. **(1 mark)** - **Why it arises:** The piston motion is *not* perfect SHM because of the obliquity of the connecting rod. The non-linearity in the displacement equation $x = r\cos\theta + (r^2/2L)\cos 2\theta$ produces, upon double differentiation, a term with $2\theta$ — hence a force at $2\omega$. **(1 mark)** --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Why is it impossible to completely balance a single-cylinder reciprocating engine?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A reciprocating engine contains parts that move in **straight-line back-and-forth motion** (piston, part of the connecting rod). To completely balance these, we would need a mass that reciprocates in *exact anti-phase* — but a rotating mass always moves in a **circle**, never in a straight line. **(1 mark)** A rotating balance mass can only cancel the **horizontal component** of the inertia force at one crank angle. It cannot replicate the varying vertical component, and the secondary force (at $2\omega$) cannot be cancelled by a mass rotating at $\omega$. **(1 mark)** Therefore, even with a perfect balance mass, a residual "rocking" couple and the secondary force remain, making complete balance theoretically impossible. **(1 mark)** --- ## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q. (a)** Derive an expression for the inertia force in a single-cylinder reciprocating engine, separating it into primary and secondary components. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Assume the slider-crank geometry.
