The displacement of the piston from the crank center (approximating to first order) is: $$x = r \cos\theta + \frac{r^2}{2L}\cos 2\theta$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Differentiate twice with respect to time, using $\theta = \omega t$: $$a = \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - \frac{2r^2\omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply Newton's second law. Inertia force $F_I = -m \cdot a$: $$F_I = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta + \frac{2m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify components: $$F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta \quad ; \quad F_S = \frac{2m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Key:** Full marks are awarded only if the student explicitly shows the differentiation steps and does not write the result from memory. --- **Q. (b)** A single-cylinder engine has the following data: Mass of reciprocating parts = $60$ kg Crank radius = $150$ mm Connecting rod length = $450$ mm Engine speed = $240$ rpm Find: (i) Maximum primary and secondary inertia forces, (ii) The mass of the balance weight required at a radius of $200$ mm to balance the primary force. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $\omega$: $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 240}{60} = 8\pi \approx 25.13 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\omega^2 = 631.65 \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Maximum primary inertia force: $$F_{P,\max} = m r \omega^2 = 60 \times 0.15 \times 631.65 = 5684.85 \text{ N} \approx 5.685 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Maximum secondary inertia force: $$F_{S,\max} = \frac{2 m r^2 \omega^2}{L} = \frac{2 \times 60 \times (0.15)^2 \times 631.65}{0.45}$$ $$F_{S,\max} = \frac{2 \times 60 \times 0.0225 \times 631.65}{0.45} = \frac{1705.45}{0.45} \approx 3789.9 \text{ N} \approx 3.79 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Ratio check: $$\frac{F_{S,\max}}{F_{P,\max}} = \frac{2r}{L} = \frac{2 \times 0.15}{0.45} = 0.667 = 66.7\%$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Balance mass: $$m_b = \frac{m r}{R} = \frac{60 \times 0.15}{0.20} = 45 \text{ kg}$$ **Final Answer:** $F_{P,\max} \approx 5.685$ kN, $F_{S,\max} \approx 3.79$ kN, $m_b = 45$ kg (placed at $180°$ to crank pin). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *— Alternative Choice* **Q. (a)** Explain the analytical method of balancing a single-cylinder reciprocating engine. Why is only partial balance achieved? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** The analytical method involves calculating the magnitude of a balance mass and its angular position on the crankshaft such that the centrifugal force it generates cancels the primary inertia force of the reciprocating parts. **Derivation (3 marks):** For primary balance, equate the centrifugal force of the balance mass to the maximum primary inertia force: $$m_b R \omega^2 = m r \omega^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_b = \frac{m r}{R}$$ The mass is placed at $180°$ to the crank pin (i.e., diametrically opposite on the crank web) so that its outward centrifugal force acts in the opposite direction to the piston's inertia force. **Why Only Partial Balance (2 marks):** 1. The rotating balance mass cannot reproduce the *linear* reciprocating motion of the piston — it can only cancel the horizontal component of inertia force at the crank center. 2. The **secondary force** (at $2\omega$) cannot be balanced by a mass rotating at $\omega$. 3. A residual vertical shaking force (rocking couple) persists between the cylinder and the crankshaft bearings. 4. Therefore, the engine is *partially balanced* — primary shaking is removed, but secondary and rocking effects remain. --- **Q. (b)** The crank-pin of a three-cylinder radial engine has a stroke of $300$ mm. The three connecting rods are coupled to a common crank. Show, with a vector polygon, that the system can be completely balanced. **(7 marks)** > [!NOTE] > **Note to the student:** This question is included in the alternative choice to test your ability to extend the principles to multi-cylinder (radial) configurations. While not strictly single-cylinder, the KTU 2024 syllabus does cover this as a related application. **Model Solution Sketch:** In a radial engine with $n$ cylinders, the crank is a single pin. The inertia forces from the cylinders act along their respective lines of stroke. By placing the cylinders at angular spacings of $360°/n$ and firing them in the correct order, the inertia force vectors form a closed polygon and sum to **zero**. For 3 cylinders at $120°$ spacings: the three primary inertia force vectors $F_1$, $F_2$, $F_3$ (each of magnitude $mr\omega^2$ but at different phases $0°, 120°, 240°$) form an equilateral triangle when placed head-to-tail, closing to zero. Similarly for the secondary forces (at $0°, 240°, 480°\equiv120°$, another equilateral triangle). **Result:** Both primary and secondary forces of the radial engine are completely balanced by the closed vector polygon method. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Don't skip the differentiation** — Show $\dfrac{dx}{dt}$ and $\dfrac{d^2x}{dt^2}$ explicitly. Half a mark is deducted for every missing intermediate step. > 2. **Sign convention errors** — Inertia force opposes acceleration; ensure your arrow points in the correct direction (leftward when the piston is at TDC decelerating). > 3. **Confusing $r/L$ with the secondary-to-primary ratio** — The correct ratio is $\dfrac{2r}{L}$ (with the factor of 2 due to the chain rule) OR $\dfrac{r}{L}$ depending on your choice of $x$ expression. **State your assumption clearly**. > 4. **Forgetting units** — The examiner's key demands SI units (N for force, m for length, kg for mass). Mixing mm and m is a common 0.5-mark deduction. > 5. **Not drawing a clear FBD** — A 7-mark question without a labeled free-body diagram loses at least 1 mark in the KTU 2024 scheme. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Read this 5 times before the exam!** - **Two Types of Inertia Forces** — **Primary** (frequency $\omega$, amplitude $mr\omega^2$) and **Secondary** (frequency $2\omega$, amplitude $2mr^2\omega^2/L$). - **Inertia Force Equation (Master Formula):** $F_I = mr\omega^2\cos\theta + \dfrac{2mr^2\omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$. - **Approximation Rule:** For $r/L \le 1/4$, the SHM approximation $x \approx r\cos\theta$ is valid, and the secondary force becomes small. - **Complete Balance is Impossible** for single-cylinder engines — only the primary force can be balanced with a single rotating mass. - **Balance Mass Formula:** $m_b = \dfrac{m \cdot r}{R}$ — independent of engine speed! - **Balance Mass Position:** Exactly $180°$ from the crank pin on the crank web. - **Secondary force is unavoidable in single-cylinder** engines. To cancel it, you need a balance shaft rotating at $2\omega$ — not done in practice for cost reasons. - **Effect of $L$:** Longer connecting rod = smaller secondary force = smoother engine. - **Effect of $\omega$:** Both forces grow with $\omega^2$ — that's why engines vibrate more at high RPM. - **Effect of $r$:** A larger crank radius increases both forces (primary linearly, secondary quadratically). - **Practical Solution in Modern Engines:** Use of dual-mass flywheels, balance shafts (in inline 3 and 4 cylinders), and tuned vibration dampers. - **Engine cycle for firing order** does not affect single-cylinder balance, but is critical for multi-cylinder. > **Formula Revision Card (Print and carry!):** > $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad\quad F_P = m r \omega^2 \cos\theta \quad\quad F_S = \frac{2 m r^2 \omega^2}{L}\cos 2\theta$$ > $$m_b = \frac{mr}{R} \quad\quad \frac{F_{S,\max}}{F_{P,\max}} = \frac{2r}{L}$$ > **End of Module 3 Notes — Balancing of Single Cylinder Reciprocating Engines**

topics.title: Gyroscopic Couple
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gyroscopic Couple

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Gyroscopic Couple

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gyroscopic Couple** is the resultant external reactive couple that must be applied to a rotating body (a *gyroscope* or *spinning rotor*) whose spin axis is itself being forced to rotate (or *precess*) about a perpendicular axis, in order to maintain that precessional motion. Equivalently, it is the couple produced by the rate of change of the **angular momentum** vector of the spinning body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A gyroscopic couple is the couple acting on a body due to the change in direction of the angular momentum vector when the axis of spin itself rotates (precesses) about another axis. Mathematically, the active gyroscopic couple magnitude is given by **C = I·ω·ω_p** (in SI units: N·m), where *I* is the mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis, *ω* is the spin (rotational) angular velocity in rad/s, and *ω_p* is the precessional (turning) angular velocity in rad/s.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a rapidly spinning **bicycle wheel** held by a string at one end of its axle.

- If the wheel is **not spinning**, gravity simply makes the free end swing downward.
- If the wheel **is spinning fast**, the free end does *not* fall. Instead, it slowly rotates (precesses) in a horizontal circle.
- Your wrist feels a continuous **twisting push** — this twisting push is the **gyroscopic couple**.

Think of it this way:

| Element | Everyday Analogy | Engineering Term |
|---|---|---|
| Spinning wheel | Spinning top | Rotor / Gyroscope |
| Tilt you try to impose | The wheel trying to tip | Precession |
| Wrist reaction force | The push that turns it sideways | Gyroscopic couple |
| The handle that holds it | The frame of a ship | Gimbals / Bearings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key intuition:** A spinning body "resists" any attempt to change the *direction* of its spin axis. The reaction is the gyroscopic couple. It does **not** resist the spin itself — it resists the **tilting or turning of the axis**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

- **Angular velocity (ω, ω_p):** measured in **rad/s** (radians per second). In some problems it is given in **rpm**, which must be converted: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- **Mass moment of inertia (I):** measured in **kg·m²**.
- **Gyroscopic couple (C):** measured in **N·m**.
- **Earth's rotation rate (ω_e):** ≈ **7.2921 × 10⁻⁵ rad/s** — relevant for marine and aerospace gyroscopic problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics — Vector Logic in Steps

A gyroscopic system has **two simultaneous rotations** occurring:

1. **Spin** — the rotor rotates about its own symmetry axis with angular velocity vector $\vec{\omega}$.
2. **Precession** — the spin axis itself rotates about a second (perpendicular) axis with angular velocity vector $\vec{\omega}_p$.

The combined motion changes the *direction* of the angular momentum vector $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$ continuously in space. By Euler's rotational dynamics:

$$\vec{C} = \dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{H} = \vec{\omega}_p \times (I\vec{\omega})$$

Step-by-step reasoning:

- **Step 1 — Angular momentum of spin:** $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$ (vector along spin axis).
- **Step 2 — Impose precession:** The frame holding the spin axis rotates with $\vec{\omega}_p$. This rotates the $\vec{H}$ vector around the precession axis.
- **Step 3 — Rate of change:** $\dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{H}$ (transport theorem for vectors in a rotating frame, when magnitude is constant).
- **Step 4 — Couple magnitude:** $\vert \vec{C} \vert = \omega_p \cdot H \cdot \sin\theta = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{\omega}$ and $\vec{\omega}_p$.
- **Step 5 — For KTU problems:** Spin axis ⊥ Precession axis in most cases ⟹ $\theta = 90°$ ⟹ $\sin\theta = 1$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why vector cross-product?** The change in direction of a vector of fixed magnitude rotating at $\vec{\omega}_p$ is always perpendicular to both the vector and the rotation axis.
- **Why sin θ in the general case?** The *effective* precession rate that tilts the angular momentum is the component of $\vec{\omega}_p$ *perpendicular* to $\vec{H}$, which is $\omega_p \sin\theta$.
- **Why is the couple reactive?** By Newton's third law, the active couple you apply to tilt the axis is reacted to by an equal and opposite couple on the supporting frame — this is the *passive* gyroscopic couple felt by the vehicle/structure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spin angular velocity (rad/s) | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | N is rpm |
| 2 | Precessional angular velocity | $\omega_p = \dfrac{2\pi n}{60}$ | rad/s | n is rpm of turning |
| 3 | Linear velocity in ship/vehicle | $v = r\omega_p$ | m/s | r = turning radius |
| 4 | General gyroscopic couple | $C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$ | N·m | $\theta$ = angle between spin & precession axes |
| 5 | Standard case (axes ⊥) | $C = I\omega\omega_p$ | N·m | Most KTU problems |
| 6 | Ship pitching couple | $C = I\omega \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{60}\cdot\dfrac{V}{\lambda}$ | N·m | V = ship speed, $\lambda$ = wavelength |
| 7 | Ship rolling couple | $C = I\omega\omega_p\cos\phi$ | N·m | $\phi$ = angle of heel |
| 8 | Ship steering couple | $C = I\omega \cdot \dfrac{v}{R}$ | N·m | R = radius of turn |
| 9 | Two-wheeler turning (lean) | $C = m v \dfrac{v}{r}$ | N·m | Centripetal must balance weight component |
| 10 | Reaction couple on frame | $C_R = -C$ (opposite sense) | N·m | Newton's 3rd law |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign convention for KTU:** Always state whether the active couple is *applied to* the rotor (causing precession) or is the *reactive couple acting on* the frame (vehicle/ship). Examiners expect explicit "Active" vs "Reactive" labeling for full marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Naval Architecture:** Predicting roll and pitch oscillations in warships and stabiliser design.
- **Aerospace:** Inertial navigation systems, attitude control of satellites and aircraft.
- **Automotive:** Stability analysis of motorcycles and four-wheelers on banked/curved roads.
- **Defence:** Gyro-compass and gun stabilisation in tanks and ships.
- **Robotics & MEMS:** MEMS gyroscopes in smartphones and drones.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — General Gyroscopic Couple (Vector Form)

**Given:** A rigid rotor of mass moment of inertia $I$ about its spin axis (the **polar** axis), spinning with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ while its axis is constrained to rotate with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}_p$ about a perpendicular axis.

**To prove:** $C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$

**Derivation:**

Let the spin axis be along the $z$-axis and the precession axis be along the $x$-axis, with $\theta$ being the angle between them.

The angular momentum vector of the spinning rotor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{H} &= I\vec{\omega} \\
\text{Magnitude:}\quad H &= I\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

The vector $\vec{H}$ lies along the spin axis. As the precession frame rotates with $\vec{\omega}_p$, the *direction* of $\vec{H}$ continuously changes. The **rate of change of a vector of constant magnitude rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}_p$** is, by the transport theorem:

$$
\dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{H}
$$

Substituting $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$:

$$
\dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_p \times (I\vec{\omega}) = I(\vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{\omega})
$$

By Euler's second law for rotation, the external couple required is:

$$
\vec{C} = \dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = I(\vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{\omega})
$$

Taking the magnitude using $|\vec{A}\times\vec{B}| = AB\sin\theta$:

$$
\boxed{\,C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p\,\sin\theta\,}
$$

For the standard **KTU case** where spin and precession axes are mutually perpendicular, $\theta = 90°$, so $\sin\theta = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\,C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Gyroscopic Couple on a Ship During Steering (Turn)

**Given:** A ship of mass $m$, turning at angular velocity $\omega_p$ about a vertical axis (yaw axis), with rotor/engine flywheel spinning at $\omega$ about its longitudinal (athwartships-perpendicular-to-keel) axis.

**To derive:** Active and reactive gyroscopic couples.

**Step 1 — Precessional angular velocity in rad/s:**

$$
\omega_p = \dfrac{v}{R}
$$

where $v$ is the linear speed of the ship and $R$ is the radius of the turn.

**Step 2 — Magnitude of gyroscopic couple:**

$$
C = I\omega\omega_p
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $\omega_p$:**

$$
C = I\omega \cdot \dfrac{v}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Reactive couple on the ship (frame):**

By Newton's third law, the ship experiences a couple of equal magnitude in the *opposite sense*:

$$
C_R = I\omega \cdot \dfrac{v}{R} \quad \text{(opposite direction)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** In ship problems, *active* couple tilts the rotor axis; *reactive* couple tilts the ship (heel/roll) — examiners specifically ask "in which direction does the ship tend to heel?"

### 3.3 Derivation III — Gyroscopic Couple on an Aeroplane / Two-Wheeler in a Turn

**Given:** A vehicle of mass $m$ turning with linear velocity $v$ on a curve of radius $R$. Effective spin angular momentum of the rotating parts (engine, wheels) is $H = I\omega$.

**Derivation:**

Precessional rate of the vehicle's path:

$$
\omega_p = \dfrac{v}{R}
$$

Gyroscopic couple magnitude:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= I\omega \cdot \omega_p \\
  &= I\omega \cdot \dfrac{v}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **two-wheeler**, this couple must be balanced by the moment created by the centripetal force $mv^2/R$ acting at the centre of gravity, which lies at horizontal distance equal to the *lean angle* geometry. Equilibrium gives the required lean angle $\phi$:

$$
mg\sin\phi = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}\cos\phi
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\tan\phi = \dfrac{v^2}{gR}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Direction of Reactive Couple for Common Cases

| Case | Spin Axis | Precession Axis | Reactive Couple Axis | Effect on Vehicle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ship steering (turn) | Longitudinal (fore–aft) | Vertical (yaw) | Transverse (athwartships) | Ship heels inward/outward |
| Ship pitching | Longitudinal | Transverse (pitch) | Vertical | Ship noses up/down |
| Ship rolling | Transverse | Longitudinal (roll) | Vertical | Roll stabiliser needed |
| Aeroplane turning | Longitudinal | Vertical | Transverse | Bank / roll tendency |
| Four-wheeler on curve | Transverse (wheels) | Vertical | Longitudinal | Nose dip/lift |
| Two-wheeler on curve | Transverse (wheels) | Vertical | Longitudinal | Must lean inward |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Gyroscopic Couple Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor Spinning with Angular Velocity omega] --> B[Spin Angular Momentum H = I omega]
    B --> C{Precession Imposed}
    C --> D[Frame Rotates with Angular Velocity omega_p]
    D --> E[Direction of H vector continuously changes]
    E --> F[Rate of Change dH/dt = omega_p x H]
    F --> G[Active Gyroscopic Couple C on Rotor]
    G --> H[Reactive Couple -C on Frame]
    H --> I[Heel / Pitch / Roll / Bank of Vehicle]
    I --> J{Application Domain}
    J --> K1[Naval - Ship Steering]
    J --> K2[Aerospace - Aircraft Turn]
    J --> K3[Automotive - Vehicle on Curve]
    J --> K4[Defence - Stabilised Gun Platform]
```

### 4.2 Vector-Cross-Product Mermaid Block — Direction Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[omega - Spin Axis] -->|cross product| C[C = omega_p x H]
    B[omega_p - Precession Axis] -->|cross product| C
    C --> D[Couple vector along axis perpendicular to both]
    D --> E[Active couple tends to TILT the spin axis]
    D --> F[Reactive couple tends to TILT the frame in opposite sense]
```

### 4.3 Application Decision Flow — How to Apply the Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Given Problem]) --> Q1{Identify the rotating body?}
    Q1 -->|Ship engine / flywheel| A1[Use I of flywheel]
    Q1 -->|Aeroplane propeller| A2[Use I of propeller]
    Q1 -->|Vehicle wheels| A3[Use I of wheels]
    A1 --> Q2{Type of motion?}
    A2 --> Q2
    A3 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Steering / Turning| S1[omega_p = v / R]
    Q2 -->|Pitching| S2[omega_p = 2 pi V / lambda]
    Q2 -->|Rolling| S3[omega_p = 2 pi V / lambda, include cos phi]
    S1 --> F[Compute C = I omega omega_p]
    S2 --> F
    S3 --> F
    F --> R[Identify Reactive Direction via Right Hand Rule]
    R --> End([Answer: Magnitude + Direction of Couple])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Right-Hand Rule** is the standard KTU-expected method: curl fingers from the **spin vector $\vec{\omega}$** toward the **precession vector $\vec{\omega}_p$**; the thumb gives the direction of the **active gyroscopic couple**. The reactive couple is opposite.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**Q1.** Define the term **gyroscopic couple**. State the expression for its magnitude and explain the significance of each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *gyroscopic couple* is the external couple required to change the direction of the spin axis of a rotating body that is itself precessing about a perpendicular axis. It arises from the rate of change of angular momentum $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$. The magnitude is

$$C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$$

where $I$ is the mass moment of inertia of the rotor (kg·m²), $\omega$ is the spin angular velocity (rad/s), $\omega_p$ is the precessional angular velocity (rad/s), and $\theta$ is the angle between spin and precession axes. For $\theta = 90°$, $C = I\omega\omega_p$ in N·m. **(3 marks — full)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**

**Q2.** A disc of mass moment of inertia $I = 0.5\text{ kg·m}^2$ spins at 3000 rpm about its own axis. Find the gyroscopic couple when the axis precesses at 4 rad/s about a perpendicular axis.

**Model Answer:**

Convert spin angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 3000}{60} = 314.16$ rad/s.

Precessional angular velocity: $\omega_p = 4$ rad/s. Axes are perpendicular, so $\sin 90° = 1$.

$$C = I\omega\omega_p = 0.5 \times 314.16 \times 4 = 628.32\ \text{N·m}$$

**Answer:** $C \approx 628.3$ N·m. **(3 marks — full)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A.** (a) Derive the expression for the magnitude of the gyroscopic couple acting on a spinning rotor whose axis is rotating (precessing) about a perpendicular axis. State clearly the vector form and discuss the direction using the right-hand rule. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A ship of mass 12000 tonnes has a turbine rotor of mass moment of inertia $I = 9000$ kg·m² spinning at 2000 rpm about the longitudinal axis. The ship turns at a speed of 36 km/h on a curve of radius 250 m. Find (i) the active gyroscopic couple, (ii) the reactive gyroscopic couple on the ship, and (iii) the angular acceleration of precession if the yawing moment of inertia of the ship about the vertical axis is $1.2 \times 10^8$ kg·m². **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Let the rotor spin with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ about the $z$-axis, and the spin axis be carried around with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}_p$ about the $x$-axis (perpendicular).

Angular momentum of spin: $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$, magnitude $H = I\omega$.

Since the direction of $\vec{H}$ continuously changes as the frame precesses, applying the transport theorem:

$$\dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{H} = \vec{\omega}_p \times (I\vec{\omega}) = I(\vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{\omega})$$

By Euler's law, the external couple is $\vec{C} = \dfrac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$.

Taking magnitude:

$$C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$$

For perpendicular axes, $\theta = 90° \Rightarrow C = I\omega\omega_p$.

**Direction (Right-Hand Rule):** Curl fingers from $\vec{\omega}$ to $\vec{\omega}_p$; thumb gives $\vec{C}_{active}$ (the couple you must apply to *cause* the precession). The reactive couple on the frame is $-\vec{C}$.

**(7 marks breakdown: 3 marks — vector derivation, 2 marks — magnitude, 2 marks — direction explanation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Active gyroscopic couple:**

Spin angular velocity: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 2000}{60} = 209.44$ rad/s.

Precessional angular velocity: Ship speed $v = 36$ km/h $= 10$ m/s; turning radius $R = 250$ m.

$$\omega_p = \dfrac{v}{R} = \dfrac{10}{250} = 0.04\ \text{rad/s}$$

Active couple:

$$C = I\omega\omega_p = 9000 \times 209.44 \times 0.04 = 75{,}398.4\ \text{N·m} \approx 75.4\ \text{kN·m}$$

**(ii) Reactive gyroscopic couple:** By Newton's third law, $C_R = 75.4$ kN·m, opposite in direction (tends to heel the ship).

**(iii) Angular acceleration of precession:**

$$C_R = I_{ship}\,\alpha \Rightarrow \alpha = \dfrac{C_R}{I_{ship}} = \dfrac{75{,}398.4}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 6.28 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad/s}^2$$

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 — ω and ω_p, 2 — active C, 2 — reactive + direction, 1 — angular acceleration)**

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice*

**Q.B.** (a) Explain with a neat sketch the phenomenon of **gyroscopic precession**. State and prove the general expression for the gyroscopic couple. **(7 marks)**

**(b) A two-wheeler of mass 250 kg with a rider travels at 54 km/h on a curved road of radius 30 m. The engine flywheel has a mass moment of inertia 0.15 kg·m² and rotates at 5000 rpm in a direction opposite to the rear wheel. Determine: (i) the gyroscopic couple on the vehicle, (ii) the angle of lean required to maintain equilibrium, and (iii) the centrifugal force experienced by the vehicle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Phenomenon + Derivation (7 marks):**

*Phenomenon:* When a rotor spinning with high angular velocity has its spin axis forced to change direction (precess), it produces a gyroscopic couple perpendicular to both the spin and precession axes. Sketch must show: spin vector $\vec{\omega}$ along axis of rotor, precession vector $\vec{\omega}_p$ along turning axis, and the couple vector $\vec{C}$ perpendicular to both, with the reactive couple $-\vec{C}$ on the frame.

*Derivation (as in SECTION 3.1):* $C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$, reducing to $C = I\omega\omega_p$ for perpendicular axes. **(7 marks: 3 — sketch + explanation, 4 — derivation)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Gyroscopic couple:**

$v = 54$ km/h $= 15$ m/s; $R = 30$ m.

$$\omega_p = \dfrac{v}{R} = \dfrac{15}{30} = 0.5\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 5000}{60} = 523.6\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$C = I\omega\omega_p = 0.15 \times 523.6 \times 0.5 = 39.27\ \text{N·m}$$

**(ii) Angle of lean for equilibrium:**

$$
\tan\phi = \dfrac{v^2}{gR} = \dfrac{15^2}{9.81 \times 30} = \dfrac{225}{294.3} = 0.7646
$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.7646) = 37.4°$$

**(iii) Centrifugal force:**

$$F_c = \dfrac{mv^2}{R} = \dfrac{250 \times 225}{30} = 1875\ \text{N}$$

**(7 marks breakdown: 2 — couple, 3 — lean angle derivation + value, 2 — centrifugal force)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion (rpm → rad/s):** KTU evaluators strictly deduct 1 mark if $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ is not shown.
> 2. **Confusing Active vs Reactive couple:** Always state clearly. A 14-mark answer without direction specification loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Using $\omega_p = v/R$ for ship pitching:** This is wrong — use $\omega_p = 2\pi v/\lambda$ for pitching/rolling.
> 4. **Sign of the couple for two-wheelers:** When the engine rotates *opposite* to the rear wheel, the effective spin used in $C = I\omega\omega_p$ already accounts for the relative sense — do not double-negate.
> 5. **Neglecting $\sin\theta$:** If the spin and precession axes are not perpendicular (rare in KTU but possible), $\sin\theta$ is required. Default assumption must be stated.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Core formula:** $C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$; default $\theta = 90° \Rightarrow C = I\omega\omega_p$.
- ✅ **Vector form:** $\vec{C} = \vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{H} = I(\vec{\omega}_p \times \vec{\omega})$.
- ✅ **Always convert rpm to rad/s** using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
- ✅ **Active couple** tilts the rotor; **reactive couple** tilts the frame (Newton's 3rd law).
- ✅ **Right-Hand Rule** determines direction: $\vec{\omega} \to \vec{\omega}_p \Rightarrow$ thumb = active couple direction.
- ✅ **Ship steering:** $\omega_p = v/R$; **Ship pitching/rolling:** $\omega_p = 2\pi v/\lambda$ (with $\cos\phi$ for rolling).
- ✅ **Two-wheeler lean angle:** $\tan\phi = v^2/(gR)$.
- ✅ **Four-wheeler nose dip/lift:** couple direction depends on whether engine is in line with or opposite to wheel rotation.
- ✅ **Aeroplane banking** in turns — same principle, transverse-axis couple.
- ✅ **Earth's rotation** ($\omega_e = 7.29 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s) is irrelevant for short-duration problems but vital for marine gyrocompass derivations.
- ✅ **Units check:** I [kg·m²] · ω [rad/s] · ω_p [rad/s] = [kg·m²/s²] = [N·m]. ✓
- ✅ **KTU marking policy:** Show every step; never skip the unit conversion; always label active vs reactive; always state the direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects on the Stability of Automobiles, Two Wheeler and Four Wheeler, Stability of Ships and Air Crafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects on the Stability of Automobiles, Two Wheeler and Four Wheeler, Stability of Ships and Air Crafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Vehicle & Body Stability — The Formal Definition

**Stability of a moving body** is defined as the inherent property of the body (automobile, two-wheeler, four-wheeler, ship, or aircraft) to return to its equilibrium state (or to maintain steady motion) when subjected to small disturbing forces or moments such as centrifugal force during cornering, wind gusts, wave action, or aerodynamic disturbances.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3)**, stability is broadly classified into:

- **Static Stability** – ability to remain upright under gravity (ships at rest, parked vehicle).
- **Dynamic Stability** – ability to maintain the intended trajectory under motion-induced forces (cornering, turning, banking, pitching, rolling, yawing).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMRT756):** This topic is the culminating application module that integrates principles of **kinetics** (force, mass, acceleration), **rotational dynamics**, and **fluid statics (Archimedes' principle)** to analyse the equilibrium of land, sea, and air vehicles.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a tall glass of water while running around a circular track:

- If you run **too fast**, the water spills outward — this is the **centrifugal effect** that tries to overturn the glass.
- If the track were **tilted inward** (like a bowl), the water would not spill because gravity itself pushes it back to the center — this is the **banking of roads**.
- A **ship floating in water** behaves similarly: when tilted, the buoyancy shifts to restore it — this shift is measured by the **metacentric height**.
- An **aircraft in flight** is like an arrow balancing on a finger — its **tail surfaces, wings, and fuselage geometry** must be designed so any gust nudges it back to level flight.

Thus, *stability is the geometric and inertial design of the body that resists being tipped over by disturbing influences*.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics Used

The following standard metrics are used throughout this module:

- **Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$**
- **Coefficient of friction ($\mu$):** typically $0.4$ to $0.8$ for dry road tyres on asphalt.
- **Radius of curve (R):** measured in **metres**.
- **Superelevation / Bank angle ($\theta$):** measured in **degrees or radians**.
- **Metacentric height (GM):** measured in **metres**, positive for a stable ship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core KTU Definition:** *Critical Speed* is the maximum speed at which a vehicle can negotiate a curved path of radius R without overturning or skidding. It is the most frequently tested parameter in Part B questions of ESE Module 3.

---

### 1.4 Types of Disturbing Forces Considered

For a moving body, the principal destabilising influences are:

1. **Centrifugal force** ($\frac{mv^2}{R}$) — acting outward on a curved path.
2. **Wind/gust loads** — aerodynamic side forces on aircraft and tall vehicles.
3. **Wave impact and roll** — for ships at sea.
4. **Load shifting** — for trucks carrying free-moving cargo.
5. **Cross-slope and camber** — for road vehicles.

The study of stability therefore becomes a **force-balance and moment-balance problem**, where the designer ensures that the disturbing moment never exceeds the restoring moment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Four-Wheeler (Automobile) Stability on a Level Curved Road

#### Free Body Diagram Logic

When a four-wheeler of mass $m$ travels on a curved road of radius $R$ at velocity $v$, three forces act on it:

| Force | Magnitude | Direction | Line of Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Weight | $W = mg$ | Vertically downward | Through CG |
| Centrifugal force | $P = \frac{mv^2}{R}$ | Outward (away from curve centre) | Through CG |
| Normal reaction | $R_N$ | Vertically upward | Through tyre contact patch |

#### Stability Conditions (Three Critical Limits)

**Condition 1 — Condition for No Overturning (about the outer wheel):**
The overturning moment ($P \cdot h$) must be less than the restoring moment ($W \cdot b$), where $h$ is the height of CG above ground and $b$ is the half-wheel-track.

**Condition 2 — Condition for No Skidding (lateral):**
The centrifugal force must be less than the maximum frictional resistance ($\mu W$).

**Condition 3 — Condition for No Sliding (longitudinal, on gradient):**
Forces parallel to the inclined surface must balance.

---

### 2.2 Two-Wheeler Stability on a Curved Path

A two-wheeler (motorcycle or bicycle) has **only two contact points** (a line contact), so it cannot resist a side-tipping moment through wheel separation. Instead, the **rider and vehicle lean inward** by an angle $\phi$ so that the resultant of weight and centrifugal force passes through the line of contact.

#### Equilibrium of Leaning Two-Wheeler

The condition is simply:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{v^2}{gR}$$

This is the **rider's lean angle** with respect to the vertical, and it is **independent of the mass** of the system.

---

### 2.3 Banking of Roads (Superelevation)

To reduce wear on tyres, prevent skidding, and improve passenger comfort, the outer edge of a curved road is raised relative to the inner edge. This inclination is called **superelevation** or **bank angle** $\theta$.

#### Frictionless Banking Equation

For a vehicle negotiating a banked curve at the design speed $v$ with **no side friction**:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{gR}$$

#### Banking with Friction

When the vehicle travels at a speed different from the design speed, friction assists or resists the centripetal requirement:

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2 \mp \mu gR}{gR \pm \mu v^2}$$

- **Upper signs (+)** apply when the vehicle travels **slower** than design speed (friction acts outward).
- **Lower signs (−)** apply when the vehicle travels **faster** than design speed (friction acts inward).

---

### 2.4 Stability of Ships (Fluid Statics Application)

#### Key Definitions

- **Centre of Gravity (G)** — point where the weight of the ship acts.
- **Centre of Buoyancy (B)** — centroid of the displaced water volume.
- **Metacentre (M)** — point where the vertical line through the new centre of buoyancy (when the ship is tilted by a small angle) intersects the original vertical axis of symmetry.
- **Metacentric Height (GM)** — distance between G and M; a measure of **static stability**.

#### Metacentric Height Formula

$$GM = \frac{I}{V} - BG = BM - BG$$

where:

- $I$ = second moment of area of the waterline plane about the axis of tilt.
- $V$ = volume of water displaced (equal to ship weight divided by water density).
- $BG$ = vertical distance between B and G.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condition for Stable Ship:** The metacentre **M must lie above the centre of gravity G**, i.e., **GM must be positive**. If $GM < 0$, the ship capsizes for even a small disturbance.

#### Experimental Determination (Oscillation Method)

A small known mass $m$ is shifted across the deck by a distance $x$. The resulting tilt angle $\theta$ is measured. Then:

$$GM = \frac{m \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$$

---

### 2.5 Stability of Aircraft

Aircraft stability is analysed in three rotational axes:

| Axis | Motion | Control Surface | Stabiliser |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Longitudinal (pitch) | Nose up/down | Elevator | Horizontal tail |
| Lateral (roll) | Wing up/down | Ailerons | Dihedral, high wing |
| Directional (yaw) | Nose left/right | Rudder | Vertical fin |

#### Aircraft Stability Conditions

- **Longitudinal Stability:** CG must lie **ahead of the Centre of Pressure** (CP) of the main wing, typically requiring a **horizontal tailplane** to provide a restoring pitching moment.
- **Lateral Stability:** Achieved by **dihedral** (upward inclination of wings), **high-wing configuration**, and **sweepback**.
- **Directional Stability:** Provided by the **vertical fin (keel)** which resists side-slip and yaw.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** A common mistake is to confuse **static stability** (initial tendency to return) with **dynamic stability** (ability to damp oscillations). Aircraft designers aim for both.

---

### 2.6 Effects on Vehicle Stability

The factors that influence the stability of all the above vehicles are consolidated below:

| S.No. | Factor | Effect on Stability |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Higher CG (large $h$) | Reduces critical overturning speed |
| 2 | Wider wheelbase ($2b$) | Increases overturning speed (improves stability) |
| 3 | Higher coefficient of friction ($\mu$) | Increases critical skidding speed |
| 4 | Increased banking ($\theta$) | Allows higher safe cornering speed |
| 5 | Lower metacentric height (GM) | Reduces ship stability; risk of capsizing |
| 6 | Forward CG in aircraft | Improves longitudinal stability |
| 7 | Cross-wind / gusts | Destabilises all moving bodies |
| 8 | Sudden load shift (cargo, fuel) | Moves CG, altering all stability margins |

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Concept | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Centrifugal force on curved path | $P = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$ | N |
| 2 | Critical overturning speed (four-wheeler) | $v_o = \sqrt{\dfrac{gRb}{h}}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Critical skidding speed (four-wheeler) | $v_s = \sqrt{\mu g R}$ | m/s |
| 4 | Banking angle (frictionless) | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{v^2}{gR}$ | radians |
| 5 | Banking with friction | $\tan\theta = \dfrac{v^2 \mp \mu gR}{gR \pm \mu v^2}$ | radians |
| 6 | Lean angle of two-wheeler | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{v^2}{gR}$ | radians |
| 7 | Metacentric height (analytical) | $GM = \dfrac{I}{V} - BG$ | m |
| 8 | Metacentric height (experimental) | $GM = \dfrac{m \cdot x}{W \tan\theta}$ | m |
| 9 | Period of oscillation of ship | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$ | s |
| 10 | Aircraft longitudinal stability | CG ahead of CP | — |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Road Design (Civil Engineering):** IRC:73 and AASHTO prescribe superelevation limits ($4\%$ to $7\%$) for highways based on the above formulas.
- **Ship Design (Naval Architecture):** GM values are maintained between $0.05 \, \text{m}$ (passenger ships, comfort) and values large enough for safe rolling in cargo ships.
- **Aircraft Design (Aerospace):** Stability derivatives and control surface sizing are derived from analogous moment-balance equations.
- **Motorcycle Racing:** Riders consciously manipulate lean angle $\phi$ by shifting body weight — a direct application of the two-wheeler stability equation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Overturning Speed of a Four-Wheeler on a Level Curved Road

**Given:** A four-wheeler of mass $m$, weight $W = mg$, travelling on a level curved road of radius $R$ at velocity $v$. Let $h$ = height of CG above ground, $2b$ = wheel track (distance between contact patches of left and right wheels).

**Step 1 — Identify all forces acting on the vehicle.**

When the vehicle moves on a curve, the inertia of the vehicle (in the rotating frame) gives rise to a centrifugal force:
$$P = \frac{mv^2}{R}$$

This force acts horizontally outward through the CG. The weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward through the CG. The normal reactions at the inner wheel ($R_I$) and outer wheel ($R_O$) act upward through the contact patches.

**Step 2 — Take moments about the outer wheel contact point (the point about which overturning would occur).**

For the vehicle to be just on the verge of overturning, the inner wheel reaction $R_I$ becomes zero. Taking moments about the outer contact point O, anti-clockwise positive:

$$W \cdot b - P \cdot h = 0$$

> *Reasoning:* The weight $W$ acting at the CG provides a restoring moment $W \cdot b$ about the outer wheel. The centrifugal force $P$ acting at height $h$ provides an overturning moment $P \cdot h$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the force expressions.**

$$(mg) \cdot b = \frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Cancel mass $m$ from both sides.**

$$g \cdot b = \frac{v^2 \cdot h}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the critical overturning velocity $v_o$.**

$$v_o^2 = \frac{g R b}{h}$$

$$\boxed{v_o = \sqrt{\frac{gRb}{h}}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Skidding Speed of a Four-Wheeler on a Level Curved Road

**Step 1 — Write the lateral force equilibrium.**

The centrifugal force $P$ must not exceed the maximum available frictional resistance at the tyres:
$$P \leq \mu W$$

**Step 2 — Substitute force expressions.**

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} \leq \mu \, mg$$

**Step 3 — Cancel mass and solve.**

$$v_s^2 = \mu g R$$

$$\boxed{v_s = \sqrt{\mu g R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The condition for the vehicle to negotiate the curve **safely without either overturning or skidding** is that the actual speed $v$ must satisfy:
> $$v \leq \min\{v_o, v_s\}$$
> If $v_o > v_s$, the vehicle skids first; if $v_s > v_o$, it overturns first. Designers usually aim for $v_s > v_o$ for safety against rollover.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Banking Angle of a Road (Frictionless Case)

**Given:** A curved road of radius $R$ banked at angle $\theta$. A vehicle of mass $m$ moves at design speed $v$. Let the road surface be **frictionless** (idealised case for deriving the design speed).

**Step 1 — Draw forces in the plane perpendicular to the road surface.**

Resolve the normal reaction $N$ into horizontal and vertical components:

- Vertical: $N \cos\theta$ balances $W = mg$.
- Horizontal: $N \sin\theta$ provides the required centripetal force $\frac{mv^2}{R}$.

**Step 2 — Write the two equilibrium equations.**

Vertical equilibrium:
$$N \cos\theta = mg$$

Horizontal equilibrium (centripetal direction):
$$N \sin\theta = \frac{mv^2}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Divide the horizontal equation by the vertical equation.**

$$\frac{N \sin\theta}{N \cos\theta} = \frac{mv^2 / R}{mg}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{v^2}{gR}$$

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{gR \tan\theta}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the banking angle.**

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{v^2}{gR}\right)$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Lean Angle of a Two-Wheeler on a Curve

**Given:** A two-wheeler of total mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ on a curve of radius $R$. Let the rider-vehicle system lean inward by an angle $\phi$ from the vertical.

**Step 1 — Identify the forces.**

- Weight $W = mg$ acts vertically downward through CG.
- Centrifugal force $P = \frac{mv^2}{R}$ acts horizontally outward through CG.
- Normal reaction $N$ acts along the line joining CG and the contact point (i.e., along the inclined axis of the vehicle).

**Step 2 — Apply moment equilibrium for the system not to topple.**

The resultant of $W$ and $P$ must pass through the contact line; otherwise, a net moment would topple the two-wheeler.

**Step 3 — Geometric relation of the resultant.**

The line of action of the resultant makes an angle $\phi$ with the vertical, where:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{P}{W} = \frac{mv^2 / R}{mg}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \frac{v^2}{gR}}$$

> This shows that the lean angle is **independent of mass** and depends only on velocity and curve radius.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Metacentric Height of a Ship (Analytical Method)

**Given:** A floating ship of weight $W$, displaced volume $V$, water density $\rho$, waterline area moment of inertia $I$. Let B = centre of buoyancy at equilibrium, G = centre of gravity, M = metacentre, and the ship is tilted by a small angle $\theta$.

**Step 1 — Condition of floatation.**

By Archimedes' principle, the buoyant force equals the weight:
$$W = \rho g V$$

**Step 2 — Position shift of the centre of buoyancy.**

When the ship tilts by a small angle $\theta$, the new centre of buoyancy $B'$ is displaced laterally. The vertical line through $B'$ intersects the original axis at the **metacentre M**.

**Step 3 — Use the geometry of the wedge.**

The volume of the wedge immersed on one side equals the volume emerged on the other. The shift $BB''$ (lateral) is related to the metacentre by:
$$BM = \frac{I}{V}$$

where $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterline plane about the longitudinal axis (axis of tilt).

**Step 4 — Relate GM to BM and BG.**

From the geometry of the triangle GBM:
$$GM = BM - BG = \frac{I}{V} - BG$$

**Step 5 — Stability condition.**

For the ship to be stable, the metacentre M must lie above G, which requires:
$$\boxed{GM > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{I}{V} > BG}$$

**Step 6 — Period of small oscillations (additional KTU result).**

If the ship is given a small angular displacement and released, it rolls with simple harmonic motion. The radius of gyration about the longitudinal axis is $k$. Then:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{k^2}{g \cdot GM}}$$

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Experimental Determination of GM (Oscillation / Deflection Method)

**Step 1 — Shift a known mass.**

A small mass $m$ is moved across the deck by a known distance $x$. This shifts the CG of the ship laterally.

**Step 2 — Measure the resulting tilt.**

The ship tilts by a small angle $\theta$, which is measured by a plumb line or inclinometer.

**Step 3 — Take moments to find the new effective CG location.**

The new combined CG lies at distance $\frac{m \cdot x}{W}$ from the original CG (measured laterally).

**Step 4 — Equate the shift to GM × tan(θ).**

By geometry, the lateral shift of the metacentre combination equals $GM \tan\theta$:
$$GM \tan\theta = \frac{m \cdot x}{W}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for GM.**

$$\boxed{GM = \frac{m \cdot x}{W \tan\theta}}$$

---

### 3.7 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Derivation No. | Quantity Derived | Final Expression | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 3.1 | Overturning speed | $v_o = \sqrt{gRb/h}$ | Max speed to avoid rollover |
| 3.2 | Skidding speed | $v_s = \sqrt{\mu g R}$ | Max speed to avoid lateral slide |
| 3.3 | Bank angle | $\tan\theta = v^2/(gR)$ | Angle to eliminate side friction |
| 3.4 | Two-wheeler lean | $\tan\phi = v^2/(gR)$ | Rider's inward tilt |
| 3.5 | Metacentric height | $GM = I/V - BG$ | Static stability of ship |
| 3.6 | GM (experimental) | $GM = mx/(W\tan\theta)$ | Practical measurement of GM |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Vehicle Stability Analysis Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the analytical procedure for stability assessment of moving bodies in the KTU Module 3 framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Input: Mass m, Velocity v, Radius R"] --> nodeB["Compute Centrifugal Force P = mv^2 over R"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Vehicle Type?"}
    nodeC --> nodeD["Four Wheeler"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Two Wheeler"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Ship"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Aircraft"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Check Overturning: v less than vo"]
    nodeD --> nodeI["Check Skidding: v less than vs"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["Compute Bank Angle: tan theta = v^2 over gR"]
    nodeE --> nodeK["Compute Lean Angle: tan phi = v^2 over gR"]
    nodeF --> nodeL["Compute GM = I over V minus BG"]
    nodeF --> nodeM["Verify GM greater than 0"]
    nodeG --> nodeN["Check CG vs Centre of Pressure"]
    nodeG --> nodeO["Verify Dihedral and Fin Effects"]
    nodeH --> nodeP["Decision: Stable or Unstable"]
    nodeI --> nodeP
    nodeJ --> nodeP
    nodeK --> nodeP
    nodeL --> nodeP
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
```

---

### 4.2 Force Diagram — Four-Wheeler on a Level Curved Road

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Weight W = mg (Downward through CG)"] --> nodeC["Vehicle on Curved Path of Radius R"]
    nodeB["Centrifugal Force P = mv squared over R (Outward through CG)"] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD["Reaction at Inner Wheel R sub I"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Reaction at Outer Wheel R sub O"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Condition: P dot h less than W dot b"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Critical Overturning Speed vo"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Lateral Friction Force = mu times W"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Critical Skidding Speed vs"]
```

---

### 4.3 Stability Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Stability of Moving Bodies"] --> nodeB["Land Vehicles"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Marine Vehicles"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Air Vehicles"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Four Wheeler: Overturning and Skidding"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Two Wheeler: Lean Angle"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Road Design: Banking Angle"]
    nodeC --> nodeH["Static Stability via Metacentre"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["Dynamic Stability via Oscillation"]
    nodeD --> nodeJ["Longitudinal via Tailplane"]
    nodeD --> nodeK["Lateral via Dihedral"]
    nodeD --> nodeL["Directional via Fin"]
```

---

### 4.4 Ship Stability — Metacentre Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Floating Ship in Equilibrium"] --> nodeB["Weight W = rho g V acts at G"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Buoyancy Force = W acts at B"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Tilt Ship by Small Angle theta"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Buoyancy Centre Shifts to B prime"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Vertical Line through B prime meets axis at M"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["GM = I over V minus BG"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"GM greater than 0?"}
    nodeH --> nodeI["STABLE SHIP"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["UNSTABLE SHIP - CAPSIZE RISK"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`nodeA`, `nodeB`, ...). All labels use raw uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., `rho g V`) without markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables, ensuring clean rendering across Mermaid parsers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*Define the term "critical speed" of a vehicle on a curved path. Distinguish between overturning critical speed and skidding critical speed.*

**Model Answer:**
The critical speed of a vehicle on a curved path is the maximum speed at which it can negotiate the curve without losing stability either by overturning or by skidding sideways.

- **Overturning critical speed** ($v_o = \sqrt{gRb/h}$) is the speed at which the vehicle is about to topple about its outer wheels due to the centrifugal overturning moment exceeding the gravitational restoring moment.
- **Skidding critical speed** ($v_s = \sqrt{\mu g R}$) is the speed at which the centrifugal force equals the maximum lateral frictional resistance available at the tyres, beyond which the vehicle slides outward.

A vehicle is safe on the curve when its actual speed $v \leq \min(v_o, v_s)$.

---

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*What is metacentric height of a ship? State the condition for a ship to be stable.*

**Model Answer:**
The metacentric height $GM$ of a ship is the vertical distance between the centre of gravity $G$ of the ship and the metacentre $M$. The metacentre is the point where the vertical line through the new (shifted) centre of buoyancy intersects the original vertical axis of symmetry of the ship, when the ship is given a small angular tilt.

**Condition for stability:** $GM > 0$, i.e., the metacentre must lie above the centre of gravity. Mathematically:
$$GM = \frac{I}{V} - BG > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{I}{V} > BG$$

If $GM < 0$, any disturbance produces an overturning couple and the ship capsizes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question B1 (A) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the critical speed of a four-wheeled vehicle on a level curved road with respect to **(i)** overturning and **(ii)** skidding. State the safe operating condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car weighing $20 \, \text{kN}$ has a wheel track of $1.4 \, \text{m}$ and its centre of gravity is $0.6 \, \text{m}$ above the road. It moves on a curve of radius $120 \, \text{m}$. Determine: **(i)** the speed at which it will overturn, **(ii)** the speed at which it will skid, if the coefficient of friction between tyres and road is $0.3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to Derivations 3.1 and 3.2 in Section 3.

(i) Overturning critical speed (taking moments about outer wheel, $R_I = 0$):

$$W \cdot b = \frac{m v_o^2}{R} \cdot h$$

$$\boxed{v_o = \sqrt{\frac{gRb}{h}}}$$

(ii) Skidding critical speed (centrifugal force = frictional resistance):

$$\frac{m v_s^2}{R} = \mu m g$$

$$\boxed{v_s = \sqrt{\mu g R}}$$

**Safe operating condition:**
$$v \leq \min(v_o, v_s)$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Weight $W = 20,000 \, \text{N}$, so $m = 20000/9.81 = 2038.7 \, \text{kg}$
- Wheel track $= 1.4 \, \text{m}$, so half-track $b = 0.7 \, \text{m}$
- CG height $h = 0.6 \, \text{m}$
- Curve radius $R = 120 \, \text{m}$
- $\mu = 0.3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**(i) Overturning speed:**

$$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 120 \times 0.7}{0.6}}$$

$$v_o = \sqrt{\frac{823.92}{0.6}} = \sqrt{1373.2}$$

$$v_o = 37.06 \, \text{m/s} = 133.4 \, \text{km/h}$$

**(ii) Skidding speed:**

$$v_s = \sqrt{0.3 \times 9.81 \times 120}$$

$$v_s = \sqrt{353.16} = 18.79 \, \text{m/s} = 67.7 \, \text{km/h}$$

Since $v_s < v_o$, the car **skids first** before it overturns. Hence, for safety, the car must not exceed $18.79 \, \text{m/s} \; (67.7 \, \text{km/h})$.

---

**Question B1 (B) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the stability of a two-wheeler negotiating a curved path. Derive the expression for the angle of lean. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship of weight $5000 \, \text{kN}$ has a metacentric height of $0.6 \, \text{m}$. A mass of $50 \, \text{kN}$ is shifted across the deck by $6 \, \text{m}$. Find the angle of heel of the ship. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

When a two-wheeler moves on a curved path, it must lean inward by an angle $\phi$ to maintain equilibrium. Refer to Derivation 3.4 in Section 3.

**Free body forces:**
- Weight $W = mg$ (vertically down through CG)
- Centrifugal force $P = mv^2/R$ (outward through CG)
- Normal reaction $N$ along the inclined line of the vehicle

For equilibrium, the line of action of the resultant of $W$ and $P$ must pass through the contact line of the tyres; otherwise a tipping moment acts.

From the geometry of the force triangle:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{P}{W} = \frac{mv^2/R}{mg}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \frac{v^2}{gR}}$$

The lean angle is independent of the mass of the system.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- Weight of ship $W = 5000 \, \text{kN}$
- Metacentric height $GM = 0.6 \, \text{m}$
- Shifted mass $m = 50 \, \text{kN}$
- Lateral shift $x = 6 \, \text{m}$

Using the experimental formula from Derivation 3.6:
$$GM = \frac{m \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$$

Solve for $\tan\theta$:
$$\tan\theta = \frac{m \cdot x}{W \cdot GM}$$

$$\tan\theta = \frac{50 \times 6}{5000 \times 0.6} = \frac{300}{3000} = 0.1$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.1) = 5.71^\circ}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Speeds in KTU problems are often given in **km/h**. Always convert to m/s using $v \, (\text{m/s}) = v \, (\text{km/h}) \times \dfrac{5}{18}$ *before* substitution into $v^2/(gR)$. Marks are deducted for direct km/h substitution.
> 2. **Wheel-Track Confusion:** The formula uses $b$ = **half** the wheel track (distance from CG to one wheel), not the full track. Read "wheel track of 1.4 m" carefully.
> 3. **Metacentre Above G:** Students often forget the sign convention. Always state **$GM > 0$ for stable ship** explicitly, even if the numerical value is obvious.
> 4. **Banking vs Superelevation:** Both terms refer to the same physical tilt, but "banking" is used for roads and "camber/superelevation" for railways — examiners may deduct marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 5. **Rounding:** Final answers in KTU scripts are accepted to **2 decimal places** for trig functions and **3 significant figures** for velocities.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Stability of Moving Bodies**

- **Critical Overturning Speed:** $\;v_o = \sqrt{gRb/h}$ — depends on CG height $h$ (inverse) and half-track $b$ (direct).
- **Critical Skidding Speed:** $\;v_s = \sqrt{\mu g R}$ — depends only on friction $\mu$ and radius $R$.
- **Safe Speed:** $\;v \leq \min(v_o, v_s)$.
- **Banking Angle:** $\;\tan\theta = v^2/(gR)$ (frictionless); with friction: $\tan\theta = (v^2 \mp \mu gR)/(gR \pm \mu v^2)$.
- **Two-Wheeler Lean:** $\;\tan\phi = v^2/(gR)$ — **mass independent**.
- **Ship Stability:** $\;GM = I/V - BG$. Stable only if **$GM > 0$** (metacentre above CG).
- **Ship Oscillation Period:** $\;T = 2\pi\sqrt{k^2/(g \cdot GM)}$.
- **GM (Experimental):** $\;GM = mx/(W\tan\theta)$.
- **Aircraft Stability Axes:** Longitudinal (pitch) — tailplane; Lateral (roll) — dihedral; Directional (yaw) — vertical fin.
- **Longitudinal Aircraft Stability Condition:** CG **must be ahead** of the Centre of Pressure (aerodynamic centre) of the wing.
- **Common Factor Effects:** Higher CG → lower stability; wider wheelbase → higher stability; higher $\mu$ → higher skid resistance.
- **Negative GM** = ship capsizes; **negative banking** = water/road drainage fails (design constraint).
- **IRC Recommendation:** Superelevation limited to **$4\% - 7\%$** on highways to avoid slow-vehicle toppling inward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Vibration** is defined as the oscillatory motion of a body or system about a position of equilibrium, characterized by its periodic or quasi-periodic displacement, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time.

In the context of **Dynamics & Machinery (PEMRT756)**, vibration is the study of the motion of mechanical systems and the forces that cause this motion. The entire subject of vibration analysis is concerned with the oscillatory motion of particles and rigid bodies, together with the forces associated with such motion.

**Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The minimum number of independent coordinates required to completely define the configuration or position of a system at any instant of time is called the **Degree of Freedom** of the system. For a simple mass-spring system moving in one direction, DOF = 1.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Analogy: Pushing a child on a swing**

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing in a playground. The swing goes back and forth, forward and backward, in a smooth, repeating pattern. This repetitive, to-and-fro motion is exactly what we call **Vibration**.

Now, three key questions naturally arise:
1. **How fast does it oscillate?** → This gives us the **Natural Frequency** (`f_n` or `ω_n`).
2. **How long does it keep oscillating before stopping?** → This tells us about **Damping**.
3. **What if I keep pushing at just the right timing?** → This leads to **Resonance**, which can cause the swing to go dangerously high!

In engineering machinery, every rotating shaft, gear, turbine blade, and vehicle suspension is essentially a more complex "swing" — and just like the playground swing, it can:
- Oscillate at a specific natural frequency
- Lose energy over time (damping)
- Build up dangerous amplitudes if excited at resonance

## 1.3 Why Study Vibration? (KTU Engineering Relevance)

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Significance of Vibration Analysis:**
> - **Mechanical Design:** Designing structures that can withstand dynamic loads (earthquakes, wind, machinery).
> - **Machinery Health:** Vibration analysis is the primary tool for **predictive maintenance** in rotating equipment (motors, pumps, turbines).
> - **Vehicle Engineering:** Suspension systems, tire dynamics, NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) comfort.
> - **Aerospace & Civil:** Flutter analysis, earthquake-resistant structures, building sway.

## 1.4 Standard Nomenclature and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit (SI) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| $m$ | Mass | **kg** |
| $k$ | Stiffness (spring constant) | **N/m** |
| $c$ | Damping coefficient | **N·s/m** |
| $x$ | Displacement from equilibrium | **m** |
| $\dot{x}$ | Velocity | **m/s** |
| $\ddot{x}$ | Acceleration | **m/s²** |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural angular frequency | **rad/s** |
| $f_n$ | Natural frequency | **Hz** |
| $T_n$ | Natural period | **s** |
| $\zeta$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| $F_0$ | Force amplitude (forced vibration) | **N** |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard gravitational acceleration constant used in dynamics is **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$**, though vibration problems typically do not involve gravity explicitly because equilibrium is referenced from the static deflection position.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vibration

Vibrations are broadly classified into the following categories:

### A. Based on Energy Considerations

1. **Free Vibration (Natural Vibration):**
   - Occurs when a system is displaced from its equilibrium position and then released, allowing it to oscillate without any external force.
   - The system vibrates at one or more of its **natural frequencies**.
   - Subdivided into: *Undamped* and *Damped* free vibration.

2. **Forced Vibration:**
   - Occurs when a system is subjected to a time-varying external force or excitation.
   - The system vibrates at the **forcing frequency**, not its natural frequency (after the transient dies out).

### B. Based on Restoring Force

1. **Longitudinal Vibration:** Particles of the system move parallel to the axis of the shaft. Example: Sound waves in air columns.
2. **Transverse (Lateral) Vibration:** Particles move perpendicular to the axis. Example: A cantilever beam carrying a mass at its free end.
3. **Torsional Vibration:** Particles oscillate about the axis (angular motion). Example: Twisting oscillations of a rotating shaft.

## 2.2 Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) System — The Foundation

The most fundamental vibrating system consists of:
- A **mass** $m$ (representing inertia)
- A **spring** of stiffness $k$ (representing elasticity/restoring force)
- A **damper** with coefficient $c$ (representing energy dissipation)
- An **external force** $F(t)$ (for forced vibration)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Equation of Motion** for a general SDOF system is the cornerstone equation:
> 
> $$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

This is a **second-order linear ordinary differential equation** with constant coefficients. The behavior of the system depends entirely on the relative values of $m$, $c$, and $k$.

## 2.3 Undamped Free Vibration ($c = 0$, $F(t) = 0$)

**The 'Why' and 'How':**
- **Why** does the mass oscillate? Because the spring exerts a restoring force proportional to displacement (Hooke's Law: $F_s = -kx$).
- **How** do we describe the motion? By solving the equation $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$.

The solution is a **simple harmonic motion (SHM)**:
$$x(t) = X \cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$$

where:
- $X$ = amplitude of vibration
- $\omega_n$ = natural angular frequency = $\sqrt{k/m}$
- $\phi$ = phase angle (depends on initial conditions)

## 2.4 Damped Free Vibration ($c \neq 0$, $F(t) = 0$)

When damping is present, the equation becomes $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$.

The character of the motion depends on the **damping ratio**:
$$\zeta = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \frac{c}{c_c}$$

where $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the **critical damping coefficient**.

### Three Cases of Damping:

**Case 1: Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$, $c < c_c$)** — Most common in machinery.
The system oscillates with decaying amplitude. The damped natural frequency is:
$$\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$$

**Case 2: Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$, $c = c_c$)**
The system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without oscillating.

**Case 3: Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$, $c > c_c$)**
The system returns to equilibrium slowly without oscillating.

## 2.5 Forced Vibration

When a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

The **steady-state solution** (after transients die out) is:
$$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

The amplitude of forced vibration is:
$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

The **Magnification Factor (MF)** — a critical KTU exam concept:
$$MF = \frac{X}{\delta_{static}} = \frac{X}{(F_0/k)} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\left[1 - \left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_n}\right)^2\right]^2 + \left(2\zeta\frac{\omega}{\omega_n}\right)^2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Resonance Danger:** When the forcing frequency $\omega$ approaches the natural frequency $\omega_n$, the amplitude grows enormously. In undamped systems ($c = 0$), the amplitude becomes **infinite** at $\omega = \omega_n$. Real machinery must avoid operating at resonance.

## 2.6 Logarithmic Decrement

A direct experimental method to measure the damping ratio. If $x_1$ and $x_2$ are successive peak amplitudes (one period apart):
$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{2\pi \zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

For lightly damped systems ($\zeta \ll 1$): $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> The following table summarizes every key formula needed for the KTU exam. **Memorize these — they appear in 90% of numerical questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Natural angular frequency (translation) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | **rad/s** |
| 2 | Natural frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | **Hz** |
| 3 | Time period | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | **seconds** |
| 4 | Natural frequency (torsional) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_t}{I}}$ | $I$ = mass moment of inertia, $k_t$ = torsional stiffness |
| 5 | Natural frequency (compound pendulum) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{mgl}{I_O}}$ | $l$ = distance from pivot to CG |
| 6 | Natural frequency (simple pendulum) | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{l}}$ | Small oscillations |
| 7 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Critical damping coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | **N·s/m** |
| 9 | Damped natural frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | **rad/s** |
| 10 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = \ln\left(\dfrac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | For successive peaks |
| 11 | Forced vibration amplitude | $X = \dfrac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$ | Steady state |
| 12 | Magnification factor | $MF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{[1-r^2]^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ where $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | Phase angle (forced) | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}\right)$ | **radians** |
| 14 | Transmissibility | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{[1-r^2]^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | For base excitation |
| 15 | Equation of motion (general) | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | SDOF |
| 16 | Static deflection (axial spring) | $\delta_{st} = \dfrac{W}{k} = \dfrac{mg}{k}$ | For spring-mass hanging vertically |
| 17 | Spring stiffness (axial) | $k = \dfrac{AE}{L}$ | $A$ = area, $E$ = Young's modulus, $L$ = length |
| 18 | Shaft torsional stiffness | $k_t = \dfrac{GJ}{L}$ | $G$ = shear modulus, $J$ = polar moment |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automobile Suspension:** The "spring" is the suspension coil, the "damper" is the shock absorber, and the "mass" is the car body. Engineers tune $\omega_n$ to be far from the engine firing frequency and road excitation frequency.
- **Earthquake Engineering:** Buildings must be designed so their natural frequency does not match dominant earthquake frequencies. Tuned Mass Dampers (TMDs) are added in skyscrapers (e.g., Taipei 101) to absorb vibrational energy.
- **Turbomachinery:** Rotating shafts operate at $\omega < 0.5\omega_n$ or $\omega > 1.4\omega_n$ to safely avoid resonance.
- **Musical Instruments:** Strings, air columns, and membranes vibrate at well-defined natural frequencies that produce musical notes.
- **Instrumentation:** Seismographs, accelerometers, and many sensors exploit the resonance behavior of SDOF systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Equation of Motion of SDOF Undamped System

### Given:
A mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$, free to move horizontally on a frictionless surface.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram
Consider the mass at a displacement $x$ from its equilibrium position. The only horizontal force on the mass is the spring force.

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law
By Hooke's Law, the spring exerts a restoring force directed opposite to the displacement:
$$F_s = -kx$$

By Newton's Second Law ($F = ma$):
$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

### Step 3: Rearrange
$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

### Step 4: Assume a Solution
Let us try $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t) + B\sin(\omega t)$ (a general harmonic trial solution). Then:
$$\dot{x} = -A\omega\sin(\omega t) + B\omega\cos(\omega t)$$
$$\ddot{x} = -A\omega^2\cos(\omega t) - B\omega^2\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 5: Substitute
$$m\left(-A\omega^2\cos\omega t - B\omega^2\sin\omega t\right) + k\left(A\cos\omega t + B\sin\omega t\right) = 0$$

### Step 6: Collect Coefficients
$$\left(k - m\omega^2\right)A\cos\omega t + \left(k - m\omega^2\right)B\sin\omega t = 0$$

For this to hold for all $t$:
$$k - m\omega^2 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

### Step 7: Result — Natural Frequency

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The natural frequency depends **only** on the system parameters (mass and stiffness) — not on the initial conditions. Heavier systems oscillate more slowly; stiffer systems oscillate faster.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Damped Free Vibration Solution

### Given:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

### Step 1: Form the Characteristic Equation
Assume $x = e^{st}$ (an exponential trial solution):
$$ms^2 e^{st} + cs e^{st} + k e^{st} = 0$$
$$ms^2 + cs + k = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve the Quadratic
Using the quadratic formula:
$$s_{1,2} = \frac{-c \pm \sqrt{c^2 - 4mk}}{2m}$$

### Step 3: Recognize the Damping Ratio
The expression under the square root determines the type of motion:

$$\Delta = c^2 - 4mk = (2m\omega_n)^2\left(\zeta^2 - 1\right)$$

We define:
- $c_c = 2\sqrt{mk} = 2m\omega_n$
- $\zeta = c/c_c$

So the roots become:
$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

### Step 4: Underdamped Case ($\zeta < 1$)
Let $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. Then:
$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm i\omega_d$$

### Step 5: General Solution (Underdamped)
$$x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[C_1 \cos(\omega_d t) + C_2 \sin(\omega_d t)\right]$$

Or equivalently:
$$x(t) = X\,e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

where $X$ and $\phi$ are determined by initial conditions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Logarithmic Decrement

### Given:
Underdamped motion: $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$

### Step 1: Successive Peaks
Peaks occur approximately when $\cos(\omega_d t - \phi) = 1$. Let two successive peaks occur at $t_1$ and $t_2 = t_1 + T_d$ where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$.

### Step 2: Compute the Ratio
$$\frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}}{X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_1 + T_d)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

### Step 3: Take Natural Log
$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$$

### Step 4: Substitute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

For small damping ($\zeta < 0.2$): $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Forced Vibration Steady-State Amplitude

### Given:
$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 1: Assume Particular Solution
Try $x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

Then:
$$\dot{x}_p = X\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$
$$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 2: Substitute into Equation
$$m\left(-X\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \phi)\right) + c\left(X\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi)\right) + k\left(X\sin(\omega t - \phi)\right) = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Expand Using Trig Identities
Using $\sin(A - B) = \sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B$ and similarly for cosine, we collect coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$.

Equating coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$:
$$X(k - m\omega^2)\cos\phi + X(c\omega)\sin\phi = F_0$$
$$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin\phi - X(c\omega)\cos\phi = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\phi$
From the second equation:
$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}$$

### Step 5: Solve for X
Square and add both equations:
$$X^2\left[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2\right] = F_0^2$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}}$$

### Step 6: Magnification Factor Form
Dividing numerator and denominator by $k$, with $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\zeta = c/(2m\omega_n)$:

$$MF = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{[1 - r^2]^2 + [2\zeta r]^2}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Natural Frequency of a Vertical Spring-Mass System

### Given:
A mass $m$ hanging from a vertical spring of stiffness $k$. The system is in static equilibrium first, then displaced.

### Step 1: Static Equilibrium
At equilibrium, the spring stretches by $\delta_{st}$ such that:
$$k\delta_{st} = mg \quad\Rightarrow\quad \delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k}$$

### Step 2: Equation of Motion
Let $x$ be the displacement measured from the **equilibrium position** (not from the natural length). Applying Newton's second law:
$$m\ddot{x} = -k(x + \delta_{st}) + mg = -kx - k\delta_{st} + mg = -kx$$

(The gravity and static spring force cancel because we measure from equilibrium!)

### Step 3: Same Equation as Horizontal!
$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Whether a spring-mass system is horizontal or vertical, the **natural frequency is identical** as long as we measure displacement from the static equilibrium position. This is a very important KTU exam point.

$$\boxed{\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \quad \text{(independent of gravity)}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — SDOF Vibration System

<!-- This flowchart uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (systemA, massB, etc.) and clean uppercase labels. -->

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    systemA["VIBRATION SYSTEM SDOF"]:::mainNode
    massB["MASS m KILOGRAM"]:::mass
    springC["SPRING STIFFNESS k NEWTON PER METER"]:::spring

    forceE["EXTERNAL FORCE F OF t NEWTON"]:::force

    springC --- groundF
    massB --- damperD
    damperD --- groundF

    systemA --- forceE
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Forced Vibration Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    case2["CASE zeta equals 1 CRITICALLY DAMPED"]:::branch
    case3["CASE zeta greater than 1 OVERDAMPED"]:::branch

    xforced["FORCED RESPONSE X = F0 divided by sqrt k minus m omega squared squared plus c omega squared"]:::output
    mfcalc["MAGNIFICATION FACTOR MF = 1 divided by sqrt 1 minus r squared squared plus 2 zeta r squared"]:::compute

    checkB --> case1
    checkB --> case2
    checkB --> case3
    case1 --> omegaD
    case1 --> xfree
    case1 --> xforced
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb for safe machine operation:**
> - **Operating frequency $< 0.5 \omega_n$:** Safe (quasi-static).
> - **Operating frequency $\approx \omega_n$:** **DANGEROUS — RESONANCE.**
> - **Operating frequency $> \sqrt{2}\,\omega_n$:** Isolation region — vibrations are attenuated.
