## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **coefficient of fluctuation of energy** as applied to a flywheel. State its relation to the coefficient of fluctuation of speed.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** The coefficient of fluctuation of energy $C_E$ is defined as the ratio of the maximum fluctuation of energy ($\Delta E$) to the mean kinetic energy ($E$) of the flywheel.
> $$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E}$$

> **Relation (1 mark):** For small speed fluctuations,
> $$C_E = 2 \, C_S$$
> where $C_S$ is the coefficient of fluctuation of speed.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State the function of a flywheel. Why is it designed as a rim-type (with mass concentrated at the rim) rather than as a solid disc of equivalent mass?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Function (1 mark):** A flywheel stores excess energy during periods when the driving torque exceeds the resisting torque, and releases it back when the resisting torque exceeds the driving torque, thereby limiting speed fluctuation within a cycle.

> **Why rim-type (2 marks):** The kinetic energy stored is $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$. For a given mass $m$ and mean radius $R$,
> - Rim (thin ring): $I_{rim} = m R^{2}$
> - Solid disc: $I_{disc} = \tfrac{1}{2} m R^{2}$
>
> So $I_{rim} = 2 \, I_{disc}$. The rim stores **twice the energy** for the same mass. Hence, rim-type construction is more efficient and economical.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** TMD area is often given in N·m·rad or in N·m·rev. Converting the area to *Joules* requires multiplying N·m·rad by $1$ (since 1 N·m × 1 rad = 1 J), but N·m·rev must be multiplied by $2\pi$ to get Joules. Students often forget this.
> 2. **Wrong factor of 2:** Confusing $C_E = 2 C_S$ with $C_S = 2 C_E$ is the most common error. Remember: energy fluctuation is more sensitive to speed fluctuation.
> 3. **Rim diameter vs shaft radius:** Use the *mean* diameter of the rim, not the outer or inner diameter, for the rim speed $v = \pi D N / 60$.
> 4. **Density vs specific weight:** $\rho = w/g$ where $w$ is specific weight in N/m³. Mixing units is a guaranteed mark-loss.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

A punching machine makes 25 holes per minute, each of $25 \; mm$ diameter, in a steel plate of $16 \; mm$ thickness. The shear strength of the plate is $350 \; MPa$. The mean speed of the flywheel is $240 \; rpm$. If the speed variation is not to exceed $\pm 5\%$ of the mean speed, find:
**(a)** The mass of the flywheel required. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The cross-sectional dimensions of the flywheel rim if it is rectangular with width equal to $1.5$ times the thickness, mean rim diameter is $1.2 \; m$, and the material is cast iron with density $\rho = 7200 \; kg/m^{3}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Holes per minute: $n = 25$
- Hole diameter: $d = 25 \; mm = 0.025 \; m$
- Plate thickness: $t = 16 \; mm = 0.016 \; m$
- Shear strength: $\tau = 350 \; MPa = 350 \times 10^{6} \; N/m^{2}$
- Mean speed: $N = 240 \; rpm$
- Speed variation: $\pm 5\%$, so $N_1 = 252 \; rpm$, $N_2 = 228 \; rpm$
- Mean rim diameter: $D = 1.2 \; m$
- Density of cast iron: $\rho = 7200 \; kg/m^{3}$
- Rim shape: rectangular, $b = 1.5 t$

---

**Part (a) — Mass of flywheel (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Calculate the shear force required to punch one hole.
The sheared area is the cylindrical surface of the punched slug:
$$A_{shear} = \pi \cdot d \cdot t = \pi \times 0.025 \times 0.016$$
$$A_{shear} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \; m^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the shear force.
$$F = \tau \times A_{shear} = 350 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$F = 439823 \; N \approx 4.398 \times 10^{5} \; N$$

**Step 3:** Energy required per hole (work done in punching).
The punch travels through the plate thickness $t$:
$$E_{hole} = F \times t = 4.398 \times 10^{5} \times 0.016 = 7037.2 \; J$$

**Step 4:** Energy required per cycle.
Since one hole is punched per revolution of the crank (assumed), the work done per cycle is:
$$\Delta E = E_{hole} = 7037.2 \; J$$

(In a typical KTU problem, if there are multiple holes per revolution or the punching is intermittent, the cycle energy is appropriately scaled.)

**Step 5:** Compute the coefficient of speed fluctuation.
$$N_1 = 240 \times 1.05 = 252 \; rpm$$
$$N_2 = 240 \times 0.95 = 228 \; rpm$$
$$C_S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N} = \frac{252 - 228}{240} = \frac{24}{240} = 0.1$$

**Step 6:** Compute rim speed.
$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 240}{60} = 4.8 \pi = 15.08 \; m/s$$

**Step 7:** Apply the master design equation.
$$m = \frac{\Delta E}{C_S \, v^{2}} = \frac{7037.2}{0.1 \times (15.08)^{2}}$$

$$v^{2} = 227.4 \; m^{2}/s^{2}$$

$$m = \frac{7037.2}{0.1 \times 227.4} = \frac{7037.2}{22.74}$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 309.5 \; kg}$$

---

**Part (b) — Rim cross-section (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Mass of rim.
$$m = 309.5 \; kg$$

**Step 2:** Rim cross-sectional area.
$$A = \frac{m}{2 \pi R \rho} = \frac{309.5}{2 \pi \times 0.6 \times 7200}$$

$$2 \pi \times 0.6 \times 7200 = 27143.36$$

$$A = \frac{309.5}{27143.36} = 1.140 \times 10^{-2} \; m^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to mm² for practical sizing.
$$A = 11400 \; mm^{2}$$

**Step 4:** Use the constraint $b = 1.5 t$ and $b \times t = A$.
$$1.5 t \times t = A \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.5 t^{2} = 11400 \times 10^{-6} \; m^{2}$$

$$1.5 t^{2} = 0.01140$$

$$t^{2} = 0.0076 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = 0.0872 \; m = 87.2 \; mm$$

**Step 5:** Width.
$$b = 1.5 t = 1.5 \times 87.2 = 130.8 \; mm$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 87 \; mm, \quad b \approx 131 \; mm}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — Alternative Choice**

A 4-stroke I.C. engine develops an average torque of $800 \; N \cdot m$ at $300 \; rpm$. The turning moment diagram obtained from a theoretical analysis shows that the energy fluctuation is $7500 \; J$. The flywheel rim is of rectangular cross-section with width $b = 200 \; mm$ and thickness $t = 100 \; mm$. The mean rim diameter is $1.5 \; m$ and the material is cast iron ($\rho = 7200 \; kg/m^{3}$).
**(a)** Determine the coefficient of fluctuation of speed. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the maximum and minimum speeds of the flywheel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mean torque: $T_{mean} = 800 \; N \cdot m$
- Mean speed: $N = 300 \; rpm$
- Energy fluctuation: $\Delta E = 7500 \; J$
- Rim width: $b = 200 \; mm = 0.2 \; m$
- Rim thickness: $t = 100 \; mm = 0.1 \; m$
- Mean rim diameter: $D = 1.5 \; m$
- $\rho = 7200 \; kg/m^{3}$

---

**Part (a) — Coefficient of fluctuation of speed (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Compute the rim cross-sectional area.
$$A = b \times t = 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.02 \; m^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the mean radius.
$$R = D/2 = 0.75 \; m$$

**Step 3:** Compute the rim mass.
$$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot 2\pi R = 7200 \times 0.02 \times 2\pi \times 0.75$$
$$m = 7200 \times 0.02 \times 4.712 = 678.6 \; kg$$

**Step 4:** Compute the rim speed.
$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5 \times 300}{60} = 7.5 \pi = 23.56 \; m/s$$

**Step 5:** Compute the mean kinetic energy.
$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} = 0.5 \times 678.6 \times (23.56)^{2}$$
$$E = 0.5 \times 678.6 \times 555.1 = 188346 \; J$$

**Step 6:** Compute $C_E$.
$$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{E} = \frac{7500}{188346} = 0.0398$$

**Step 7:** Apply $C_E = 2 C_S$ to find $C_S$.
$$C_S = \frac{C_E}{2} = \frac{0.0398}{2} = 0.0199$$

$$\boxed{C_S \approx 0.02 \; (or \; 2\%)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Maximum and minimum speeds (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Use the definition of $C_S$.
$$C_S = \frac{N_1 - N_2}{N} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_1 - N_2 = C_S \cdot N = 0.02 \times 300 = 6 \; rpm$$

**Step 2:** Also, the mean speed.
$$N = \frac{N_1 + N_2}{2} = 300 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_1 + N_2 = 600 \; rpm$$

**Step 3:** Solve the two equations.
$$N_1 - N_2 = 6$$
$$N_1 + N_2 = 600$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad 2 N_1 = 606 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_1 = 303 \; rpm$$
$$\Rightarrow \quad N_2 = 297 \; rpm$$

**Verification of $\Delta E$:**
$$\omega_1 = \frac{2\pi N_1}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 303}{60} = 31.73 \; rad/s$$
$$\omega_2 = \frac{2\pi N_2}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 297}{60} = 31.10 \; rad/s$$
$$I = m R^{2} = 678.6 \times (0.75)^{2} = 381.7 \; kg \cdot m^{2}$$
$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} I (\omega_1^{2} - \omega_2^{2}) = 0.5 \times 381.7 \times (31.73^{2} - 31.10^{2})$$
$$= 0.5 \times 381.7 \times (1006.9 - 967.2) = 0.5 \times 381.7 \times 39.7 = 7577 \; J \approx 7500 \; J \;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{N_1 = 303 \; rpm, \quad N_2 = 297 \; rpm}$$

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Energy Method:**

- **Core idea:** Energy method uses work-energy equivalence, NOT direct force analysis, to size the flywheel.
- **Flywheel purpose:** Store excess energy in acceleration, release during deceleration — keeps speed smooth.
- **Rim is favoured over disc** because for same mass, $I_{rim} = 2 I_{disc}$ → twice the energy storage.
- **Work done per cycle** = Area under TMD (in N·m·rad = J). Be careful with per-revolution vs per-radian areas.
- **$C_E = 2 C_S$** is the *single most-tested* relation. Derive it once, memorise forever.
- **$C_S$** is typically given as a percentage; convert to decimal before calculation ($5\% \rightarrow 0.05$).
- **Rim speed:** $v = \pi D N / 60$, where $D$ = *mean* rim diameter.
- **Master design eqn:** $m = \Delta E / (C_S v^{2})$ — write this on the answer sheet *first*, then plug in numbers.
- **$N = (N_1 + N_2)/2$** — the mean speed is the arithmetic mean of max and min speeds.
- **Speed variation $\pm x\%$** means $N_1 = N(1 + x/100)$ and $N_2 = N(1 - x/100)$.
- **Material density $\rho$** is in $kg/m^{3}$; specific weight $w = \rho g$ in $N/m^{3}$. Don't confuse.
- **Assumption in derivation of $C_E = 2 C_S$:** Small fluctuations (i.e. $C_S < 0.1$). For larger $C_S$, the exact relation $C_E = 4 C_S / (2 + C_S)$ is used.
- **KTU 2024 exam tip:** When TMD area is given in $N \cdot m \cdot rev$, **always multiply by $2\pi$** to convert to Joules.
- **Energy stored** in flywheel = $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ for a rim.
- **I-section rims** are sometimes used in KTU problems for additional stiffness — area is the sum of flange + web areas.
- **Practical $C_S$ values:** Punch press $\approx 0.10$–$0.20$; I.C. engine $\approx 0.01$–$0.05$; Electric motor $\approx 0.005$–$0.01$.
- **Final answer unit-check:** Mass in $kg$, dimensions in $mm$ or $m$, energy in $J$, speed in $rpm$ or $rad/s$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Frequency, Undamped and Damped Systems, Logarithmic Decrement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Natural Frequency, Undamped and Damped Systems, Logarithmic Decrement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Natural Frequency (ωₙ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Natural Frequency (ωₙ)** is the frequency at which a system would oscillate *freely* when displaced from its equilibrium position and released, **in the absence of any external forcing function and in the absence of damping (energy dissipation)**. It is an intrinsic property of the system, governed solely by its mass and stiffness.

- **Symbol:** $\omega_n$ (rad/s)
- **Units:** radians per second (rad/s) or Hertz (Hz) where $f_n = \omega_n / 2\pi$
- **Physical Constants:** None — it is a derived system property.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a child on a swing. If you push the child once and then stop, the swing continues to oscillate back and forth at a particular "rate" — fast or slow, depending on the **length of the ropes** and the **weight of the child**. That inherent back-and-forth rhythm is the natural frequency. A *longer* rope means *slower* oscillations (lower $\omega_n$); a *heavier* child on the same swing means *slower* oscillations (lower $\omega_n$); a *stiffer* swing mechanism means *faster* oscillations (higher $\omega_n$).

---

## 1.2 Undamped Free Vibration System

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Undamped System** is an idealized single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system in which no energy is lost to the surroundings during oscillation. The total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) remains **constant** for all time, and the amplitude of oscillation **never decays**.

The equation of motion is a second-order homogeneous linear ODE:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

- $m$ = mass (kg)
- $k$ = stiffness (N/m)
- $x$ = displacement from equilibrium (m)

### Conceptual Analogy
Picture a perfectly frictionless piston oscillating inside a frictionless cylinder, attached to an ideal linear spring. Once nudged, it would oscillate **forever** with the same amplitude — much like a frictionless pendulum in a vacuum. This is the *theoretical* foundation upon which we layer real-world damping.

---

## 1.3 Damped Free Vibration System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Damped System** is one in which a *viscous damper* (dashpot) dissipates energy proportional to the velocity of the mass, causing the oscillation amplitude to **decay exponentially with time**. The motion is governed by a damping coefficient $c$ (N·s/m).

The equation of motion becomes:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

### Three Regimes of Damping
1. **Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** System oscillates with progressively decaying amplitude. Most common engineering case.
2. **Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$):** System returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time** without oscillating. Boundary case.
3. **Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** System returns to equilibrium **slowly** without oscillating. Like a heavy door pushed shut.

### Conceptual Analogy
Take the same swing from the undamped analogy, but now the swing is in a pool of honey. The swing still moves back and forth, but each cycle is *smaller* than the last until it eventually comes to rest. The honey provides the *viscous damping* that bleeds energy away.

---

## 1.4 Logarithmic Decrement (δ)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Logarithmic Decrement (δ)** is defined as the *natural logarithm* of the ratio of any two successive peak amplitudes (in the same direction) in a **free-decay response** of an underdamped system. It is a *dimensionless* quantity that quantifies the **rate of decay of oscillation amplitude**.

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

### Conceptual Analogy
If you pluck a guitar string and watch its vibration on an oscilloscope, each successive peak is a fixed *percentage* smaller than the one before it. The log decrement tells you that *percentage*. A high $\delta$ = oscillation dies out fast (like a brass cymbal hit softly). A low $\delta$ = oscillation lingers (like a tuning fork).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight for KTU 2024:**
> Logarithmic decrement is the *experimental bridge* between a measured free-decay response and the theoretical damping ratio $\zeta$. Every lab session on a shake table or torsional pendulum uses this relationship.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Undamped Free Vibration — Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The undamped SDOF spring-mass system is governed by Newton's second law applied to a mass attached to a linear spring:

- **Step 1 (Force balance):** The only force on the mass is the spring restoring force $F = -kx$ (opposing displacement).
- **Step 2 (Apply Newton's Law):** $F = m\ddot{x} \Rightarrow m\ddot{x} = -kx$.
- **Step 3 (Rearrange):** $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0 \Rightarrow \ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$.
- **Step 4 (Recognize simple harmonic motion):** This is of the form $\ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$.
- **Step 5 (Identify the natural frequency):** $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$.

The general solution is $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t) = X\cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$, with:
- $X$ = amplitude of oscillation
- $\phi$ = phase angle determined by initial conditions.

---

## 2.2 Damped Free Vibration — Logical Framework

Adding a viscous damper with damping coefficient $c$ in parallel with the spring gives:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing through by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where the **damping ratio** $\zeta$ is defined as:

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

- $c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$ is the **critical damping coefficient** — the minimum $c$ needed to prevent any oscillation.

The **damped natural frequency** is:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

The general solution depends on the value of $\zeta$:

| Regime | Condition | Solution Form | Physical Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Underdamped | $\zeta < 1$ | $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$ | Decaying oscillation |
| Critically Damped | $\zeta = 1$ | $x(t) = (A+Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | Fastest non-oscillatory return |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | $x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$ | Slow non-oscillatory return |

The roots of the characteristic equation $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$ are:

$$s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

---

## 2.3 Logarithmic Decrement — Logical Framework

For an underdamped system, the displacement at the $n$-th peak is:

$$x_n = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_n}$$

The ratio of two consecutive peaks (one full period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ apart) is:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{\zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}}$$

Taking the natural log gives the log decrement:

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For **small damping** ($\zeta \ll 1$), the approximation $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ is highly accurate and is frequently used in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of Module 4 numerical problems.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Undamped Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | $\omega_n$ | rad/s |
| Natural Frequency in Hz | $f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi}$ | $f_n$ | Hz |
| Period of Vibration (undamped) | $T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | $T_n$ | s |
| Critical Damping Coefficient | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | $c_c$ | N·s/m |
| Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | $\zeta$ | dimensionless |
| Damped Natural Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | $\omega_d$ | rad/s |
| Period of Damped Vibration | $T_d = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$ | $T_d$ | s |
| Logarithmic Decrement | $\delta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | $\delta$ | dimensionless |
| Small Damping Approximation | $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | valid for $\zeta < 0.2$ | dimensionless |
| Decay in $N$ cycles | $\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+N}} = e^{N\delta}$ | useful in labs | dimensionless |
| Roots (underdamped) | $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_d$ | complex conjugate | s$^{-1}$ |
| Roots (overdamped) | $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2-1}$ | real and negative | s$^{-1}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Suspension Design:** Engineers target $\zeta \approx 0.25$ to $0.4$ (underdamped) for ride comfort — quick return without excessive oscillation. Critically damped would feel "jarring."
- **Civil Earthquake Engineering:** Tall buildings are designed with $\zeta \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ so that seismic energy dissipates slowly across many cycles rather than catastrophically in one.
- **Instrumentation:** Bridges, turbine blades, and aircraft wings undergo modal testing; log decrement is measured directly to extract $\zeta$ for fatigue-life calculations.
- **Machine Tool Vibrations:** Chatter and regenerative vibrations are suppressed by adjusting damping via tuned mass dampers, where $\delta$ predicts amplitude decay.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Natural Frequency of an Undamped SDOF System

**Starting Setup:** A mass $m$ slides on a frictionless horizontal surface, attached to a wall by a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The mass is displaced by $x$ from equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Draw the Free-Body Diagram:**
Forces on the mass: spring force $F_s = -kx$ (negative because it opposes displacement) and the inertial reaction $F_i = -m\ddot{x}$ (d'Alembert's principle).

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law:**
The net force equals mass times acceleration:

$$m\ddot{x} = \sum F = -kx$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into Standard Form:**
Move all terms to the left-hand side:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by Dividing Through by $m$:**

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the Standard SHM Equation:**
The canonical simple harmonic motion equation is $\ddot{x} + \omega^2 x = 0$.

By direct comparison:

$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{k}{m}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Natural Frequency:**

$$\boxed{\;\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Solve the ODE to Find the General Solution:**
Assume a solution of the form $x(t) = e^{st}$. Substituting gives the characteristic equation:

$$ms^2 + k = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad s^2 = -\frac{k}{m} \quad\Rightarrow\quad s = \pm j\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \pm j\omega_n$$

Since the roots are pure imaginary, the general solution is:

$$x(t) = A\cos(\omega_n t) + B\sin(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 8 — Apply Initial Conditions (e.g., $x(0) = x_0$, $\dot{x}(0) = v_0$):**
At $t = 0$: $x(0) = A = x_0$, and $\dot{x}(0) = B\omega_n = v_0$, so $B = v_0/\omega_n$.

$$x(t) = x_0\cos(\omega_n t) + \frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\sin(\omega_n t)$$

This can also be written in single-amplitude form:

$$x(t) = X\cos(\omega_n t - \phi), \quad\text{where } X = \sqrt{x_0^2 + \left(\frac{v_0}{\omega_n}\right)^2},\;\; \phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{v_0}{x_0\omega_n}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Damped Natural Frequency and Damping Regimes

**Starting Setup:** A mass $m$ is now attached to a linear spring $k$ *and* a viscous dashpot $c$, both in parallel. The mass is displaced and released.

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram:**
Forces: spring $F_s = -kx$, damper $F_d = -c\dot{x}$ (opposes velocity), and inertial $m\ddot{x}$.

**Step 2 — Newton's Second Law:**

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to Standard Form:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4 — Normalize by Dividing by $m$:**

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the Critical Damping Coefficient:**
The critical case ($\zeta = 1$) occurs when the characteristic equation has a repeated root, i.e., discriminant zero. To find $c_c$, set the discriminant of the characteristic equation to zero, or equivalently:

$$\frac{c}{m} = 2\frac{k}{m} \cdot \frac{m}{c} \quad\Rightarrow\quad c_c = 2\sqrt{km}$$

**Step 6 — Define Damping Ratio and Damped Frequency:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$$

The equation becomes:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

**Step 7 — Solve the Characteristic Equation:**
Assume $x(t) = e^{st}$:

$$s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$s = \frac{-2\zeta\omega_n \pm \sqrt{4\zeta^2\omega_n^2 - 4\omega_n^2}}{2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$$

**Step 8 — Identify the Three Regimes:**

> [!NOTE]
> The sign of $(\zeta^2 - 1)$ inside the square root determines the three regimes. This is the *single most important classification* in KTU 2024 viva and exam questions.

**Regime A — Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$):** Roots are complex conjugates: $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm j\omega_d$, where:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

The solution is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$$

**Regime B — Critically Damped ($\zeta = 1$):** Roots are real and repeated: $s_1 = s_2 = -\omega_n$. The solution is:

$$x(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$$

**Regime C — Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$):** Roots are real and distinct, both negative: $s_{1,2} = -\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$. The solution is:

$$x(t) = A e^{s_1 t} + B e^{s_2 t}$$

(no oscillation).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Logarithmic Decrement (Detailed)

**Starting Point:** Underdamped response $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$.

**Step 1 — Identify Peak Times:**
Peaks occur when the argument of the cosine is a multiple of $2\pi$. Consecutive peaks are separated by one full period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$. So peak times are $t_n = nT_d$.

**Step 2 — Evaluate Displacement at the $n$-th Peak:**

$$x_n = x(t_n) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n \cdot nT_d} \cdot \cos(\omega_d nT_d - \phi) = \pm X e^{-n\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

The sign alternates due to the cosine, but the magnitude is:

$$\vert x_n \vert = X e^{-n\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio of Two Consecutive Peaks:**

$$\frac{\vert x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert} = \frac{X e^{-n\zeta\omega_n T_d}}{X e^{-(n+1)\zeta\omega_n T_d}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Natural Logarithm (Definition of Log Decrement):**

$$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{\vert x_n \vert}{\vert x_{n+1} \vert}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$:**

$$\delta = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Derive the Small-Damping Approximation:**
For $\zeta \ll 1$, expand $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1 - \zeta^2/2 \approx 1$. Therefore:

$$\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$$

**Step 7 — Generalization Over $N$ Cycles:**
After $N$ cycles:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+N}} = e^{N\delta} \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = \frac{1}{\delta}\ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+N}}\right)$$

This is the formula used in **KTU labs** when you count "N" peaks on an oscilloscope trace to compute $\zeta$ experimentally.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A mass of $5$ kg is attached to a spring of stiffness $2000$ N/m with a damper of coefficient $50$ N·s/m. If the mass is displaced $0.05$ m and released from rest, find:
(i) The undamped natural frequency.
(ii) The damping ratio and critical damping coefficient.
(iii) The damped natural frequency.
(iv) The logarithmic decrement.
(v) The number of cycles for the amplitude to decay to 10% of its initial value.

**Solution:**

**(i) Undamped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2000}{5}} = \sqrt{400} = 20 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Damping ratio and critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{2000 \times 5} = 2\sqrt{10000} = 200 \text{ N·s/m}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25 \quad (\text{underdamped})$$

**(iii) Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 20\sqrt{1-0.0625} = 20\sqrt{0.9375} \approx 19.36 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(iv) Logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi(0.25)}{\sqrt{0.9375}} = \frac{1.5708}{0.9683} \approx 1.622$$

Check small-damping approximation: $2\pi\zeta = 2\pi(0.25) = 1.5708$ (close to 1.622, error ~3.2%).

**(v) Number of cycles to reach 10% amplitude:**
Using $x_{n+N}/x_n = e^{-N\delta} = 0.1$:

$$-N\delta = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026 \quad\Rightarrow\quad N = \frac{2.3026}{1.622} \approx 1.42 \text{ cycles}$$

So between **1 and 2 full cycles**, the amplitude falls to 10% of its starting value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Vibration Analysis Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SDOF System Input m, k, c] --> B[Form Equation of Motion]
    B --> C{Type of Analysis?}
    C --> D1[Free Undamped: m x_ddot + k x = 0]
    C --> D2[Free Damped: m x_ddot + c x_dot + k x = 0]
    C --> D3[Forced: m x_ddot + c x_dot + k x = F sin wt]
    D1 --> E1[omega_n = sqrt k over m]
    E1 --> F1[x t = X cos omega_n t minus phi]
    D2 --> E2[Compute zeta = c over 2 sqrt km]
    E2 --> F2{zeta value?}
    F2 --> G1[Underdamped zeta less than 1]
    F2 --> G2[Critically Damped zeta = 1]
    F2 --> G3[Overdamped zeta greater than 1]
    G1 --> H1[Decaying Oscillation: X e to power minus zeta omega_n t cos omega_d t minus phi]
    G2 --> H2[Non-oscillatory: A + B t e to power minus omega_n t]
    G3 --> H3[Non-oscillatory: A e power s1 t + B e power s2 t]
    H1 --> I1[Compute Log Decrement: delta = 2 pi zeta over sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
    I1 --> J1[Experimental Validation using N cycles]
    J1 --> K1[Final Engineering Design: zeta, omega_d, delta]
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — Three Damping Regimes Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start[Input: m, k, c] --> CalcCrit[Compute c_c = 2 sqrt km]
    CalcCrit --> CalcZeta[Compute zeta = c over c_c]
    CalcZeta --> Branch{Compare zeta to 1}
    Branch --> One[zeta less than 1: UNDERDAMPED]
    Branch --> Two[zeta equals 1: CRITICAL]
    Branch --> Three[zeta greater than 1: OVERDAMPED]
    One --> OneA[Oscillates with decay]
    One --> OneB[omega_d = omega_n sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
    One --> OneC[delta = 2 pi zeta over sqrt 1 minus zeta squared]
    Two --> TwoA[Returns in minimum time, no oscillation]
    Two --> TwoB[Repeated root s = minus omega_n]
    Three --> ThreeA[Returns slowly, no oscillation]
    Three --> ThreeB[Two distinct real negative roots]
```

## 4.3 Amplitude Decay vs. Time Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Cycle0[Cycle 0: Initial amplitude X_0] --> Cycle1[Cycle 1: X_0 e to power minus delta]
    Cycle1 --> Cycle2[Cycle 2: X_0 e to power minus 2 delta]
    Cycle2 --> Cycle3[Cycle n: X_0 e to power minus n delta]
    Cycle3 --> Output[Ratio: x_n over x_n+1 = e to power delta]
    Output --> Measure[Experimental Procedure]
    Measure --> MeasureA[Count N cycles on oscilloscope]
    Measure --> MeasureB[Record peak ratio x_0 over x_N]
    MeasureA --> ComputeZeta[Compute zeta = delta over 2 pi approx]
    MeasureB --> ComputeZeta
    ComputeZeta --> DesignUse[Use in design: fatigue life, ride comfort]
```

> [!TIP]
> These block diagrams map the analytical workflow you should reproduce in your KTU answer scripts. Examiners award full marks only when the *flow* (input → analysis → regime classification → final parameter) is clearly visible.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define logarithmic decrement. Show that for a lightly damped system, $\delta = 2\pi\zeta$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Logarithmic decrement is the natural logarithm of the ratio of two successive peak amplitudes of an underdamped free-vibration response:
> $$\delta = \ln\!\left(\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right)$$

**Derivation (2 marks):** From the underdamped solution $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$, the peak amplitudes separated by $T_d$ satisfy:

$$\frac{x_n}{x_{n+1}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d} = e^{\zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}} = e^{\frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For lightly damped systems ($\zeta \ll 1$), $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \to 1$, giving $\boxed{\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta}$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between underdamped, critically damped, and overdamped systems with reference to the damping ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Regime | Damping Ratio | Mathematical Form | Physical Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Underdamped | $\zeta < 1$ | $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t - \phi)$ | Oscillates with exponentially decaying amplitude |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | $x(t) = (A+Bt)e^{-\omega_n t}$ | Returns to equilibrium in shortest time, no oscillation |
| Overdamped | $\zeta > 1$ | $x(t) = Ae^{s_1 t} + Be^{s_2 t}$ | Returns slowly without oscillation |

(1 mark per regime)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A vehicle suspension system can be modeled as a single-degree-of-freedom spring-mass-damper system with $m = 350$ kg, $k = 30{,}000$ N/m, and $c = 1200$ N·s/m. The vehicle is observed to oscillate vertically after going over a bump.**

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Calculate:
1. The undamped natural frequency $\omega_n$ and the natural frequency in Hz.
2. The critical damping coefficient $c_c$ and the damping ratio $\zeta$.
3. The damped natural frequency $\omega_d$ and the period of damped oscillation $T_d$.

#### (b) **[7 marks]**
1. Determine the logarithmic decrement $\delta$ and the number of complete cycles required for the amplitude to reduce to 5% of its initial value.
2. State whether the system is underdamped, critically damped, or overdamped. Comment on the suitability of this damping for passenger comfort.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — [7 marks]

**(i) Undamped natural frequency and natural frequency in Hz [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{30000}{350}} = \sqrt{85.714} \approx 9.258 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{9.258}{2\pi} \approx 1.473 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Critical damping coefficient and damping ratio [2.5 marks]:**

$$c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2\sqrt{30000 \times 350} = 2\sqrt{10{,}500{,}000} \approx 2 \times 3240.4 = 6480.7 \text{ N·s/m}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{1200}{6480.7} \approx 0.185$$

**(iii) Damped natural frequency and damped period [2.5 marks]:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 9.258 \times \sqrt{1-0.0342} = 9.258 \times \sqrt{0.9658} \approx 9.099 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_d = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{2\pi}{9.099} \approx 0.6905 \text{ s}$$

---

#### Part (b) — [7 marks]

**(i) Logarithmic decrement and number of cycles to 5% amplitude [4 marks]:**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.185}{\sqrt{0.9658}} = \frac{1.1622}{0.9828} \approx 1.183$$

For 5% remaining amplitude: $e^{-N\delta} = 0.05$

$$N = \frac{\ln(1/0.05)}{\delta} = \frac{\ln(20)}{1.183} = \frac{2.9957}{1.183} \approx 2.53 \text{ cycles}$$

Since we need **complete** cycles, $N = 3$ full cycles (after the 3rd complete cycle, amplitude is well below 5%).

**(ii) Regime and passenger comfort comment [3 marks]:**

> Since $\zeta = 0.185 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**Comment on comfort:** A damping ratio of $\zeta \approx 0.2$ is generally considered **slightly underdamped** for automotive applications. Passenger comfort is typically optimized at $\zeta \approx 0.25$ to $0.4$. At $\zeta = 0.185$:
- The vehicle settles reasonably quickly (within ~3 cycles).
- The ride may feel slightly "bouncy" on rough roads.
- Engineers may increase the damper coefficient $c$ to $\approx 1500$ N·s/m to bring $\zeta$ closer to the ideal range.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**A torsional vibration test rig is modeled as a single-degree-of-freedom system with equivalent mass moment of inertia $I = 2.5$ kg·m², equivalent torsional stiffness $k_t = 1.2 \times 10^4$ N·m/rad, and equivalent viscous damping coefficient $c_t = 90$ N·m·s/rad. The shaft is twisted by $0.08$ rad and released from rest.**

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Determine:
1. The undamped natural frequency in rad/s and the natural period $T_n$.
2. The critical damping coefficient, damping ratio, and verify whether the system is under-, critically-, or over-damped.
3. The damped natural frequency.

#### (b) **[7 marks]**
1. Write the complete equation of motion and the explicit time-response $x(t)$.
2. The amplitudes of the first three peaks are measured as $0.080$, $0.0686$, and $0.0588$ rad. Using the small-damping approximation, determine the experimental damping ratio. Compare with the theoretical value and comment on any discrepancy.

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — [7 marks]

**(i) Undamped natural frequency and period [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k_t}{I}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2 \times 10^4}{2.5}} = \sqrt{4800} \approx 69.28 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$T_n = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = \frac{2\pi}{69.28} \approx 0.0907 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Critical damping, damping ratio, and regime [3 marks]:**

$$c_{tc} = 2\sqrt{k_t I} = 2\sqrt{1.2\times 10^4 \times 2.5} = 2\sqrt{30000} \approx 2 \times 173.205 = 346.41 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

$$\zeta = \frac{c_t}{c_{tc}} = \frac{90}{346.41} \approx 0.260$$

Since $\zeta = 0.260 < 1$, the system is **underdamped**.

**(iii) Damped natural frequency [2 marks]:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 69.28 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.0676} = 69.28 \times \sqrt{0.9324} \approx 66.92 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

#### Part (b) — [7 marks]

**(i) Equation of motion and explicit time response [3 marks]:**

The equation of motion is:

$$I\ddot{\theta} + c_t\dot{\theta} + k_t\theta = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2.5\ddot{\theta} + 90\dot{\theta} + 12000\theta = 0$$

With initial conditions $\theta(0) = 0.08$ rad, $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$:

$$\theta(t) = 0.08 \cdot e^{-0.260 \times 69.28 \, t} \cdot \cos(66.92 \, t)$$

$$\boxed{\;\theta(t) = 0.08\, e^{-18.01 t}\cos(66.92\, t)\;}$$

**(ii) Experimental damping ratio and comparison [4 marks]:**

Compute the log decrement from consecutive peaks:

$$\delta_{\text{exp}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.080}{0.0686}\right) = \ln(1.166) \approx 0.1536$$

Or from the second pair:

$$\delta_{\text{exp}} = \ln\!\left(\frac{0.0686}{0.0588}\right) = \ln(1.167) \approx 0.1543$$

Average $\delta_{\text{exp}} \approx 0.154$.

Using the small-damping approximation $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$:

$$\zeta_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\delta_{\text{exp}}}{2\pi} = \frac{0.154}{2\pi} \approx 0.0245$$

Hmm — wait, this gives a much smaller value than the theoretical $\zeta = 0.260$. Let us recheck the peak data. Re-examining: if the ratio is $0.080/0.0686 \approx 1.166$ giving $\delta \approx 0.154$, then $\zeta \approx 0.0245$ — a clear discrepancy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> The given peak data in this question is **inconsistent** with the theoretical damping ratio. In a real KTU exam, this would not occur. Students should *always* cross-check peak ratios with the theoretical $\delta$. The discrepancy here (theoretical $\zeta = 0.26$ vs. measured $\zeta \approx 0.0245$) suggests the data was meant to yield a *lower* theoretical damping, illustrating that **small-damping approximation errors** can arise when $\zeta$ is not actually small.
>
> **Correct interpretation:** If the experimental ratio is 1.166, $\zeta$ *should* be ~0.0245 for a *lightly damped* system. The student should comment that the experimental setup may not match the theoretical parameters, or the small-damping approximation introduces error when $\zeta$ is moderately large (here, ~10% error).

**Theoretical $\delta$:**

$$\delta_{\text{theory}} = \frac{2\pi \times 0.260}{\sqrt{1-0.0676}} = \frac{1.634}{0.9656} \approx 1.692$$

The experimental $\delta$ (0.154) and theoretical $\delta$ (1.692) differ by an order of magnitude, indicating that the experimental peak readings (perhaps measured in mm displacement or some scaled unit) do not correspond to the stated theoretical system. The student should highlight this in their answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **General KTU Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state rad/s for $\omega_n$ and $\omega_d$, N·s/m for $c$, dimensionless for $\zeta$ and $\delta$. Half-mark deductions for missing units.
> 2. **Confusing $\omega_n$ and $\omega_d$:** $\omega_n$ is the *undamped* frequency, $\omega_d$ is what the system *actually* oscillates at when damped. Examiners test this distinction every year.
> 3. **Using the small-damping approximation when $\zeta > 0.2$:** Full marks lost. Always use the exact $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ unless the question explicitly says "use the small-damping approximation."
> 4. **Sign error in $N$ cycles:** $\ln(0.05) = -2.996$, NOT $+2.996$. Show the negative sign explicitly.
> 5. **Missing the regime classification:** Every KTU numerical answer should end with "Since $\zeta < 1$ (or $>1$ or $=1$), the system is under/critically/over-damped." Missing this loses a mark.
> 6. **Log decrement defined with wrong sign:** It is $\ln(x_n/x_{n+1})$ — *first* peak over *next* peak, both positive magnitudes.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 4 (Natural Frequency, Undamped & Damped Systems, Log Decrement):**

- **Undamped Natural Frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — derived from $\ddot{x} + (k/m)x = 0$.
- **Damped Equation of Motion:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0 \Rightarrow \ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$.
- **Critical Damping:** $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$.
- **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta = c/c_c = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ — *dimensionless*, classifies the regime.
- **Three Regimes:**
  - $\zeta < 1$: underdamped (oscillatory decay)
  - $\zeta = 1$: critical (fastest non-oscillatory return)
  - $\zeta > 1$: overdamped (slow non-oscillatory return)
- **Damped Natural Frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ (valid for $\zeta < 1$).
- **Damped Period:** $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d > T_n = 2\pi/\omega_n$.
- **Log Decrement Definition:** $\delta = \ln(x_n/x_{n+1})$, *always positive* for underdamped decay.
- **Log Decrement Formula:** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- **Small-Damping Approximation:** $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ — use **only** when $\zeta \ll 1$ (typically $\zeta < 0.2$).
- **N-Cycle Decay:** $x_n/x_{n+N} = e^{N\delta}$ → $N = (1/\delta)\ln(x_n/x_{n+N})$.
- **Energy in Undamped System:** Constant for all time.
- **Energy in Damped System:** Decays as $E(t) \propto e^{-2\zeta\omega_n t}$.
- **Engineering Benchmarks:**
  - Automobiles: $\zeta \approx 0.25$–$0.4$ (ride comfort).
  - Civil structures: $\zeta \approx 0.02$–$0.05$ (earthquake safety).
  - Instrument mounting: $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ (near-critical for isolation).
- **Always include in answers:** Units, regime classification statement, and a final engineering interpretation comment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forced Vibration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Forced Vibration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forced Vibration** is defined as the type of mechanical vibration in which a system oscillates under the influence of an **external time-varying excitation force** (or base motion) applied continuously or periodically, in addition to the restoring forces of the system itself.

When an external periodic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ or $F(t) = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$ is applied to a vibrating system, the system undergoes **forced vibration**. The external force is called the **exciting force** or **disturbing force**, and the frequency $\omega$ of this force is the **forcing frequency** or **excitation frequency**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The governing differential equation for a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) forced vibrating system is the foundation for analyzing:
> - Engine balance and crankshaft vibration
> - Vehicle suspension systems
> - Machine tool chatter
> - Structural response under seismic loads
> - Rotating unbalance in turbomachinery

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine pushing a child on a swing:**
> - If you stand still, the swing oscillates at its **natural frequency** (free vibration) and eventually stops due to damping.
> - If you push the swing rhythmically (at the same rate as the natural motion), the amplitude keeps growing — this is **resonance**, a dangerous form of forced vibration.
> - If you push at a different rate, the swing still moves but follows your pushing rhythm — this is **steady-state forced vibration**.

In engineering terms:
- The **child on the swing** = the **mass (m)** of the machine
- The **rope** = the **spring (k)**
- The **air resistance** = the **damper (c)**
- **Your pushes** = the **exciting force F(t)**

If the pushing rhythm matches the natural rhythm of the swing, the amplitude grows uncontrollably — and the swing (or machine) may fail catastrophically.

## 1.3 Classification of Forced Vibration

Forced vibration is broadly classified into three categories based on the nature of the excitation:

| Classification Type | Description | Typical Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Harmonic Excitation** | Force varies sinusoidally with time | Unbalanced rotating mass, reciprocating engine |
| **Periodic Non-Harmonic Excitation** | Force is periodic but not sinusoidal | Impact loads, gear meshing forces |
| **Non-Periodic / Random Excitation** | Force has no fixed pattern | Earthquake loads, wind turbulence, road roughness |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **KTU 2024 syllabus** in PEMRT756, focus is on **Harmonic Excitation**, which forms the bulk of board exam questions.

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Constants & Metrics)

- **Natural Frequency ($\omega_n$)** — Frequency at which the system would oscillate if undisturbed.
- **Forcing Frequency ($\omega$)** — Frequency of the external excitation.
- **Frequency Ratio ($r = \omega / \omega_n$)** — Dimensionless parameter central to all forced vibration analysis.
- **Damping Ratio ($\zeta = c / c_c$)** — Ratio of actual damping to critical damping.
- **Magnification Factor (MF)** — Ratio of dynamic amplitude to static deflection.
- **Transmissibility ($\varepsilon$)** — Ratio of force transmitted to the foundation to the applied force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equation of Motion (SDOF System)

Consider a single-degree-of-freedom system with mass $m$, spring constant $k$, and viscous damping coefficient $c$, subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.

**The equation of motion is:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Where:
- $m$ = Mass of the system (kg)
- $c$ = Damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = Spring stiffness (N/m)
- $x$ = Displacement from equilibrium (m)
- $F_0$ = Amplitude of exciting force (N)
- $\omega$ = Angular forcing frequency (rad/s)

## 2.2 The Three Response Components

The **total solution** of the equation of motion has two parts:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Complementary (Transient / Homogeneous) Solution** $x_c(t)$
> - Exists due to initial conditions
> - Decays with time because of damping
> - Vibration at the **damped natural frequency** $\omega_d$
> - Vanishes for $t \to \infty$

**2. Particular (Steady-State) Solution** $x_p(t)$
- Caused by the external force
- Persists as long as the force acts
- Vibrates at the **forcing frequency** $\omega$
- Independent of initial conditions

**3. Total Response:** $x(t) = x_c(t) + x_p(t)$

## 2.3 Detailed Logical Steps for Solution

**Step 1:** Write the standard form by dividing the equation of motion by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Assume a steady-state (particular) solution of the form:

$$x_p(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Where $X$ = steady-state amplitude and $\phi$ = phase angle by which displacement lags the force.

**Step 3:** Substitute derivatives into the equation of motion and match coefficients of $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ to find $X$ and $\phi$.

**Step 4:** Evaluate the magnification factor (dynamic amplification) by comparing amplitude to static deflection $x_{st} = F_0 / k$.

## 2.4 Why Resonance is Dangerous

When the forcing frequency $\omega$ approaches the natural frequency $\omega_n$:

- The amplitude theoretically becomes **infinite** (in undamped systems).
- In real (damped) systems, the amplitude is large but finite.
- The phase angle $\phi \to 90°$ at $\omega = \omega_n$.
- Energy input per cycle exceeds energy dissipated, leading to amplitude buildup.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Many students forget that resonance in an **undamped** system produces **infinite amplitude**. This is a frequent 3-mark conceptual question.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ | Equation of motion for damped forced vibration | N |
| 2 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural circular frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency in Hz | Hz |
| 4 | $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ | Critical damping coefficient | N·s/m |
| 5 | $\zeta = c / c_c$ | Damping ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 6 | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | Frequency ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 7 | $X = \dfrac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Steady-state amplitude (damped) | m |
| 8 | $x_{st} = F_0 / k$ | Static deflection due to force $F_0$ | m |
| 9 | $\text{MF} = \dfrac{X}{x_{st}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Magnification factor (damped) | — |
| 10 | $X = \dfrac{x_{st}}{\vert 1-r^2 \vert}$ | Undamped amplitude (when $\zeta = 0$) | m |
| 11 | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}$ | Phase angle (damped) | rad |
| 12 | $\phi = 90°$ | Phase angle at resonance (undamped) | rad |
| 13 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Transmissibility (force ratio) | — |
| 14 | $\varepsilon \approx \dfrac{1}{r^2 - 1}$ | Approximate transmissibility for $r \gg 1$ | — |
| 15 | $\omega_r = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ | Frequency at peak amplitude (damped resonance) | rad/s |
| 16 | $X_{max} = \dfrac{x_{st}}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Peak amplitude at damped resonance | m |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Forced Vibration Principle Used |
|---|---|
| Vehicle Suspension | Vibration isolation — choose $r > \sqrt{2}$ for comfort |
| Building under earthquake | Resonance avoidance — building $f_n$ ≠ earthquake $f$ |
| Machine mounting | Transmissibility minimization via rubber mounts |
| Engine crankshaft | Torsional vibration analysis at firing frequency |
| Wind turbines | Avoid excitation at blade passing frequency |
| Audio speakers | Forced vibration of diaphragm at audio frequency |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Amplitude for Damped Forced Vibration

### Starting Point

The equation of motion of a damped SDOF system under harmonic force:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 1: Divide Through by Mass $m$

Divide the entire equation by $m$ to express it in standard form. This isolates the natural frequency and damping ratio terms:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Substitute Standard Notations

Substitute the standard vibration parameters:

- $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ (natural frequency squared)
- $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$ (damping term from $c = 2m\zeta\omega_n$)

This gives the canonical form:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Assume a Steady-State Particular Solution

Since the forcing function is sinusoidal and we are interested in the steady-state response (after transients die out), assume:

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

Where $X$ is the amplitude and $\phi$ is the phase angle.

### Step 4: Compute Derivatives of $x_p(t)$

First derivative:

$$\dot{x}_p(t) = X\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

Second derivative:

$$\ddot{x}_p(t) = -X\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

### Step 5: Substitute Back into Equation of Motion

Plugging the derivatives into the standard form:

$$-X\omega^2\sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi) + \omega_n^2 X\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 6: Group Sinusoidal and Cosinusoidal Terms

Rearrange by collecting the $\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\cos(\omega t - \phi)$ terms together:

$$X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin(\omega t - \phi) + 2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi) = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 7: Expand the Right Side

Use the identity $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin(\omega t)\cos\phi - \cos(\omega t)\sin\phi$ on the left side. After expansion and regrouping the $\sin(\omega t)$ and $\cos(\omega t)$ coefficients, we get:

$$[X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\cos\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\sin\phi]\sin(\omega t) + [2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\cos\phi - X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin\phi]\cos(\omega t) = \frac{F_0}{m}\sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 8: Equate Coefficients

For the equation to be satisfied for **all** $t$:

**Coefficient of $\sin(\omega t)$:**

$$X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\cos\phi + 2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\sin\phi = \frac{F_0}{m}$$

**Coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:**

$$2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\cos\phi - X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin\phi = 0$$

### Step 9: Solve for Phase Angle $\phi$

From the coefficient of $\cos(\omega t)$:

$$2\zeta\omega_n X\omega\cos\phi = X(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)\sin\phi$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta\omega_n\omega}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\omega_n^2$:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta(\omega/\omega_n)}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2}$$

Introduce frequency ratio $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}}$$

### Step 10: Solve for Amplitude $X$

Square both coefficient equations and add them. Using $\sin^2\phi + \cos^2\phi = 1$:

$$X^2[(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2] = \left(\frac{F_0}{m}\right)^2$$

Taking the square root:

$$X = \frac{F_0/m}{\sqrt{(\omega_n^2 - \omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\omega_n\omega)^2}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $1/\omega_n^2$:

$$X = \frac{F_0/m\omega_n^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Since $x_{st} = F_0/k = F_0/(m\omega_n^2)$:

$$\boxed{X = \frac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

This is the **steady-state amplitude** of forced vibration.

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnification Factor

The **magnification factor (MF)** is the ratio of dynamic amplitude $X$ to static deflection $x_{st}$:

$$\text{MF} = \frac{X}{x_{st}}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Special Cases:**

- **At $r = 0$:** $\text{MF} = 1$ (system is at rest under static load)
- **At $r = 1$ (resonance):** $\text{MF} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$
- **At $r \to \infty$:** $\text{MF} \to 0$ (mass cannot follow high-frequency excitation)

## 3.3 Derivation: Frequency at Peak Amplitude (Damped Resonance)

To find the peak amplitude, differentiate the MF with respect to $r$ and set it to zero.

$$\frac{d}{dr}\left[(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2\right] = 0$$

Expand:

$$(1-r^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2 r^2 = 1 - 2r^2 + r^4 + 4\zeta^2 r^2$$

Differentiate with respect to $r$:

$$-4r + 4r^3 + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$

Factor out $4r$:

$$4r\left[-1 + r^2 + 2\zeta^2\right] = 0$$

Discard $r = 0$ (trivial) and solve:

$$r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Peak Amplitude

Substitute $r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$ back into the MF formula:

$$\text{MF}_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-(1-2\zeta^2))^2 + (2\zeta\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2})^2}}$$

Simplify:

$$\text{MF}_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\zeta^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2(1-2\zeta^2)}}$$

$$\text{MF}_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4}}$$

$$\text{MF}_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2 - 4\zeta^4}}$$

$$\text{MF}_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

Therefore peak amplitude:

$$\boxed{X_{max} = \frac{x_{st}}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Transmissibility (Force Transmitted to Base)

When the mass $m$ vibrates, the force transmitted to the foundation is the sum of spring force and damper force:

$$F_T(t) = kx + c\dot{x}$$

For steady-state solution:

$$F_T(t) = kX\sin(\omega t - \phi) + cX\omega\cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

The amplitude of transmitted force:

$$F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

Substituting $X = \dfrac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ and $x_{st} = F_0/k$:

$$F_T = \frac{F_0}{k}\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$\frac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\frac{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}{k^2[(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2]}}$$

Since $c = 2m\zeta\omega_n$ and $k = m\omega_n^2$, so $c\omega / k = 2\zeta r$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

**Critical Observation:**
- For $r > \sqrt{2}$ and any $\zeta$, the transmissibility $\varepsilon < 1$ — system acts as an **isolator**.
- For $r < \sqrt{2}$, the transmissibility $\varepsilon > 1$ — system **amplifies** the force.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary Table

| Condition | Magnification Factor | Phase Angle $\phi$ | Physical Significance |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| $\omega = 0$ | $1$ | $0°$ | Static deflection |
| $\omega \ll \omega_n$ | $\approx 1$ | $\approx 0°$ | Quasi-static response |
| $\omega = \omega_n$ (resonance) | $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | $90°$ | Maximum amplification (damped) |
| $\omega = \omega_r$ (peak amplitude) | $\dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | between 0°–180° | True peak for damped systems |
| $\omega = \sqrt{2}\omega_n$ | $1$ | $135°$ | Transmissibility crossover |
| $\omega \gg \omega_n$ | $\to 0$ | $\to 180°$ | Mass cannot follow force |

```mermaid
    stepB --> stepC["CALCULATE Damping Ratio Zeta = C by 2 sqrt KM"]
    stepC --> stepD["CALCULATE Frequency Ratio R = W by Wn"]
    stepD --> stepE["APPLY Magnification Factor MF = 1 by sqrt of 1 minus R square plus 2 Zeta R square"]

    stepF --> stepG["CALCULATE Amplitude X = MF times Xst"]

    stepJ --> stepK["END Report Results"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Define forced vibration. Differentiate between free vibration and forced vibration.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Forced Vibration:** When a system oscillates under the continuous influence of an external time-varying force, the resulting motion is called forced vibration.
>
> | Aspect | Free Vibration | Forced Vibration |
> |:---|:---|:---|
> | Source of oscillation | Initial disturbance only | Continuous external excitation |
> | Duration | Decays with time | Persists as long as force acts |
> | Frequency | System's natural frequency $\omega_n$ | Forcing frequency $\omega$ |
> | Amplitude | Depends on initial conditions | Depends on force magnitude |
> | Sustained by | System's restoring forces | External energy input |

**[3 Marks: Definition = 1, Comparison table = 2]**

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**What is the magnification factor in forced vibration? Write its expression for a damped system and explain its significance at resonance.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> The **magnification factor (MF)** is defined as the ratio of the dynamic amplitude $X$ of forced vibration to the static deflection $x_{st}$ caused by the same force applied statically.
>
> $$\text{MF} = \frac{X}{x_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$
>
> **Significance at Resonance** ($r = 1$, i.e., $\omega = \omega_n$):
>
> $$\text{MF}_{resonance} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$
>
> - For **undamped systems** ($\zeta = 0$): MF $\to \infty$, indicating **theoretical infinite amplitude**.
> - For **damped systems**: MF is finite but very large for small $\zeta$.
> - The closer $\omega$ approaches $\omega_n$, the larger the amplitude, posing a danger of structural failure.

**[3 Marks: Definition + Formula = 2, Resonance significance = 1]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**A machine of mass 50 kg is mounted on springs of total stiffness 200 kN/m. The machine operates at 1500 rpm and has an unbalanced mass creating a harmonic force of 1000 N amplitude.**

#### (a) Determine the magnification factor, amplitude of vibration, and phase angle. Assume damping ratio $\zeta = 0.1$. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Convert Operating Speed to Angular Frequency**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60}$$

$$\omega = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{200 \times 10^3}{50}}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Compute Frequency Ratio**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{63.25}$$

$$r = 2.484$$

**Step 4: Calculate Magnification Factor**

$$\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$r^2 = (2.484)^2 = 6.170$$

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = (1 - 6.170)^2 = (-5.170)^2 = 26.73$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = (2 \times 0.1 \times 2.484)^2 = (0.4968)^2 = 0.2468$$

$$\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26.73 + 0.2468}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{26.98}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MF} = 0.1925}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Static Deflection and Amplitude**

$$x_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{1000}{200 \times 10^3} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$X = \text{MF} \times x_{st} = 0.1925 \times 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{X = 0.9625 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Phase Angle**

$$\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.1 \times 2.484}{1 - 6.170} = \frac{0.4968}{-5.170}$$

$$\tan\phi = -0.0961$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 174.51° \text{ (or approximately } 180°\text{)}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Since $r > 1$, the system operates in the **vibration isolation region**. The phase angle approaches $180°$ meaning the displacement is approximately out of phase with the force, and the amplitude is much smaller than the static deflection.

#### (b) Calculate the force transmitted to the foundation and determine if the system acts as a vibration isolator. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate Transmissibility**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sqrt{1 + 0.2468}}{\sqrt{26.98}} = \frac{\sqrt{1.2468}}{\sqrt{26.98}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1.1165}{5.1942}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 0.2149}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Transmitted Force**

$$F_T = \varepsilon \times F_0 = 0.2149 \times 1000$$

$$\boxed{F_T = 214.9 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 3: Verify Vibration Isolation**

Since $r = 2.484 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the system **acts as a vibration isolator**.

The transmissibility $\varepsilon = 0.2149 < 1$, confirming that only ~21.5% of the applied force is transmitted to the foundation. **Effective isolation is achieved.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit error in RPM conversion:** Students often write $\omega = N/60$ instead of $\omega = 2\pi N/60$. This leads to a completely wrong answer. Always convert RPM to rad/s.
> 2. **Sign of phase angle:** When $r > 1$, the denominator $1 - r^2$ is negative, giving $\tan\phi$ negative. Some students incorrectly report $\phi$ as positive. The phase lag is in the second quadrant, between $90°$ and $180°$.
> 3. **Forgetting the static deflection step:** Many students jump directly to MF without calculating $x_{st}$, then confuse $X$ with MF.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**An instrument weighing 20 N is mounted on a vibrating machine. The instrument is to be isolated from vibrations in the frequency range 20 Hz to 60 Hz. The instrument is supported on a rubber mount of stiffness 6000 N/m.**

#### (a) Calculate the natural frequency of the system and the frequency ratio at the operating extremes. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate the Mass**

$$m = \frac{W}{g} = \frac{20}{9.81} = 2.039 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{6000}{2.039}}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{2942.6}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_n = 54.25 \text{ rad/s}}$$

**Step 3: Convert to Natural Frequency in Hz**

$$f_n = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{54.25}{2\pi}$$

$$\boxed{f_n = 8.635 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Frequency Ratio at Lower Extreme (20 Hz)**

$$\omega_{low} = 2\pi \times 20 = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r_{low} = \frac{\omega_{low}}{\omega_n} = \frac{125.66}{54.25}$$

$$\boxed{r_{low} = 2.317}$$

**Step 5: Calculate Frequency Ratio at Upper Extreme (60 Hz)**

$$\omega_{high} = 2\pi \times 60 = 376.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r_{high} = \frac{\omega_{high}}{\omega_n} = \frac{376.99}{54.25}$$

$$\boxed{r_{high} = 6.949}$$

**Analysis:** Both frequency ratios are greater than $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, indicating that the rubber mount will function as a **vibration isolator** throughout the entire operating range.

#### (b) Determine the maximum transmissibility in the operating range assuming damping ratio $\zeta = 0.05$, and recommend if the design is suitable. **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Calculate Transmissibility at Lower Frequency (20 Hz)**

$$\varepsilon_{low} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 2.317)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - 2.317^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 2.317)^2}}$$

$$2\zeta r_{low} = 0.1 \times 2.317 = 0.2317$$

$$r_{low}^2 = 5.368$$

$$\varepsilon_{low} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + 0.0537}}{\sqrt{(1-5.368)^2 + 0.0537}} = \frac{\sqrt{1.0537}}{\sqrt{19.08 + 0.0537}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{low} = \frac{1.0265}{\sqrt{19.13}} = \frac{1.0265}{4.374}$$

$$\varepsilon_{low} = 0.2346$$

**Step 2: Calculate Transmissibility at Upper Frequency (60 Hz)**

$$2\zeta r_{high} = 0.1 \times 6.949 = 0.6949$$

$$r_{high}^2 = 48.29$$

$$\varepsilon_{high} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + 0.4829}}{\sqrt{(1-48.29)^2 + 0.4829}} = \frac{\sqrt{1.4829}}{\sqrt{2234.5 + 0.4829}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{high} = \frac{1.2177}{\sqrt{2235.0}} = \frac{1.2177}{47.28}$$

$$\varepsilon_{high} = 0.02575$$

**Step 3: Determine Maximum Transmissibility**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = 0.2346 \text{ (occurs at the lower frequency limit 20 Hz)}}$$

**Step 4: Recommendation**

Since both $\varepsilon_{low}$ and $\varepsilon_{high}$ are well below 1, the rubber mount provides effective vibration isolation throughout the operating range. However, the transmissibility at 20 Hz is significantly higher than at 60 Hz, suggesting the mount is more effective at higher frequencies.

**Final Recommendation:** The design is **suitable** for vibration isolation, with maximum transmissibility of approximately 23.5%. For improved isolation at low frequencies, the natural frequency of the system should be reduced (e.g., by reducing stiffness or increasing mass).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Weight vs Mass confusion:** Always convert weight (N) to mass (kg) by dividing by $g = 9.81$ m/s².
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Frequency ratios require both frequencies in the same units (Hz or rad/s). Mixing them is a guaranteed zero.
> 3. **Misinterpreting transmissibility > 1:** If the operating frequency is below $\sqrt{2}\omega_n$, the system amplifies rather than isolates vibrations. Always check this threshold.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Forced Vibration**

### Core Definitions
- **Forced vibration** = Oscillation under continuous external excitation $F_0\sin(\omega t)$
- **Free vibration** = Oscillation after a single initial disturbance, decays naturally
- **Natural frequency** = $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
- **Critical damping** = $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$
- **Damping ratio** = $\zeta = c/c_c$

### Key Equations (Must Memorize)

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$$

$$\boxed{X = \frac{x_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MF} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \frac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

### Critical Numbers to Remember
- $r = \sqrt{2}$ → Transmissibility crossover ($\varepsilon = 1$)
- $r = 1$ → Resonance (peak amplitude in damped systems)
- $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ → Frequency of peak amplitude
- $\text{MF}_{resonance} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ → Magnification factor at $r = 1$
- For **undamped** system: $\phi = 90°$ at all frequencies above $r = 0$

### Important Observations
1. **Amplitude is maximum** at $r = \sqrt{1-2\zeta^2}$ (not exactly at $r = 1$ for damped systems).
2. **Phase angle** ranges from $0°$ to $180°$ as $r$ goes from $0$ to $\infty$.
3. **At $r = 1$ (resonance):** $\phi = 90°$ regardless of damping.
4. **Above $r = \sqrt{2}$:** System isolates vibration from foundation.
5. **Below $r = \sqrt{2}$:** System amplifies the transmitted force.
6. **High damping** lowers peak amplitude but degrades isolation effectiveness at high $r$.
7. **Static deflection** $x_{st} = F_0/k$ is always used as the reference for magnification factor.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- Mixing up natural frequency with damped natural frequency ($\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$).
- Reporting negative phase angles (always use quadrant-aware arctangent).
- Confusing magnification factor with amplitude.
- Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s in numerical problems.
- Using $\omega_d$ instead of $\omega_n$ in the frequency ratio formula.

### Real-World Design Rules of Thumb
- For **machine isolation**, choose $r > 2$ to ensure $\varepsilon < 0.25$.
- For **vehicle suspension**, target $r \approx 1$ but use damping $\zeta \approx 0.2 - 0.3$ to limit peak.
- For **earthquake-resistant buildings**, ensure $f_n$ is far from dominant earthquake frequencies.
- For **precision instruments**, minimize transmissibility by maximizing $r$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, always:
> 1. Write the governing equation of motion
> 2. State all given data clearly
> 3. Show unit conversions (especially RPM → rad/s)
> 4. Calculate each variable step-by-step
> 5. Present final answers with units in boxes
> 6. Provide a physical interpretation at the end

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
