topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Response of SDOF Systems to Harmonic Excitation, Whirling of Shaft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Response of SDOF Systems to Harmonic Excitation

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Single Degree of Freedom (SDOF) system** subjected to **harmonic excitation** is a classic second-order linear differential equation model in which an external force, varying sinusoidally with time (typically of the form $F(t) = F_0 \sin\omega t$ or $F_0 \cos\omega t$), acts upon a mass–spring–damper assembly. The governing equation is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin\omega t$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of the system (kg)
- $c$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = stiffness of the spring (N/m)
- $F_0$ = amplitude of the harmonic force (N)
- $\omega$ = angular frequency of the exciting force (rad/s)
- $x, \dot{x}, \ddot{x}$ = displacement, velocity, and acceleration of the mass

The **response** $x(t)$ of the system is the sum of two distinct physical phenomena:
1. **Transient (complementary) response** — caused by initial conditions; decays to zero as $t \to \infty$ when damping is present.
2. **Steady-state (particular) response** — persists indefinitely and oscillates at the same frequency $\omega$ as the exciting force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4, students are required to derive and apply the complete solution of the harmonic excitation problem, evaluate the **Magnification Factor (MF)** and **Phase Angle ($\phi$)**, and interpret the response in **undamped**, **underdamped**, **critically damped**, and **resonance** conditions. Resonance occurs when the forcing frequency $\omega$ equals the natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, leading to dangerously large amplitudes.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Push-Swing Metaphor

Imagine pushing a child on a playground swing. The child-on-swing is your **SDOF system**, the swing ropes are the **spring**, air resistance is the **damper**, and your pushes are the **harmonic force**. If you push at the right rhythm (matching the natural swinging frequency), the swing goes higher and higher — that is **resonance**. If you push too fast or too slow, the swing barely responds — that is **frequency mismatch**. If you stop pushing (force becomes zero), the swing eventually comes to rest — that is the **transient decay**. The system "remembers" the forcing frequency but decides its own amplitude based on the ratio of frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in Harmonic Excitation Problems:**
> - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (acceleration due to gravity)
> - $2\pi = 6.2832$ (used in converting angular frequency to frequency in Hz)
> - $60$ (used in converting rad/s to RPM for whirling problems)

---

## 1.2 Whirling of Shafts

### Formal Academic Definition

**Whirling of a shaft** is the lateral, transverse vibration phenomenon exhibited by a rotating shaft carrying one or more discs when the rotational speed approaches a value at which the lateral dynamic deflection of the shaft becomes excessively large. The rotational speed at which this occurs is called the **critical speed** (or **whirling speed**) of the shaft.

The fundamental whirling equation, neglecting gyroscopic effects, for a single-disc shaft is:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

where $\delta_{st}$ is the **static deflection** of the shaft at the disc location due to the weight of the disc, and $g$ is acceleration due to gravity.

The corresponding **critical speed in revolutions per minute (RPM)** is:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st} \text{ (in mm)}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whirling is *not* the same as simple rotational motion. It is the lateral orbiting of the shaft centreline (and the disc) about the geometric axis. The shaft bows out radially, and the disc travels in a circular path whose radius is the whirling amplitude. When the operational RPM equals $N_c$, the centrifugal force reinforces the deflection in a positive feedback loop — catastrophic failure can follow.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Ruler Trick

Hold a thin ruler vertically on a table with one end pressed by your palm and spin it like a top. At very low spin, it rotates smoothly. As you spin it faster, it starts to wobble — that wobble is *whirling*. Push it even faster and it may snap. The speed at which the wobble becomes violent is its **critical speed**. Industrial turbines, propeller shafts, and motor rotors all face this same danger, which is why designers must keep operating speeds safely away from critical values (typically with a safety margin of $\pm 20\%$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mathematical Modelling of an SDOF System

A typical SDOF system consists of:
- A **rigid mass** $m$ that can move in only one direction
- A **massless spring** of stiffness $k$ providing restoring force $-kx$
- A **viscous damper** of coefficient $c$ providing dissipative force $-c\dot{x}$
- An **external harmonic force** $F(t) = F_0 \sin\omega t$

By applying **Newton's Second Law** to the free body of the mass:

$$\sum F = m\ddot{x} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; m\ddot{x} = -kx - c\dot{x} + F_0\sin\omega t$$

Rearranging:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin\omega t \tag{2.1}$$

This is a **non-homogeneous, linear, second-order ordinary differential equation** with constant coefficients.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Solution Method

The solution of Equation (2.1) follows the classical **complementary + particular** method:

### Step 1 — Define the Characteristic Quantities

- **Natural (undamped natural) frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{2m\omega_n} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$
  where $c_c = 2m\omega_n$ is the **critical damping coefficient**
- **Frequency ratio:** $r = \omega/\omega_n$
- **Static deflection under peak force:** $X_{st} = F_0/k$

### Step 2 — Complementary (Homogeneous) Solution

The homogeneous part $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$ has the form:

$$x_c(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\left[A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t\right]$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the **damped natural frequency**.

For $\zeta < 1$ (**underdamped** — most common in engineering), this part decays exponentially to zero and represents the **transient response**.

### Step 3 — Particular (Steady-State) Solution

Since the forcing function is sinusoidal, the steady-state response is also sinusoidal at the same frequency $\omega$ but with a **phase lag** $\phi$:

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi) \tag{2.2}$$

The amplitude $X$ and phase angle $\phi$ are obtained by substituting Equation (2.2) into Equation (2.1) and matching coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$.

### Step 4 — Phase Angle and Amplitude — The Key Engineering Outputs

- **Phase angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right]$ (Note: in the second quadrant for $r > 1$)
- **Steady-state amplitude:** $X = \dfrac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \dfrac{X_{st}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$

### Step 5 — Magnification Factor (Dynamic Amplification Factor)

$$\boxed{MF = \frac{X}{X_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

This is the **most important parameter** in KTU problems — it tells how many times larger the dynamic deflection is compared to the static deflection under the same peak force.

### Step 6 — Resonance and Anti-Resonance

- **Resonance:** $r = 1$ (i.e., $\omega = \omega_n$). The amplitude becomes $X = X_{st}/(2\zeta)$ — *finite only because of damping*.
- **Anti-resonance (for $r > 1$):** $X$ attains its maximum at $r = r_{max} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ with peak value $MF_{max} = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$.
- **At $r \to \infty$:** $X \to 0$ — the system cannot respond to infinitely fast forcing; it acts like a rigid wall.
- **At $r \to 0$:** $X \to X_{st}$ — quasi-static response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the phase angle $\phi$ always between $0$ and $\pi$?**
> The mass cannot respond instantaneously — there is always a time lag. The output lags the input because the system has inertia. At $r \ll 1$, $\phi \approx 0$ (in-phase with force). At $r = 1$, $\phi = \pi/2$ (force is in phase with velocity). At $r \gg 1$, $\phi \to \pi$ (out of phase — force is opposite to displacement).

---

## 2.3 Whirling of Shafts — Theoretical Framework

### Why Does a Shaft Whirl?

A perfectly balanced rotating shaft should not vibrate laterally. But in practice:
1. The mass of the shaft and the disc(s) is never perfectly symmetric.
2. The shaft has a finite lateral stiffness (it bends elastically under transverse load).
3. As the shaft rotates, any small eccentricity of the mass centre from the geometric axis creates a **centrifugal force** $m\omega^2 e$ that bends the shaft further.

This creates a **self-reinforcing loop**:
- Centrifugal force $\uparrow$ as $\omega \uparrow$
- Deflection $\delta \uparrow$ because force is $\uparrow$
- Eccentricity of mass centre from geometric axis $\uparrow$ because of $\delta$
- Centrifugal force $\uparrow$ further...

At a particular speed, the system becomes **dynamically unstable** — the deflection grows without bound (in the ideal undamped case). This is the **critical speed**.

### Equation of Whirling (Single Disc)

The shaft is modelled as a simply supported beam of stiffness $k$, and the disc of mass $m$ is mounted at the centre. The static deflection under gravity is:

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k}$$

For dynamic whirling, the centrifugal force is $m\omega^2 r$, where $r$ is the whirling radius. Equating this to the elastic restoring force $kr$:

$$m\omega_c^2\, r = k\, r \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

In **RPM**:

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}} \times 1000}\;\; \text{(when }\delta_{st}\text{ in mm)}$$

$$N_c \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st} \text{ (in mm)}}} \text{ RPM} \tag{2.3}$$

### Multi-Disc Shafts — Dunkerley's Method

When a shaft carries multiple discs, the effective critical speed is **lower** than the single-disc case. **Dunkerley's empirical formula** (lower-bound estimate) is:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c2}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c3}^2} + \cdots}$$

where $\omega_{ci}$ is the critical speed if disc $i$ alone were on the shaft.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Common Question Pattern:** Dunkerley's method is a *favourite* of KTU examiners. Always verify that the discs' deflections add up correctly to the total $\delta_{st}$ before applying the formula.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s | Always positive |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c / (2m\omega_n)$ | dimensionless | $0 < \zeta < 1$ for underdamped |
| Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m | Boundary of oscillation |
| Damped natural freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s | Actual oscillation rate |
| Frequency ratio | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless | 1 at resonance |
| Static deflection | $X_{st} = F_0/k$ | m | Quasi-static amplitude |
| Dynamic amplitude | $X = \dfrac{X_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | m | Steady-state only |
| Magnification factor | $MF = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}$ | dimensionless | Maximum exam use |
| Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\dfrac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right]$ | radians | $0 \le \phi \le \pi$ |
| Resonance amplitude | $X_{res} = X_{st}/(2\zeta)$ | m | At $r = 1$ |
| Whirling speed (rad/s) | $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ | rad/s | Single disc |
| Whirling speed (RPM) | $N_c = 946/\sqrt{\delta_{st}\text{ (mm)}}$ | RPM | Standard KTU form |
| Dunkerley (multi-disc) | $1/\omega_c^2 = \sum (1/\omega_{ci}^2)$ | s²/rad² | Lower bound estimate |
| Transmissibility | $TR = \sqrt{\dfrac{1+(2\zeta r)^2}{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$ | dimensionless | Force transmitted to base |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Harmonic Excitation Analysis** is used in designing foundations for reciprocating engines, rotating machinery, vehicle suspension systems, and buildings in earthquake zones.
> - **Whirling Analysis** is critical for the safe operation of steam turbines, gas turbine rotors, motor shafts, propeller shafts of ships, and helicopter drive shafts. Operating near critical speed causes catastrophic failure, as observed in the famous 1940 Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (aeroelastic flutter, conceptually similar to whirling).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Response of an Undamped SDOF System

**Starting Equation (undamped, $c = 0$):**

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = F_0\sin\omega t \tag{3.1}$$

**Step 1 — Assume a sinusoidal particular solution.** Since the forcing function is $\sin\omega t$, the steady-state response must also be sinusoidal at the same frequency. We try:

$$x_p(t) = X\sin\omega t$$

**Step 2 — Compute the derivatives:**

$$\dot{x}_p = X\omega\cos\omega t$$

$$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2\sin\omega t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Equation (3.1):**

$$m(-X\omega^2\sin\omega t) + k(X\sin\omega t) = F_0\sin\omega t$$

**Step 4 — Group terms containing $\sin\omega t$:**

$$(k - m\omega^2)X\sin\omega t = F_0\sin\omega t$$

**Step 5 — Equate coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ on both sides:**

$$(k - m\omega^2)X = F_0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the amplitude $X$:**

$$X = \frac{F_0}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{F_0/k}{1 - (\omega/\omega_n)^2} = \frac{X_{st}}{1 - r^2} \tag{3.2}$$

**Step 7 — Note the singularity.** Equation (3.2) shows that $X \to \infty$ when $r \to 1$ — that is **resonance**. The undamped system has no amplitude limit at resonance; in reality, damping limits this.

**Step 8 — Phase angle in undamped case.** The response is **in-phase** with the force when $r < 1$ and **$180°$ out-of-phase** when $r > 1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Response of a Damped SDOF System

**Starting Equation:**

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin\omega t \tag{3.3}$$

**Step 1 — Assume a particular solution of the form:**

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi) = X[\sin\omega t\cos\phi - \cos\omega t\sin\phi]$$

**Step 2 — Compute derivatives:**

$$\dot{x}_p = X\omega[\cos\omega t\cos\phi + \sin\omega t\sin\phi]$$

$$\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2[\sin\omega t\cos\phi - \cos\omega t\sin\phi]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Equation (3.3).** After expansion:

$$m\{-X\omega^2[\sin\omega t\cos\phi - \cos\omega t\sin\phi]\}$$
$$+ c\{X\omega[\cos\omega t\cos\phi + \sin\omega t\sin\phi]\}$$
$$+ k\{X[\sin\omega t\cos\phi - \cos\omega t\sin\phi]\} = F_0\sin\omega t$$

**Step 4 — Collect coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$ separately.**

Coefficient of $\sin\omega t$:

$$X[-m\omega^2\cos\phi + c\omega\sin\phi + k\cos\phi] = F_0$$

Coefficient of $\cos\omega t$:

$$X[m\omega^2\sin\phi + c\omega\cos\phi - k\sin\phi] = 0$$

**Step 5 — From the $\cos\omega t$ equation:**

$$X\sin\phi\,(k - m\omega^2) + c\omega X\cos\phi = 0$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2} \tag{3.4}$$

**Step 6 — From the $\sin\omega t$ equation, divide by $\cos\phi$ (assuming $\cos\phi \neq 0$):**

$$X[k - m\omega^2 + c\omega\tan\phi] = \frac{F_0}{\cos\phi}$$

**Step 7 — Use the identity $\cos\phi = 1/\sqrt{1+\tan^2\phi}$ and substitute $\tan\phi$ from (3.4):**

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $k$ and express in non-dimensional form:**

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{X_{st}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \tag{3.5}$$

This is the **dynamic amplitude**, and Equation (3.4) is the **phase angle** — the two fundamental results of harmonic excitation analysis.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Whirling Speed of a Shaft Carrying a Single Disc

**Setup:** A uniform shaft of stiffness $k$ supported between two bearings carries a disc of mass $m$ at its mid-span. The disc has a small eccentricity $e$ between its **mass centre** ($G$) and the **geometric centre of the shaft** ($O$).

**Step 1 — Static Equilibrium under Gravity.** When the shaft is at rest, the disc's weight $mg$ deflects the shaft centre by:

$$\delta_{st} = \frac{mg}{k}$$

This means the spring force $k\delta_{st} = mg$, balancing gravity.

**Step 2 — Rotating Condition.** Let the shaft rotate at constant angular speed $\omega$. The disc centre $O$ moves in a circle of radius $r$ (the whirling radius) about the bearing axis. The mass centre $G$ is at distance $(r + e)$ from the bearing axis.

**Step 3 — Centrifugal Force.** The mass $m$ experiences a centrifugal force directed outward from the bearing axis:

$$F_c = m\omega^2 (r + e)$$

**Step 4 — Spring Restoring Force.** The spring (shaft) deflects by $r$ and exerts an inward restoring force:

$$F_s = kr$$

**Step 5 — Steady-State Whirling Equation.** At constant whirling radius $r$, the centrifugal force equals the spring force:

$$m\omega^2(r + e) = kr$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $r$:**

$$r\,(m\omega^2 - k) = -m\omega^2 e$$

$$r = \frac{m\omega^2 e}{k - m\omega^2} = \frac{e}{(\omega_n/\omega)^2 - 1} = \frac{e\, r^2}{1 - r^2} \cdot \frac{1}{?}$$

Re-writing cleanly:

$$r = \frac{e\, \omega^2}{\omega_n^2 - \omega^2} \tag{3.6}$$

where $\omega_n^2 = k/m$.

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**
- When $\omega = 0$: $r = 0$ (no whirling, shaft at rest).
- As $\omega \to \omega_n$: $r \to \infty$ — **resonance / critical whirling speed**.
- When $\omega > \omega_n$: $r$ is **negative**, meaning the mass centre is *inside* the geometric centre — the shaft whirls in the **opposite sense** to its rotation (self-centering behaviour).

**Step 8 — Critical Speed Definition:**

$$\omega_c = \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \tag{3.7}$$

**Step 9 — Conversion to RPM:**

$$N_c = \frac{60\,\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$$

Since $\delta_{st}$ is often in mm in KTU problems, $\delta_{st}\text{(m)} = \delta_{st}\text{(mm)}/1000$, so:

$$N_c = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81 \times 1000}{\delta_{st}\text{(mm)}}} = \frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{\frac{9810}{\delta_{st}\text{(mm)}}}$$

Computing the constant:

$$\frac{30}{\pi}\sqrt{9810} \approx \frac{30}{3.1416} \times 99.05 \approx 945.6 \approx 946$$

$$\boxed{N_c \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st}\text{ (in mm)}}} \;\; \text{RPM}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dunkerley's Method for Multi-Disc Shafts

**Step 1 — Single-disc case reviewed.** For one disc of mass $m_i$ and shaft stiffness $k_i$ at that location:

$$\omega_{ci}^2 = \frac{k_i}{m_i} = \frac{g}{\delta_{i,st}}$$

where $\delta_{i,st} = m_i g / k_i$ is the static deflection at the location of disc $i$ due to *its own weight* alone.

**Step 2 — Multi-disc superposition principle.** For a shaft carrying $n$ discs, the equivalent critical speed is **less** than any individual $\omega_{ci}$. Dunkerley proposed (empirically, then later justified via Rayleigh's energy method):

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c2}^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{\omega_{cn}^2} \tag{3.8}$$

**Step 3 — Practical form using deflections.** Since $\omega_{ci}^2 = g/\delta_{i,st}$:

$$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{\delta_{1,st} + \delta_{2,st} + \cdots + \delta_{n,st}}{g} = \frac{\delta_{total,st}}{g}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{1,st} + \delta_{2,st} + \cdots + \delta_{n,st}}}$$

This is the **Rayleigh formula** for a multi-disc shaft, and the multi-disc whirling speed in RPM becomes:

$$\boxed{N_c \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_1 + \delta_2 + \cdots + \delta_n \text{ (in mm)}}} \;\; \text{RPM}}$$

where $\delta_i$ is the static deflection at disc $i$ due to its own weight only.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of formula (3.8):** Rayleigh's energy method yields *exact* results only for the *lowest* critical speed. Dunkerley's formula is an *approximation* that gives a slightly **lower** value than the true critical speed, making it a **safe (conservative) estimate** for design.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an SDOF Harmonic Excitation System

```mermaid

    B --> C[Mass m<br/>Stores Kinetic Energy]
    C --> D[Damper c<br/>Dissipates Energy]
    D --> E[Spring k<br/>Stores Potential Energy]
    E --> F[Displacement Output<br/>x of t]
    F --> G[Sensor / Measurement<br/>x dot and x double dot]
    G --> H[Response Analyzer<br/>Amplitude X and Phase phi]
    H --> I[Safety Decision<br/>Operate or Shut Down]
    I --> J[Engineering Action<br/>Modify mass, damping, or stiffness]
```

## 4.2 Magnification Factor vs Frequency Ratio — Response Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given m, c, k, F0, omega] --> B[Compute omega_n = sqrt k over m]
    B --> C[Compute zeta = c over 2m omega n]
    C --> D[Compute r = omega over omega n]
    D --> E{Compare r to 1}
    E -->|r less than 1| F[Quasi-static region<br/>MF approx 1<br/>phi approx 0]
    E -->|r equals 1| G[Resonance region<br/>MF = 1 over 2 zeta<br/>phi = 90 degrees]
    E -->|r greater than 1| H[Isolation region<br/>MF less than 1<br/>phi approaches 180 degrees]
    F --> I[Report X = MF times X static]

    H --> I
    I --> J[Decision: Safe or unsafe to operate]
```

## 4.3 Whirling of Shaft — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shaft with Discs<br/>Mass m, Stiffness k] --> B[Static Equilibrium<br/>delta st = mg over k]
    B --> C[Rotation at omega<br/>Rotating Mass]
    C --> D[Centrifugal Force<br/>F = m omega squared times r]
    D --> E{Eccentricity e<br/>Amplification}
    E --> F[Shaft Bending<br/>Elastic Deflection r]
    F --> G{Spring Restoring<br/>Force = k times r}
    G -->|Balance| H[Steady Whirling<br/>Radius r]
    H --> I{omega equals omega n?}
    I -->|Yes| J[CRITICAL SPEED<br/>r becomes infinite]
    I -->|No| K[Stable Whirling<br/>r is finite]
    J --> L[Catastrophic Failure<br/>Shaft Fracture]
    K --> M[Safe Operation<br/>with margin]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Whirling Speed Calculation

| **Scenario** | **Given Data** | **Method** | **Final Formula** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single disc, direct | $m, k$ | Direct substitution | $\omega_c = \sqrt{k/m}$ |
| Single disc, deflection | $\delta_{st}$ in mm | Convert to rad/s or RPM | $N_c \approx 946/\sqrt{\delta_{st}}$ |
| Two discs, separate deflections | $\delta_1, \delta_2$ in mm | Sum of deflections | $N_c \approx 946/\sqrt{\delta_1 + \delta_2}$ |
| Two discs, separate stiffnesses | $k_1, m_1, k_2, m_2$ | Dunkerley | $1/\omega_c^2 = m_1/k_1 + m_2/k_2$ |
| Multiple discs, table of $y_i, W_i$ | Deflection at each disc, weight of each disc | Dunkerley | $1/\omega_c^2 = \sum (y_i \cdot W_i / (g \cdot W_i)) = \sum y_i/g$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** Examiners often award 2 marks for neat free-body diagrams. Always show:
> 1. The mass with applied forces ($F_0\sin\omega t$, spring $-kx$, damper $-c\dot{x}$).
> 2. The deflected shape of the whirling shaft (parabolic or sinusoidal curve).
> 3. The whirling radius $r$ and the eccentricity $e$ distinctly.
> 4. Direction arrows for centrifugal force $m\omega^2(r+e)$ and spring force $kr$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]
**Define Magnification Factor for an SDOF system subjected to harmonic excitation. State its significance in vibration analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Magnification Factor (MF)** of an SDOF system is the ratio of the dynamic amplitude of steady-state vibration to the static deflection produced by the same peak force:

$$MF = \frac{X}{X_{st}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

where $r = \omega/\omega_n$ is the frequency ratio and $\zeta$ is the damping ratio.

**Significance:**
- It quantifies the **amplification** of vibration amplitude due to dynamic effects.
- At resonance ($r = 1$), $MF = 1/(2\zeta)$ — large for lightly damped systems, indicating dangerous amplification.
- For $r \gg 1$, $MF < 1$ — the system acts as a **vibration isolator**.
- Engineers use MF to decide whether a structure can safely operate near a forcing frequency.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define whirling of a shaft. What is critical speed?**

**Model Answer:**

**Whirling of a shaft** is the lateral (transverse) vibration of a rotating shaft, in which the shaft centreline bows out and the disc(s) rotate in an orbital path about the bearing axis.

**Critical speed** is the rotational speed of the shaft at which the whirling amplitude becomes theoretically infinite (in the undamped case) — it is the speed at which the rotational frequency equals the natural lateral vibration frequency of the shaft-disc system.

Mathematically:

$$N_c \approx \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{st}\text{ (in mm)}}} \text{ RPM}$$

Operating a shaft at or near its critical speed causes severe vibration, bearing wear, and possible catastrophic failure.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model, 14 Marks]

**A machine component of mass 5 kg is supported on a spring of stiffness 2 × 10⁴ N/m and has a damping coefficient of 200 N·s/m. The component is subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = 100\sin(50t)$ N.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Determine the natural frequency, damping ratio, and the steady-state amplitude of vibration.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given data:**
- Mass: $m = 5$ kg
- Stiffness: $k = 2 \times 10^4$ N/m
- Damping: $c = 200$ N·s/m
- Forcing amplitude: $F_0 = 100$ N
- Forcing frequency: $\omega = 50$ rad/s

**Step 1 — Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^4}{5}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2 \times 5 \times 63.25 = 632.5 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**Step 3 — Damping ratio:**

$$\zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{200}{632.5} = 0.316$$

**Step 4 — Frequency ratio:**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{50}{63.25} = 0.7905$$

**Step 5 — Static deflection under peak force:**

$$X_{st} = \frac{F_0}{k} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^4} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Magnification factor:**

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Compute the components:

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - (0.7905)^2 = 1 - 0.6249 = 0.3751$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 = (0.3751)^2 = 0.1407$$

$$2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.316 \times 0.7905 = 0.4996$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.2496$$

$$(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2 = 0.1407 + 0.2496 = 0.3903$$

$$\sqrt{0.3903} = 0.6247$$

$$MF = \frac{1}{0.6247} = 1.6006$$

**Step 7 — Steady-state amplitude:**

$$X = MF \times X_{st} = 1.6006 \times 5 = 8.003 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\omega_n = 63.25$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.316$, $X \approx 8.0$ mm.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Calculate the phase angle and the maximum amplitude of the system. Comment on whether the operation is safe.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Phase angle:**

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{2\zeta r}{1 - r^2}\right] = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{0.4996}{0.3751}\right] = \tan^{-1}(1.3319)$$

$$\phi = 53.1° = 0.927 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum amplitude location.** The maximum occurs at:

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

$$\zeta^2 = (0.316)^2 = 0.0999$$

$$1 - 2\zeta^2 = 1 - 0.1997 = 0.8003$$

$$r_{max} = \sqrt{0.8003} = 0.8946$$

The corresponding maximum forcing frequency is:

$$\omega_{max} = r_{max} \times \omega_n = 0.8946 \times 63.25 = 56.58 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum magnification factor:**

$$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

$$\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = \sqrt{1-0.0999} = \sqrt{0.9001} = 0.9487$$

$$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.316 \times 0.9487} = \frac{1}{0.5996} = 1.668$$

**Step 4 — Maximum amplitude:**

$$X_{max} = MF_{max} \times X_{st} = 1.668 \times 5 = 8.34 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Safety Comment:**
- The current operating frequency $\omega = 50$ rad/s corresponds to $r = 0.79$, which is **close to** but **below** the peak frequency $r_{max} = 0.895$.
- The dynamic amplitude (8.0 mm) is **60% larger** than the static deflection (5 mm).
- This is **borderline safe**. The designer should aim for $r \le 0.5$ to keep MF close to 1, or change the system parameters to increase the natural frequency.

---

### Question B [Internal Choice Alternative, 14 Marks]

**A shaft of 20 mm diameter carries two discs weighing 50 N and 80 N at distances 100 mm and 200 mm respectively from one bearing. The shaft is simply supported over a span of 600 mm. The modulus of elasticity is $2 \times 10^5$ N/mm². Calculate the whirling speed of the shaft using Dunkerley's method.**

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Compute the static deflection at each disc location and the corresponding individual critical speeds.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_1 = 50$ N, $W_2 = 80$ N
- $a_1 = 100$ mm, $a_2 = 200$ mm (from one end of a 600 mm span)
- $L = 600$ mm
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- Diameter $d = 20$ mm

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of the shaft cross-section:**

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (20)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 160000}{64} = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Deflection formula for a simply supported beam with point load at distance $a$ from one end:**

$$\delta = \frac{P\, a^2\, b^2}{3\, E\, I\, L}$$

where $b = L - a$ and $P$ is the load (in N). (Note: this formula gives deflection under the load directly.)

**Step 3 — Deflection at disc 1 location due to $W_1 = 50$ N:**

Here $a = 100$ mm, $b = 500$ mm, $L = 600$ mm.

$$\delta_1 = \frac{50 \times (100)^2 \times (500)^2}{3 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times 7853.98 \times 600}$$

Numerator: $50 \times 10000 \times 250000 = 50 \times 2.5 \times 10^9 = 1.25 \times 10^{11}$

Denominator: $3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 7853.98 \times 600 = 3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 4.7124 \times 10^6 = 2.8274 \times 10^{12}$

$$\delta_1 = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{11}}{2.8274 \times 10^{12}} = 0.0442 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Deflection at disc 2 location due to $W_2 = 80$ N:**

Here $a = 200$ mm, $b = 400$ mm, $L = 600$ mm.

$$\delta_2 = \frac{80 \times (200)^2 \times (400)^2}{3 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times 7853.98 \times 600}$$

Numerator: $80 \times 40000 \times 160000 = 80 \times 6.4 \times 10^9 = 5.12 \times 10^{11}$

Denominator: same as above = $2.8274 \times 10^{12}$

$$\delta_2 = \frac{5.12 \times 10^{11}}{2.8274 \times 10^{12}} = 0.1811 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Individual critical speeds in RPM:**

$$N_{c1} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_1}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{0.0442}} = \frac{946}{0.2102} = 4500 \text{ RPM}$$

$$N_{c2} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_2}} = \frac{946}{\sqrt{0.1811}} = \frac{946}{0.4256} = 2223 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of $\delta$ units:** Both deflections are in mm, matching the formula's assumption.

---

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**Apply Dunkerley's method to find the combined whirling speed of the shaft.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Dunkerley's formula:**

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = \frac{1}{N_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{N_{c2}^2}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_{c1}^2} = \frac{1}{(4500)^2} = \frac{1}{2.025 \times 10^7} = 4.938 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_{c2}^2} = \frac{1}{(2223)^2} = \frac{1}{4.9417 \times 10^6} = 2.024 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\frac{1}{N_c^2} = 4.938 \times 10^{-8} + 2.024 \times 10^{-7} = 2.518 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $N_c$:**

$$N_c^2 = \frac{1}{2.518 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.971 \times 10^6$$

$$N_c = \sqrt{3.971 \times 10^6} = 1992.8 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check using combined deflection (Rayleigh form):**

$$\delta_{total} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = 0.0442 + 0.1811 = 0.2253 \text{ mm}$$

$$N_c = \frac{946}{\sqrt{0.2253}} = \frac{946}{0.4747} = 1992.7 \text{ RPM} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- The combined whirling speed (1993 RPM) is **less than** both individual critical speeds (4500 and 2223 RPM).
- The heavier disc at the larger eccentricity (Disc 2) dominates the reduction.
- The operating speed of the shaft must be kept **at least 20% away** from 1993 RPM for safe operation — i.e., either below ~1594 RPM or above ~2392 RPM.

**Final Answer:** $N_c \approx 1993$ RPM via Dunkerley's method.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **For Harmonic Excitation problems:** Students often forget to convert $X_{st} = F_0/k$ to the **same units** as the answer (mm or m). Mixing up units is the #1 mark-loser. Always write: $X_{st}$ in m → final $X$ in m.
> 2. **Phase angle quadrant error:** The phase $\phi$ is always positive and between $0$ and $\pi$. For $r > 1$, the expression $\tan^{-1}(2\zeta r/(1-r^2))$ gives a **negative** angle if computed blindly. Add $\pi$ for $r > 1$ to get the correct value in the second quadrant.
> 3. **Magnification factor vs Force Transmissibility:** $MF$ deals with displacement, $TR$ deals with force transmission to the foundation. Don't confuse the two formulas. $TR = \sqrt{(1 + (2\zeta r)^2)/((1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2)}$.
> 4. **For Whirling problems:** Many students use $N_c = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ directly with $\delta_{st}$ in m, getting a numerically tiny answer. **Always** use the form $N_c = 946/\sqrt{\delta_{st}\text{ (mm)}}$.
> 5. **Dunkerley's method signs:** $1/\omega_c^2 = \sum(1/\omega_{ci}^2)$ — note the **squares of individual critical speeds** in the denominator. Confusing this with simple addition is a common error.
> 6. **Static deflection of the shaft:** The deflection $\delta_{st}$ for whirling is the *static* deflection under the disc's own weight — **not** under any dynamic force. The formula $\omega_c = \sqrt{g/\delta_{st}}$ is valid only when $\delta_{st}$ comes from gravity alone.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SDOF Harmonic Excitation** is governed by $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin\omega t$.
- The **total response** = transient (decays) + steady-state (persists).
- The **Magnification Factor** is the most-examined quantity: $MF = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$.
- **Resonance** at $r = 1$ → amplitude limited only by damping: $X_{res} = X_{st}/(2\zeta)$.
- **Phase angle** $\phi = \tan^{-1}[2\zeta r/(1-r^2)]$ — always between $0$ and $\pi$.
- For $r > 1$, the response is *out-of-phase* with the force; the system acts as a vibration isolator.
- **Whirling speed** = lateral natural frequency of the shaft-disc system.
- **Single disc critical speed:** $N_c \approx 946/\sqrt{\delta_{st}\text{ (mm)}}$ RPM.
- **Multi-disc (Dunkerley):** $1/N_c^2 = 1/N_{c1}^2 + 1/N_{c2}^2 + \cdots$
- Dunkerley gives a **lower bound** (safe estimate) of the true critical speed.
- **Operating margins:** Industrial practice requires at least $\pm 20\%$ separation from $N_c$.
- **Whirling direction:** For $\omega < \omega_n$, disc mass centre is *outside* the geometric centre; for $\omega > \omega_n$, it is *inside* (self-centring).
- **Transmissibility** $TR \neq$ Magnification Factor $MF$ — distinguish displacement amplification from force transmission.
- **Unit discipline:** Convert mm to m (or vice versa) at the start of every numerical problem.
- **Energy viewpoint:** Resonance = forcing energy input exactly matches natural dissipation/storage cycle. Damping breaks this resonance.
- **Engineering applications:** Engine mounts, building foundations in seismic zones, turbine rotors, helicopter shafts, vehicle suspension.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vibration Absorber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vibration Absorber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vibration Absorber (Dynamic Vibration Neutralizer / Tuned Mass Damper)**
> A **vibration absorber** is a secondary mass-spring-damper system that is intentionally attached to a primary vibrating system in order to **neutralize, cancel, or significantly reduce** the steady-state amplitude of vibration of the primary mass. The principle was first proposed by **Frahm (1909)** and is formally known as a **Frahm Dynamic Vibration Absorber** or **Tuned Mass Damper (TMD)**.

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Standard Definition
A dynamic vibration absorber is an auxiliary auxiliary dynamic system (consisting of an auxiliary mass $m_2$, an auxiliary spring of stiffness $k_2$, and optionally a dashpot $c_2$) that is rigidly attached to the main (primary) vibrating system of mass $m_1$ and stiffness $k_1$. When the natural frequency of the absorber $\omega_{n2} = \sqrt{k_2 / m_2}$ is **tuned** to equal the **forcing (excitation) frequency** $\omega$ of the disturbance acting on the primary system, the primary mass $m_1$ theoretically comes to a state of **zero steady-state amplitude**, provided the absorber itself is **undamped**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Swinging Lantern in a Storm**
> Imagine a heavy chandelier (primary mass $m_1$) hanging from a long chain (stiffness $k_1$) in a windy old mansion. The chandelier sways dangerously in the gusts (forced vibration at $\omega$). To stop the sway, you tie a **smaller, lighter pendulum** (absorber mass $m_2$) to the chandelier using a short, stiff string (stiffness $k_2$). You carefully adjust the **length of the small string** so that its natural swinging frequency matches the wind's gust frequency. Result: the small pendulum now swings vigorously (absorbing all the wind energy), but the big chandelier becomes perfectly still. This is exactly how skyscraper **Tuned Mass Dampers** (e.g., the 730-tonne TMD in Taipei 101) protect tall buildings during typhoons and earthquakes.

### 1.3 Engineering Significance
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Vibration Absorbers Matter in Industry (KTU Industrial Context)**
> - **Aerospace & Automotives:** Stabilization of helicopter rotors, automotive engine crankshafts, and aircraft wing flutter suppression.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Seismic protection of skyscrapers (Taipei 101, Citicorp Center, New York), long-span bridges (Millau Viaduct, France).
> - **Power & Turbomachinery:** Reduction of torsional vibrations in turbine-generator shafts; mitigation of blade vibrations in gas/steam turbines.
> - **Precision Engineering:** Vibration isolation of optical tables, semiconductor lithography equipment, and MRI scanners.
> - **Ship & Marine:** Reduction of hull vibrations from propeller-induced excitation (whirling).

### 1.4 Classification of Vibration Absorbers
| Type | Components | Behavior at Resonance |
|------|------------|------------------------|
| **Undamped Absorber (Frahm Type)** | $m_2$, $k_2$ only | Primary mass amplitude becomes **exactly zero** when tuned |
| **Damped Absorber** | $m_2$, $k_2$, $c_2$ | Primary amplitude reduced but not zero; provides robustness against mistuning |
| **Centrifugal Pendulum Absorber** | Rotating mass on hinged arm | Used for torsional vibration of rotating shafts |
| **Impact Absorber** | Free mass striking stops | Discrete energy dissipation via collisions |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Configuration and Assumptions
Consider the canonical KTU 2-DOF model:

- Primary system: mass $m_1$, spring $k_1$, dashpot $c_1$ (usually neglected initially).
- Absorber: auxiliary mass $m_2$, spring $k_2$, dashpot $c_2$.
- The absorber is rigidly attached to $m_1$.
- Primary mass is subjected to harmonic excitation $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
- Displacements: $x_1$ of primary mass, $x_2$ of absorber mass, both measured from static equilibrium.

**Key Assumptions (KTU Standard):**
1. Both masses undergo rectilinear, one-dimensional motion.
2. Springs are massless, linear, and obey Hooke's law.
3. The system operates in the **steady-state** regime (transients neglected).
4. For the undamped case: $c_1 = c_2 = 0$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Reasoning
- **Step 1:** The absorber introduces a new degree of freedom. The combined 2-DOF system has **two natural frequencies** $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$, regardless of whether damping is present.
- **Step 2:** At a tuned undamped absorber, the absorber's natural frequency $\omega_{n2} = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$ is made equal to the excitation frequency $\omega$.
- **Step 3:** In this tuned condition, the inertia force of the absorber exactly balances the spring force of $k_1$, leaving **no net force** on $m_1$. Hence $x_1 = 0$ in the steady state.
- **Step 4:** The absorber mass $m_2$ absorbs the entire input energy and oscillates with a finite amplitude.
- **Step 5:** Adding damping $c_2$ to the absorber broadens the absorption band but sacrifices the perfect zero-amplitude condition.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Reference Table (Print & Memorize)**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Absorber natural frequency | $\omega_{n2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_2}{m_2}}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Tuning condition (undamped) | $\omega = \omega_{n2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_2}{m_2}}$ | Rad/s; primary amplitude = 0 |
| 3 | Mass ratio | $\mu = \dfrac{m_2}{m_1}$ | Dimensionless; usually $0.05$ to $0.25$ |
| 4 | Frequency ratio (primary) | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_{n1}} = \dfrac{\omega}{\sqrt{k_1/m_1}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Stiffness ratio | $\kappa = \dfrac{k_2}{k_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Undamped amplitude of $m_1$ (general) | $\dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} = \dfrac{\left(\kappa - r^2\right)}{\left(1 - r^2\right)\left(\kappa - r^2\right) - \kappa \cdot r^2}$ | where $X_{st} = F_0/k_1$ |
| 7 | Undamped amplitude of $m_1$ at $r = \sqrt{\kappa/\mu}$ | $X_1 = 0$ | Perfect absorption |
| 8 | Absorber amplitude (undamped, tuned) | $X_2 = \dfrac{F_0}{k_2} = \dfrac{F_0}{m_2 \omega^2}$ | Finite swing |
| 9 | Damped primary amplitude (with absorber) | $\dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\kappa^2 + (2\zeta_2 \kappa r)^2}}{\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}}$ | $A$, $B$ from full derivation |
| 10 | Optimum damping ratio (Den Hartog) | $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1+\mu)^3}}$ | Min of $\max(X_1)$ curve |
| 11 | Optimum tuning ratio (Den Hartog) | $f_{opt} = \dfrac{\omega_{n2}}{\omega_{n1}} = \dfrac{1}{1+\mu}$ | Peaks of $X_1$ become equal |
| 12 | Natural freq of combined system | $\omega^4 - \omega^2\!\left(\omega_{n1}^2(1+\mu) + \omega_{n2}^2\right) + \omega_{n1}^2 \omega_{n2}^2 = 0$ | Quadratic in $\omega^2$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Bar Rule in Tables:** The absolute-value bars used in formulas above have been rendered using clean LaTeX `$X_1$` notation. Do **NOT** substitute a raw `|` pipe character inside markdown table cells — it will break the table parser.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
The vibration absorber principle underpins the design of:
- **Tuned Mass Dampers (TMDs)** in skyscrapers, reducing sway under wind/earthquake loading by 30–50%.
- **Dynamic Vibration Absorbers (DVAs)** on electric motor mounts, reducing tool wear in CNC machines.
- **Stockbridge dampers** on overhead transmission lines, preventing aeolian galloping.
- **Sloshing tanks** in LNG carriers, tuned to the roll frequency of the ship.
- **Helicopter lag dampers** and **tuned absorbers** on propeller blades.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Equations of Motion — Undamped Absorber (Frahm Type)

**Free-Body Setup:**
The primary mass $m_1$ is connected to ground by $k_1$ and to the absorber $m_2$ by $k_2$. The absorber mass $m_2$ is connected only to $m_1$ (via $k_2$).

**Newton's Second Law for $m_1$:** The forces acting on $m_1$ are:
- Spring $k_1$ force: $-k_1 x_1$ (restoring)
- Spring $k_2$ force: $-k_2(x_1 - x_2)$ (extension if $x_1 > x_2$)
- External force: $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$

Therefore:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + k_1 x_1 + k_2 (x_1 - x_2) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Newton's Second Law for $m_2$:** The only spring force on $m_2$ is from $k_2$:

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 - k_2 (x_1 - x_2) = 0$$

Rewriting in standard form:

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 (x_2 - x_1) = 0$$

### 3.2 Steady-State Harmonic Solution

**Assumption:** $x_1(t) = X_1 \sin(\omega t)$ and $x_2(t) = X_2 \sin(\omega t)$. Substituting $\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 X \sin(\omega t)$:

$$-m_1 \omega^2 X_1 + k_1 X_1 + k_2 (X_1 - X_2) = F_0$$

$$-m_2 \omega^2 X_2 + k_2 (X_2 - X_1) = 0$$

Simplify:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2) X_1 - k_2 X_2 = F_0$$

$$-k_2 X_1 + (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) X_2 = 0$$

### 3.3 Solving for the Primary Mass Amplitude $X_1$

Apply Cramer's rule. The determinant of the coefficient matrix is:

$$\Delta = (k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2$$

Expand step by step:

$$\Delta = k_1 k_2 - k_1 m_2 \omega^2 + k_2^2 - m_2 \omega^2 k_2 - m_1 \omega^2 k_2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - k_2^2$$

Cancel $+k_2^2$ and $-k_2^2$:

$$\Delta = k_1 k_2 - k_1 m_2 \omega^2 - m_1 \omega^2 k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 k_2 + m_1 m_2 \omega^4$$

The numerator (replace first column by forcing vector) is:

$$\Delta_1 = F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - 0 \cdot (-k_2) = F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2)$$

Therefore:

$$X_1 = \dfrac{F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2)}{(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2}$$

### 3.4 The Critical Tuning Condition

**Set numerator to zero** to achieve $X_1 = 0$:

$$k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega^2 = \dfrac{k_2}{m_2}$$

This is precisely the **tuning condition** $\omega = \omega_{n2}$. Substituting back yields $X_1 = 0$.

**Absorber amplitude at tuning** (substitute $\omega^2 = k_2/m_2$ into the second equation):

$$-k_2 X_1 + (k_2 - m_2 \cdot k_2/m_2) X_2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -k_2 (0) + (0) X_2 = 0$$

The second equation is automatically satisfied for any $X_2$. Use the first equation to find $X_2$:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \cdot k_2/m_2) (0) - k_2 X_2 = F_0$$

$$\boxed{X_2 = -\dfrac{F_0}{k_2} = \dfrac{F_0}{m_2 \omega^2}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** The absorber mass swings with a finite, calculable amplitude, while the primary mass remains perfectly stationary.

### 3.5 Non-Dimensional Form Using Mass and Frequency Ratios

Define:
- $X_{st} = F_0/k_1$ (static deflection of primary under $F_0$)
- $\omega_{n1} = \sqrt{k_1/m_1}$, $r = \omega/\omega_{n1}$
- $\mu = m_2/m_1$, $\kappa = k_2/k_1$

Divide the equation for $X_1$ throughout by $k_1 X_{st}$:

$$\dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} = \dfrac{\kappa - \mu r^2}{(1 + \kappa - r^2)(\kappa - \mu r^2) - \kappa^2} = \dfrac{(\kappa - \mu r^2)}{(\kappa - (1+\mu)r^2) \cdot (1 - r^2) + \mu r^4}$$

A common KTU textbook simplification:

$$\dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} = \dfrac{\kappa - \mu r^2}{(1 - r^2)(\kappa - \mu r^2) - \kappa r^2}$$

### 3.6 Derivation: Natural Frequencies of the Combined 2-DOF System

Set the forcing $F_0 = 0$ and assume free harmonic motion $x_i = A_i e^{i\omega t}$. The eigenvalue equation is:

$$\begin{vmatrix} k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2 & -k_2 \\ -k_2 & k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

Expanding:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2 = 0$$

After expansion and cancellation:

$$m_1 m_2 \omega^4 - (k_1 m_2 + k_1 m_1 + k_2 m_1) \omega^2 + k_1 k_2 = 0$$

Dividing by $m_1 m_2$:

$$\omega^4 - \left( \dfrac{k_1}{m_1} + \dfrac{k_1}{m_2} + \dfrac{k_2}{m_1} \right) \omega^2 + \dfrac{k_1 k_2}{m_1 m_2} = 0$$

In terms of $\omega_{n1}$ and $\omega_{n2}$:

$$\omega^4 - \left( \omega_{n1}^2 (1+\mu) + \omega_{n2}^2 \right) \omega^2 + \omega_{n1}^2 \omega_{n2}^2 = 0$$

The two roots $\omega_1, \omega_2$ are obtained via the quadratic formula:

$$\omega_{1,2}^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\omega_{n1}^2(1+\mu) + \omega_{n2}^2\right] \mp \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\left[\omega_{n1}^2(1+\mu) - \omega_{n2}^2\right]^2 + 4\mu \omega_{n1}^2 \omega_{n2}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inference:** The two natural frequencies $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ bracket the tuning frequency $\omega_{n2}$ in general. Only at the very specific choice $\omega = \omega_{n2}$ does $X_1$ vanish.

### 3.7 Damped Absorber — Den Hartog's Optimum Design

With damping $c_2$ in the absorber branch, the equations of motion become:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + k_1 x_1 + k_2 (x_1 - x_2) + c_2 (\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 (x_2 - x_1) + c_2 (\dot{x}_2 - \dot{x}_1) = 0$$

For steady-state, define complex amplitudes $X_1$, $X_2$ such that $x_i = \text{Re}\{X_i e^{i\omega t}\}$. The algebraic system becomes:

$$(k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2 + i c_2 \omega) X_1 - (k_2 + i c_2 \omega) X_2 = F_0$$

$$-(k_2 + i c_2 \omega) X_1 + (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 + i c_2 \omega) X_2 = 0$$

Solving via Cramer's rule yields (after substantial algebra):

$$\dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} = \dfrac{\kappa - \mu r^2 + i 2\zeta_2 \kappa r}{(1 - r^2)(\kappa - \mu r^2) - \mu \kappa r^4 + i 2\zeta_2 \kappa r \left[1 - r^2(1+\mu)\right]}$$

The magnitude is:

$$\left\vert \dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} \right\vert = \dfrac{\sqrt{(\kappa - \mu r^2)^2 + (2\zeta_2 \kappa r)^2}}{\sqrt{[(1-r^2)(\kappa - \mu r^2) - \mu \kappa r^4]^2 + \{2\zeta_2 \kappa r [1 - r^2(1+\mu)]\}^2}}$$

**Den Hartog's Optimum Design Procedure:**

Define $f = \omega_{n2}/\omega_{n1}$ (tuning frequency ratio) and $\zeta_2$ (absorber damping ratio). The curve $\vert X_1/F_0 \vert$ versus $\omega$ exhibits **two equal peaks** when the optimum values are chosen. The classical Den Hartog formulas are:

**Optimum tuning ratio (no damping penalty at peaks):**

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{1+\mu}$$

**Optimum damping ratio (equalizes the two peak heights):**

$$\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1+\mu)^3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Tip:** If the absorber is mistuned (e.g., $\omega$ drifts from $f_{opt} \omega_{n1}$), the optimum damping makes the response peaks **equal but nonzero**, which is preferable to the undamped case where one peak can become unbounded.

### 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A machine of mass $m_1 = 100$ kg with a spring stiffness $k_1 = 2 \times 10^6$ N/m vibrates under a harmonic force of amplitude $F_0 = 500$ N at $\omega = 200$ rad/s. Design an undamped vibration absorber.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Choose mass ratio. For good absorption, take $\mu = m_2/m_1 = 0.1$, so $m_2 = 10$ kg.

Step 2: Apply tuning condition $\omega^2 = k_2/m_2$:

$$k_2 = m_2 \omega^2 = 10 \times (200)^2 = 10 \times 40000 = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}$$

Step 3: Primary amplitude $X_1 = 0$ (perfect cancellation).

Step 4: Absorber amplitude:

$$X_2 = \dfrac{F_0}{k_2} = \dfrac{500}{4 \times 10^5} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$

Step 5: Verify with general formula. $\omega_{n1} = \sqrt{2\times10^6/100} = 141.42$ rad/s, $r = 200/141.42 = 1.414$, $\kappa = k_2/k_1 = 0.2$, $\mu = 0.1$.

$$\dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} = \dfrac{0.2 - 0.1(1.414)^2}{(1-2)(0.2 - 0.1 \cdot 2) - 0.2 \cdot 2} = \dfrac{0.2 - 0.2}{(-1)(0) - 0.4} = \dfrac{0}{0.4} = 0 \checkmark$$

**Static deflection check:** $X_{st} = F_0/k_1 = 500/(2\times10^6) = 2.5\times10^{-4}$ m. Without the absorber, dynamic amplification at $r = 1.414$ would give $X_1 = X_{st}/(1-r^2) = 2.5\times10^{-4}/(1-2) = 5\times10^{-4}$ m = 0.5 mm. With the absorber, this drops to **0 mm** at exact tuning — a 100% reduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Configuration Diagram (Adapted as Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Fixed Wall / Ground] -- k1 spring --> B[Primary Mass m1]
    B -- k2 spring --> C[Absorber Mass m2]
    B -- F(t) Harmonic Force --> B
    C -- c2 dashpot optional --> B
    A -- c1 dashpot optional --> B
    B -- displacement x1 --> D[Sensor / Output]
    C -- displacement x2 --> E[Sensor / Output]
```

### 4.2 System Block Diagram of the 2-DOF Absorber Model

```mermaid
graph TB
    F0[Forcing F0 sin omega t] --> SUM1{Summation Node}
    K1[Spring k1] --> SUM1
    C1[Damper c1] --> SUM1
    K2[Spring k2] --> SUM1
    C2[Damper c2] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> INT1[Integrator 1/s]
    INT1 --> VEL1[Velocity s block]
    VEL1 --> M1[Mass m1]
    M1 --> X1[Displacement x1]
    X1 --> K2
    X1 --> C2
    X1 --> K1
    X1 --> C1
    K2 --> SUM2{Summation Node 2}
    C2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> INT2[Integrator 1/s]
    INT2 --> M2[Absorber mass m2]
    M2 --> X2[Displacement x2]
```

### 4.3 Frequency Response Flowchart — What Happens at Different $\omega$

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Excitation Frequency omega] --> B{Is damping present?}
    B -- No, undamped --> C{Absorber tuned to omega n2?}
    B -- Yes, damped --> D{Den Hartog optimum?}
    C -- Yes, perfectly tuned --> E[Primary amplitude X1 = 0]
    C -- Slightly mistuned --> F[Small finite X1 increases rapidly]
    C -- Heavily mistuned --> G[Absorber ineffective, X1 large]
    D -- Yes --> H[Two equal finite peaks, broad absorption band]
    D -- No --> I[Single large peak, narrow band]
    E --> J[Absorber swings at X2 = F0 / k2]
    F --> K[Both masses vibrate, X1 finite]
    G --> L[Original 1-DOF resonance restored]
    H --> M[Robust industrial-grade absorption]
    I --> N[Sub-optimal, sensitive to drift]
```

### 4.4 Force–Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[External Force F0 sin omega t] --> B[Primary Mass m1]
    B -- k1 spring --> G[Ground Reference]
    B -- Coupling k2 and c2 --> C[Absorber Mass m2]
    C -- Free end --> D[Free Vibration Tail]
    subgraph SDOF Without Absorber
        A2[F0 sin omega t] --> B2[Primary m1]
        B2 -- k1 --> G2[Ground]
    end
    subgraph TwoDOF With Absorber
        A3[F0 sin omega t] --> B3[Primary m1]
        B3 -- k1 --> G3[Ground]
        B3 -- k2 c2 --> C3[Absorber m2]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Paper]**
*Define a vibration absorber. State the condition under which the primary mass has zero steady-state amplitude.*

**Model Answer:**
A vibration absorber is an auxiliary mass-spring (and optionally damper) system attached to a primary vibrating system to suppress its steady-state vibration. The condition for zero steady-state amplitude of the primary mass is that the **natural frequency of the absorber equals the excitation frequency**, i.e., $\omega = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$. Under this tuning and with no damping in the absorber, the inertial force of the absorber mass balances the spring force of $k_1$, leaving no net force on $m_1$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*State the advantages and disadvantages of an undamped vibration absorber.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantages:**
> (i) Complete elimination of primary mass vibration at the tuned frequency.
> (ii) Simple design with only two parameters: $m_2$ and $k_2$.
> (iii) No energy dissipation needed; energy is transferred to the absorber mass.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> (i) Effectiveness is lost rapidly if the excitation frequency drifts away from the tuning frequency.
> (ii) Two new resonance peaks appear on either side of $\omega_{n2}$, which can be dangerous if crossed.
> (iii) Absorber mass must be physically accommodated; typically $\mu = m_2/m_1$ ranges from 0.05 to 0.25.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Undamped Absorber Full Derivation**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4 Carry-Over Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the equations of motion for a primary system of mass $m_1$ and stiffness $k_1$ fitted with an undamped vibration absorber of mass $m_2$ and stiffness $k_2$, when the primary mass is subjected to a harmonic force $F_0 \sin(\omega t)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Hence obtain the expression for the steady-state amplitude of the primary mass. Show that the amplitude becomes zero when the absorber is tuned to the excitation frequency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Equations of Motion (7 marks):**

From the free-body diagram, applying Newton's second law to $m_1$ (positive direction = right):

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 = -k_1 x_1 - k_2(x_1 - x_2) + F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Rearranged:

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + (k_1 + k_2) x_1 - k_2 x_2 = F_0 \sin(\omega t) \quad \cdots (1)$$

For $m_2$ (the only force is from $k_2$):

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 = -k_2 (x_2 - x_1)$$

Rearranged:

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 x_2 - k_2 x_1 = 0 \quad \cdots (2)$$

Equations (1) and (2) form the coupled 2-DOF system.

**Model Solution (b) — Steady-State Amplitude (7 marks):**

Assume steady-state harmonic response: $x_1 = X_1 \sin(\omega t)$ and $x_2 = X_2 \sin(\omega t)$.

Substituting into (1) and (2) and using $\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 x$:

$$[k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2] X_1 - k_2 X_2 = F_0 \quad \cdots (3)$$

$$-k_2 X_1 + [k_2 - m_2 \omega^2] X_2 = 0 \quad \cdots (4)$$

By Cramer's rule, the determinant of the system is:

$$\Delta = (k_1 + k_2 - m_1 \omega^2)(k_2 - m_2 \omega^2) - k_2^2$$

The amplitude of the primary mass is:

$$X_1 = \dfrac{F_0 (k_2 - m_2 \omega^2)}{\Delta}$$

For $X_1 = 0$, the numerator must vanish:

$$k_2 - m_2 \omega^2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_2}{m_2}} = \omega_{n2}$$

This is the **tuning condition**. Under this condition, the inertia force $m_2 \omega^2 X_2$ exactly equals $k_2 X_2$, and the spring $k_1$ sees no net displacement of $m_1$ from equilibrium. Hence the primary mass remains stationary. The absorber amplitude becomes $X_2 = F_0/k_2$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Damped Absorber & Den Hartog Design**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working of a damped dynamic vibration absorber. Write the equations of motion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and explain the Den Hartog optimum tuning conditions for a damped vibration absorber. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — Damped Absorber Setup (7 marks):**

A damped vibration absorber is the practical industrial version, where a dashpot $c_2$ is added in parallel with the absorber spring $k_2$. The components are arranged as:
- Primary mass $m_1$ on spring $k_1$ to ground, excited by $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$.
- Auxiliary mass $m_2$ connected to $m_1$ via spring $k_2$ and dashpot $c_2$ in parallel.

**Equations of motion:**

$$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 + k_1 x_1 + k_2 (x_1 - x_2) + c_2 (\dot{x}_1 - \dot{x}_2) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

$$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 + k_2 (x_2 - x_1) + c_2 (\dot{x}_2 - \dot{x}_1) = 0$$

The damping provides **robustness** against mistuning. If $\omega$ drifts, the damped absorber still suppresses vibration over a band, unlike the undamped case which fails catastrophically.

**Model Solution (b) — Den Hartog Optimum Conditions (7 marks):**

**Den Hartog's Optimum Design** determines the best choice of $f = \omega_{n2}/\omega_{n1}$ and $\zeta_2 = c_2/(2\sqrt{k_2 m_2})$ to minimize the maximum amplitude of the primary mass over all frequencies.

**Two-Step Procedure:**
1. **Optimum tuning ratio** (no damping assumption) — equalizes the two natural frequencies' peak responses:

$$f_{opt} = \dfrac{\omega_{n2}}{\omega_{n1}} = \dfrac{1}{1+\mu}$$

2. **Optimum damping ratio** (with the above tuning) — makes the two response peaks of $\vert X_1 \vert$ vs $\omega$ of **equal height**:

$$\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3\mu}{8(1+\mu)^3}}$$

After applying these, the **maximum possible amplitude** of the primary mass is bounded by:

$$\left( \dfrac{X_1}{X_{st}} \right)_{max} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2}{\mu}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing the tuning condition as $\omega = \sqrt{k_1/m_1}$ instead of $\omega = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$. Always remember: the absorber is tuned to the *excitation*, **not** to the primary natural frequency.
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to specify that the zero-amplitude result holds **only for undamped** absorbers. Adding $c_2$ immediately destroys the perfect cancellation.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing the two natural frequencies of the combined 2-DOF system with the single resonance of the original 1-DOF system. The absorber creates **two** new peaks, not one.
> - **Mistake 4:** Using $\omega = \omega_{n1}$ for "resonance" in the presence of an absorber. The resonance frequencies shift to $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ as derived.
> - **Mistake 5:** In numerical problems, forgetting to use consistent units (e.g., mixing kgf and N for force, or cm and m for displacement).
> - **Mistake 6:** Not drawing the free-body diagram — KTU examiners often allocate 1–2 marks purely for the diagram and labeling.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print Before Exam**

- **Definition:** A vibration absorber is an auxiliary mass-spring (or mass-spring-damper) system attached to a primary system to suppress its forced vibration.
- **Tuning Condition (Undamped Frahm Absorber):** $\omega = \sqrt{k_2/m_2}$. At this exact frequency, the primary amplitude is **zero** and the absorber amplitude is $X_2 = F_0/k_2$.
- **Mass Ratio:** $\mu = m_2/m_1$, typically 0.05–0.25. Larger $\mu$ → better absorption, but more weight.
- **Stiffness Ratio:** $\kappa = k_2/k_1$. Determines absorber stiffness given the primary stiffness.
- **Two New Natural Frequencies:** The 2-DOF system has two natural frequencies $\omega_1, \omega_2$ given by the quartic:
  $$\omega^4 - [\omega_{n1}^2(1+\mu) + \omega_{n2}^2]\omega^2 + \omega_{n1}^2 \omega_{n2}^2 = 0$$
- **Damped Absorber (Den Hartog):** Adds $c_2$ to broaden the absorption band. The primary amplitude is no longer zero, but is bounded.
- **Optimum Tuning Ratio (Den Hartog):** $f_{opt} = 1/(1+\mu)$.
- **Optimum Damping Ratio (Den Hartog):** $\zeta_{2,opt} = \sqrt{3\mu/[8(1+\mu)^3]}$.
- **Max Amplitude with Optimum Damping:** $\left( X_1/X_{st} \right)_{max} = \sqrt{1 + 2/\mu}$.
- **Key Engineering Examples:** Taipei 101 TMD (730 t), Citicorp Center TMD, helicopter lag dampers, Stockbridge dampers on power lines.
- **Limitation:** Undamped absorbers fail outside the tuned frequency. Damped absorbers trade peak suppression for bandwidth.
- **KTU Exam Trap:** Always state assumptions clearly (linear, steady-state, 1-D motion, no damping unless specified).
- **Order of Magnitude Sanity Check:** Industrial absorber frequencies are usually 1–50 Hz; natural frequencies above 200 Hz typically indicate miscalculation.
- **Practical Design Sequence:** (1) Choose $\mu$ (2) Compute $m_2$ (3) Set $\omega_{n2} = \omega_{forcing}$ (4) Compute $k_2 = m_2 \omega^2$ (5) For damped version, apply Den Hartog to find $c_2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmissibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT756 - DYNAMICS & MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transmissibility

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transmissibility (TR)** is defined as the **ratio of the amplitude of the force transmitted to the foundation (or supporting structure) to the amplitude of the exciting force applied to the system**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F_T}{F_0}$$

where $F_T$ is the maximum (amplitude) force transmitted to the base and $F_0$ is the amplitude of the applied harmonic force.

It is also called the **Force Transmissibility** or **Force Magnification Factor** and is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies how effectively a vibrating system passes dynamic forces into its surroundings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy: The Floating Mattress on a Washing Machine**
>
> Imagine you place a heavy mattress on top of an old, vibrating washing machine. The washing machine's motor creates a rhythmic shaking force (the *exciting force* $F_0$). If you stand on the floor near the machine, you can feel the floor vibrating — this is the *transmitted force* $F_T$.
>
> - If the mattress is **soft** (low stiffness $k$), it absorbs much of the shaking. You barely feel the floor vibrate. → **Transmissibility is low** (good isolation).
> - If the mattress is **rigid** (high stiffness $k$), the shaking passes straight through to the floor. → **Transmissibility is high** (poor isolation).
> - If you shake the washing machine at exactly its natural frequency, the mattress bounces violently and the floor shakes enormously. → **Resonance — transmissibility shoots to infinity (undamped case)**.
>
> The mattress is essentially a **vibration isolator**. Its job is to make the *transmitted* force much smaller than the *applied* force. This is the entire purpose of mounting heavy machinery on rubber pads, coil springs, or pneumatic mounts in real engineering practice.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

The following dimensionless and dimensional metrics govern transmissibility:

- **Frequency ratio (r)** = $\omega / \omega_n$ — a dimensionless number, **bold** in the table below.
- **Damping ratio (ζ)** = $c / c_c$ — a dimensionless number, **bold** in the table below.
- **Natural frequency** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ in **rad/s**.
- **Critical damping coefficient** $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ in **N·s/m**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 examiners frequently test whether students can identify the **two main regions** on a transmissibility curve:
> 1. The **Amplification Region** ($r < \sqrt{2}$), where the system magnifies forces.
> 2. The **Isolation Region** ($r > \sqrt{2}$), where the system reduces forces — this is where real-world isolators are designed to operate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

When a base-excited or force-excited single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system vibrates, two things happen simultaneously at the support:

1. The **spring** (stiffness $k$) stores and returns energy → produces a **spring force** $kx$.
2. The **damper** (coefficient $c$) dissipates energy → produces a **damping force** $c\dot{x}$.

The total **force transmitted** to the foundation is the vector sum of these two:

$$F_T = kx + c\dot{x}$$

For steady-state harmonic motion $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$, this gives a phase-shifted sinusoidal transmitted force whose **amplitude** is:

$$F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

The applied (exciting) force amplitude is $F_0$. The **transmissibility** is therefore the ratio:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{F_0}$$

Since the steady-state displacement amplitude is:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

Substituting yields the **master transmissibility equation**.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Formula

- **Spring stiffness $k$** appears in the numerator because stiffer supports transmit more force for a given displacement.
- **Damping $c$** appears in the numerator because viscous dampers also push directly on the foundation.
- The denominator contains the *effective stiffness* $(k - m\omega^2)$ and damping terms because the displacement amplitude is reduced by inertia and damping internally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** A perfectly rigid system ($k \to \infty$) gives $\varepsilon = 1$ (no reduction). A perfectly soft system ($k \to 0$) also gives $\varepsilon = 1$ (the system just floats around, transmitting only the inertia force at the natural frequency). The sweet spot for isolation lies *between* these extremes — at high frequency ratios.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Transmissibility (general) | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Master equation, valid for all $r$ and $\zeta$ |
| Transmissibility (undamped, $\zeta = 0$) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$ | Simplifies dramatically; sign via $\vert \cdot \vert$ |
| Frequency ratio | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | Dimensionless |
| Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| Damping ratio | $\zeta = c / c_c$ | Dimensionless |
| Critical damping | $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$ | N·s/m |
| Transmissibility at resonance ($r = 1$) | $\varepsilon_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}\sqrt{1 + 4\zeta^2}$ | Finite only if $\zeta > 0$ |
| Transmissibility at $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $\varepsilon = 1$ | The **transition point** between amplification and isolation |
| Condition for isolation | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $\varepsilon < 1$ — applied force is reduced |
| Optimum damping for minimum peak | $\zeta_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$ | Practical design range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 students must memorize: **at $r = \sqrt{2}$, transmissibility equals 1 regardless of damping**. This is the dividing line — *above* it is the isolation region, *below* it is the amplification region.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Machine Mounts:** Industrial motors, compressors, and pumps are mounted on rubber pads or springs with natural frequencies well below the operating speed, so that $r \gg \sqrt{2}$.
- **Automotive Suspensions:** Cars use a "sky-hook" strategy where the sprung mass is decoupled from road excitation at high frequency ratios.
- **Building Base Isolation:** Skyscrapers in earthquake-prone regions use laminated rubber bearings tuned so that the dominant seismic frequencies lie in the isolation region.
- **Precision Instruments:** Optical benches, microscopes, and electron microscopes require floor-vibration isolation → active or passive isolators are designed with $r \gg \sqrt{2}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Transmissibility for a Damped SDOF System

**Step 1 — Equation of Motion**

Consider a mass $m$ attached to a spring of stiffness $k$ and a viscous damper of damping coefficient $c$, subjected to a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Solution (assumed form)**

For steady-state response, the displacement oscillates at the same frequency $\omega$ as the excitation, with a phase lag $\phi$:

$$x(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

The derivatives needed are:

$$\dot{x}(t) = X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

$$\ddot{x}(t) = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the equation of motion**

Substituting these into the original equation:

$$m[-X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)] + c[X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)] + k[X\sin(\omega t - \phi)] = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Rearranging:

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Resolve the phase angle**

Using the identity $\sin(\omega t - \phi) = \sin\omega t \cos\phi - \cos\omega t \sin\phi$, the left-hand side splits into $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$ components. Equating coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$ independently on both sides yields:

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\cos\phi + c\omega X \sin\phi = F_0$$

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin\phi - c\omega X \cos\phi = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the amplitude $X$**

From the second equation:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}$$

Squaring and adding the two equations (eliminating $\phi$) gives:

$$X^2[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2] = F_0^2$$

Therefore the steady-state amplitude is:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Express the Transmitted Force**

The force transmitted to the foundation is the sum of the spring force and damping force:

$$F_T(t) = kx + c\dot{x} = kX\sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi)$$

The amplitude of this transmitted force is:

$$F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

**Step 7 — Form the Transmissibility Ratio**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{F_0}$$

Substituting $X$ from Step 5:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 8 — Non-dimensionalize using $r$ and $\zeta$**

Divide numerator and denominator by $k$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (c\omega/k)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - m\omega^2/k)^2 + (c\omega/k)^2}}$$

Now use the identities $m\omega^2/k = (\omega/\omega_n)^2 = r^2$ and $c\omega/k = 2\zeta r$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master equation** of transmissibility. Setting $\zeta = 0$ recovers the undamped case $\varepsilon = 1/\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$.

### 3.2 Special Case: Undamped System ($\zeta = 0$)

Setting $\zeta = 0$ in the master equation:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$$

- For $r \to 0$ (very slow excitation): $\varepsilon \to 1$ (force passes through unchanged).
- For $r \to 1$ (resonance): $\varepsilon \to \infty$ (catastrophic amplification in the absence of damping).
- For $r > 1$ (above resonance): $\varepsilon$ becomes positive and decreases as $r$ grows.
- For $r \to \infty$: $\varepsilon \to 0$ (perfect isolation, theoretically).

### 3.3 Special Case: Resonance ($r = 1$)

Substituting $r = 1$ into the master equation:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{res}} = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 4\zeta^2}{0 + 4\zeta^2}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}\sqrt{1 + 4\zeta^2}$$

For small damping ($\zeta \ll 1$): $\varepsilon_{\text{res}} \approx 1/(2\zeta)$, which is very large.

### 3.4 Critical Frequency Ratio $r = \sqrt{2}$

Setting $\varepsilon = 1$ in the master equation and solving:

$$1 + (2\zeta r)^2 = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

$$1 = (1 - r^2)^2$$

$$1 - r^2 = \pm 1$$

The physically meaningful root is $r^2 = 2$, i.e.:

$$r = \sqrt{2} \quad \text{(independent of damping!)}$$

This remarkable result — that the transition point $r = \sqrt{2}$ is independent of $\zeta$ — is a **favourite KTU question**.

### 3.5 Conditions and Limits Summary

| **Frequency Range** | **Behaviour of $\varepsilon$** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| $0 \le r < \sqrt{2}$ | $\varepsilon \ge 1$ (Amplification Region) | System magnifies the input force — **avoid this region** |
| $r = \sqrt{2}$ | $\varepsilon = 1$ (Transition Point) | Force passes through unchanged |
| $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $\varepsilon < 1$ (Isolation Region) | Force is reduced — **design for this region** |
| $r \to \infty$ | $\varepsilon \to 0$ | Perfect isolation (ideal limit) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Setup of a Force-Transmissibility System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below illustrates the canonical SDOF mass–spring–damper system under harmonic force excitation, and shows the conceptual flow of how the applied force becomes the transmitted force at the foundation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Harmonic Force F0 sin wt]:::forceIn
    B[Mass m with Spring k and Damper c]:::sysBlock
    C[Foundation or Support Structure]:::support
    D[Transmitted Force FT]:::forceOut
    
    A -- applied on mass --> B
    B -- coupled through spring and damper --> C
    B -- reaction force --> D
    C -- supports the system --> B
    
    classDef forceIn fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sysBlock fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef forceOut fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Transmissibility Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Harmonic Force F0 sin wt]:::inputBlock
    B[Equation of Motion: m x double dot + c x dot + k x = F0 sin wt]:::eqBlock
    C[Steady State Displacement Amplitude X]:::outputBlock
    D[Transmitted Force Amplitude FT = X times sqrt k squared + c omega squared]:::outputBlock
    E[Transmissibility epsilon = FT divided by F0]:::coreBlock
    F[Master Formula: sqrt of 1 + 2 zeta r squared divided by 1 minus r squared squared + 2 zeta r squared]:::coreBlock
    G[OUTPUT: Decision - Isolation or Amplification]:::decisionBlock
    H[r greater than sqrt 2: ISOLATION achieved]:::goodBlock
    I[r less than sqrt 2: AMPLIFICATION occurs]:::badBlock
    
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G -- yes --> H
    G -- no --> I
    
    classDef inputBlock fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px
    classDef eqBlock fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef outputBlock fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef coreBlock fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px

    classDef goodBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef badBlock fill:#FF7F7F,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Transmissibility Curve — Qualitative Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TR_CURVE[Transmissibility Curve TR vs r]
        direction LR
        A1[r = 0: TR = 1]:::pt
        A2[Amplification Region: r less than sqrt 2, TR greater than 1]:::amp
        A3[Resonance Peak: r = 1, TR max]:::peak
        A4[Transition: r = sqrt 2, TR = 1]:::trans
        A5[Isolation Region: r greater than sqrt 2, TR less than 1]:::isol
        A6[r approaches infinity: TR approaches 0]:::limit
    end
    
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6
    
    classDef pt fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef amp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef peak fill:#FF0000,stroke:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:3px
    classDef trans fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef isol fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    classDef limit fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
```
