## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**Define transmissibility. State the condition for a system to be a good vibration isolator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Transmissibility** is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the force transmitted to the foundation to the amplitude of the exciting force. Mathematically, $\varepsilon = F_T / F_0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Condition for good vibration isolation:** The frequency ratio must satisfy $r = \omega/\omega_n > \sqrt{2}$, which ensures that the transmissibility $\varepsilon < 1$. The larger the value of $r$, the better the isolation.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, and 1 mark for the condition $r > \sqrt{2}$.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, 3 marks]**

**At what frequency ratio is the transmissibility equal to unity, and is this value dependent on damping? Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Transmissibility equals unity ($\varepsilon = 1$) at a frequency ratio of $r = \sqrt{2}$.

This value is **independent of the damping ratio $\zeta$**, because in the master equation:

$$1 + (2\zeta r)^2 = (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

the terms involving $\zeta$ cancel identically, leaving $1 = (1 - r^2)^2$, which gives $r^2 = 2$.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the value, 1 mark for the cancellation logic, 1 mark for the conclusion of damping independence.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4]**

**Derive the expression for the transmissibility of a damped single-degree-of-freedom system subjected to a harmonic excitation $F_0\sin(\omega t)$. Show that the condition for isolation is $r > \sqrt{2}$.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of the Transmissibility Formula (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Write the equation of motion for a damped SDOF system under harmonic force:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Assume steady-state solution $x(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

**Step 3:** Substituting into the equation of motion and equating coefficients of $\sin\omega t$ and $\cos\omega t$ separately gives the two simultaneous equations:

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\cos\phi + c\omega X \sin\phi = F_0$$

$$X(k - m\omega^2)\sin\phi - c\omega X \cos\phi = 0$$

**Step 4:** Squaring and adding eliminates $\phi$:

$$X^2[(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2] = F_0^2$$

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 5:** The transmitted force to the base is the sum of spring and damper forces:

$$F_T = kx + c\dot{x}$$

Its amplitude is:

$$F_T = X\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}$$

**Step 6:** The transmissibility is the ratio:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 7:** Non-dimensionalize using $r = \omega/\omega_n$, $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $\zeta = c/(2m\omega_n)$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}}$$

#### Part (b) — Proof that Isolation Requires $r > \sqrt{2}$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** For the system to be a good isolator, the transmitted force must be *less* than the applied force, i.e.:

$$\varepsilon < 1$$

**Step 2:** Setting $\varepsilon < 1$ in the master equation:

$$\frac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2} < 1$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by the (always positive) denominator:

$$1 + (2\zeta r)^2 < (1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2$$

**Step 4:** The $(2\zeta r)^2$ terms cancel:

$$1 < (1 - r^2)^2$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root of both sides (both sides are positive for $r \neq 1$):

$$1 < \vert 1 - r^2 \vert$$

**Step 6:** For $r > 1$, this becomes $1 < r^2 - 1$, which simplifies to $r^2 > 2$, i.e.:

$$\boxed{r > \sqrt{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remark:** The result is *independent of the damping ratio* — no matter how much damping is present, the transition between amplification and isolation always occurs at $r = \sqrt{2}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 4, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) A machine of mass 200 kg is supported on springs of total stiffness $4 \times 10^5$ N/m. The machine produces a harmonic force of amplitude 800 N at an operating speed of 1500 rpm. Determine: (i) the frequency ratio, (ii) the steady-state amplitude of vibration, and (iii) the force transmitted to the foundation.** (7 Marks)

**(b) If the same machine is now fitted with a damper of damping ratio $\zeta = 0.2$, find the new transmissibility and the transmitted force. Comment on the effect of damping on isolation.** (7 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 10^5}{200}} = \sqrt{2000} = 44.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the excitation frequency:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency ratio:**

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{44.72} = 3.513$$

**Step 4 — Undamped amplitude:**

$$X = \frac{F_0}{k(1 - r^2)} = \frac{800}{4 \times 10^5 \times (1 - 3.513^2)} = \frac{800}{4 \times 10^5 \times (1 - 12.34)} = \frac{800}{4 \times 10^5 \times (-11.34)}$$

$$X = \frac{800}{-4.536 \times 10^6} = -1.764 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

The negative sign simply indicates a $180°$ phase lag (the system moves opposite to the force). The **amplitude** is:

$$|X| = 1.764 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.1764 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Transmissibility (undamped):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - 12.34 \vert} = \frac{1}{11.34} = 0.0882$$

**Step 6 — Transmitted force:**

$$F_T = \varepsilon \times F_0 = 0.0882 \times 800 = 70.56 \text{ N}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comment:** Since $r = 3.513 > \sqrt{2}$, the system is operating in the **isolation region**, and the transmitted force is only $\approx 8.8\%$ of the applied force.

#### Part (b) — Solution with Damping (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Recompute the transmissibility with $\zeta = 0.2$:**

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1 + (2 \times 0.2 \times 3.513)^2}{(1 - 3.513^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.2 \times 3.513)^2}}$$

Compute the terms:

$$2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.2 \times 3.513 = 1.4052$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = 1.9746$$

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 12.34 = -11.34$$

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = 128.60$$

Therefore:

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{\frac{1 + 1.9746}{128.60 + 1.9746}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.9746}{130.57}} = \sqrt{0.02278} = 0.151$$

**Step 2 — Transmitted force with damping:**

$$F_T = \varepsilon \times F_0 = 0.151 \times 800 = 120.8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison and Comment:**

| **Quantity** | **Undamped** | **Damped ($\zeta = 0.2$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Transmissibility | 0.0882 | 0.151 |
| Transmitted force | 70.56 N | 120.8 N |

> [!WARNING]
> **Comment:** Adding damping *increased* the transmitted force from 70.56 N to 120.8 N! This is because the system is already well into the isolation region ($r = 3.513 \gg \sqrt{2}$). In this region, damping actually *degrades* isolation by allowing the damper to push additional force directly into the foundation. The general rule: **damping helps at or near resonance but hurts at high $r$**. For optimum isolation, designers use *low damping* and rely on a high $r$ to achieve $\varepsilon \ll 1$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Transmissibility problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value signs in the undamped formula** $\varepsilon = 1/\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$. KTU examiners frequently deduct 1 mark if a student writes $1/(1 - r^2)$ without the modulus when $r > 1$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up *displacement transmissibility* and *force transmissibility*.** These are equal only in the undamped case. With damping, displacement transmissibility is $\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$, which is the same as force transmissibility — but students sometimes write the wrong formula.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s.** The single most common unit error. Always use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
>
> 4. **Concluding that damping always helps isolation.** It does not. Damping *reduces* transmissibility near resonance but *increases* it deep in the isolation region.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning the critical condition $r > \sqrt{2}$** in design questions. A complete answer must always state this and explain that the point $r = \sqrt{2}$ is **independent of damping**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Transmissibility**
>
> - **Definition:** $\varepsilon = F_T / F_0$ — ratio of transmitted force amplitude to applied force amplitude.
> - **Master equation (damped):** $\varepsilon = \sqrt{[1 + (2\zeta r)^2] / [(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2]}$.
> - **Master equation (undamped):** $\varepsilon = 1 / \vert 1 - r^2 \vert$.
> - **Frequency ratio:** $r = \omega / \omega_n$, where $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$.
> - **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = c / c_c$, where $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$.
> - **Transition point:** $\varepsilon = 1$ at $r = \sqrt{2}$ — **independent of damping**.
> - **Isolation condition:** $r > \sqrt{2}$ ensures $\varepsilon < 1$ — design goal of all isolators.
> - **Amplification region:** $r < \sqrt{2}$ — avoid this in operation.
> - **At resonance** ($r = 1$): $\varepsilon = (1/2\zeta)\sqrt{1 + 4\zeta^2}$ — finite only with damping.
> - **Damping trade-off:** Damping reduces peak at resonance but worsens isolation at high $r$.
> - **Design rule of thumb:** For practical isolators, choose $r$ in the range $3$ to $5$ to achieve $\varepsilon \approx 0.1$ to $0.15$.
> - **Displacement transmissibility** equals force transmissibility (same formula, since the spring force is proportional to displacement).
> - **Engineering applications:** Engine mounts, building base isolators, precision instrument tables, automotive suspensions, helicopter rotor isolation.
> - **KTU exam traps:** Units (rad/s vs rpm), absolute value, damping independence at $r = \sqrt{2}$, and the "damping improves isolation" misconception.
